articles.author: Ashley Murray
articles.body:
First lady Melania Trump makes a brief statement to deny any connection with late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein on April 9, 2026. (Image via White House livestream)

WASHINGTON — First lady Melania Trump said Thursday she was “never involved in any capacity” with the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and that “baseless lies” about her are being circulated.

articles.title: Woods signs with Oakwood University
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
Dallas County senior Devin Woods solidified his future on the diamond Thursday, April 9, officially signing a letter of intent to play baseball at Oakwood University.

Surrounded by family, coaches and teammates, the standout athlete celebrated a milestone that bridges his successful high school career with his dreams of playing professional baseball and a having a career in forensic science.

“I’m just now realizing that I’m moving on and going up,” Woods said, reflecting on the transition from

Andy Health become state’s first Purple Heart Hospital

Andalusia Health held a ceremony on Thursday April 9 to celebrate being Alabama’s first Purple Heart Hospital. Hospital employees, community members, and local veterans gathered in the main lobby of the hospital to show their support for our Purple Heart Veterans and their families.

articles.body:
Officers seek to prevent unregulated substances being sold locally

“We served... anywhere that sells alcohol a letter stating that they need to stop selling these products immediately,” Andalusia Chief of Police Paul Hudson stated. The letter, which came from the District Attorney’s Office, referred to kratom products, unregulated and dangerous controlled substances that have recently been trickling into Covington County and the state of Alabama as a whole.

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Grace’s Haven Ministries, a nonprofit organization whose mission is to help citizens in the area with their mental health, have opened a thrift store in the heart of Andalusia.

Owners Angie and Clint Bishop moved to the Andalusia area from North Alabama in 2024. “My husband is a pastor at Fairmount Baptist Church in Red Level,” Angie Bishop said. “He’s also a mental health counselor. articles.author: Sarah Kate Rhodes
articles.body:
The month of April is near to the hearts of the team at the Covington County Child Advocacy Center because it is Child Abuse prevention month. The CAC has programming planned throughout the month to help raise awareness and increase community support for victims of child abuse.

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Two LBW Community College students will soon receive prestigious recognition for exceptional academic achievement, distinguished leadership and community service.

Emma Helms of Ashford and Chloe Yates of Mobile are named as 2026 All-Alabama Academic Team members with the Alabama Community College system.

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
During their first meeting in April, the Andalusia City Council passed three resolutions: approving the use of funds to replace the damaged Adult Activity Center roof, authorizing a sales tax rebate for a new Cenergy gas station, and abating nuisance properties.

Athens High School teacher Michael May has been selected as one of the “Sweet Sixteen” finalists among educators statewide for the 2026-2027 Alabama Teacher of the Year award.

Held annually by the Alabama State Department of Education, this year’s award recently narrowed a beginning field of more than 155 teacher applicants down to the current field of 16 finalists. The first week of April marked a chance for local students to get some early glimpses at future career paths, as juniors from Athens High School took part in Job Shadowing Week — an all-local partnership between the school system, Limestone County employers, and the Athens-Limestone Chamber of Commerce.

Food Festival

articles.title: (The Owl’s Eye) Random kindness?
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Mrs. Owl was out and about yesterday. Sitting in traffic, she allowed another car to enter the lane from a side road. Why? I wondered. I thought, “Can Owls even drive?”

“Remember the long-ago idea to perform random acts of kindness?” she commented. “That’s one. Hopefully that driver will do something nice to someone else later.”

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
The Alabama Law Enforcement Agency (ALEA) is once again partnering with Alabama People Against a Littered State (PALS) and organizations across the state to support the annual “Don’t Drop It on Alabama” spring cleanup campaign.

Throughout the month of April, the initiative encourages Alabamians to take an active role in keeping their communities clean by participating in local cleanup efforts. Volunteer opportunities are available statewide for city, county, school, neighborhood and civic group

articles.title: Two found dead at home in west Athens; APD investigating
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Athens police are investigating the deaths of two local residents, after a Friday morning call drew law enforcement to a home at the Watercress Subdivision on April 10.

Athens Police Chief Anthony Pressnell said Friday afternoon that two adults had been found dead at the residence, located just west of Lucas Ferry Road in west Athens.

Pressnell added that Athens police arrived at the scene shortly after receiving an initial call at approximately 9:30 a.m. Friday, and confirmed that the two dec

articles.title: Neighborhood Bridges-Ardmore to host fundraising dinner & silent auction April 18
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
An Ardmore nonprofit on mission to give local kids their best shot at success is gearing up for its second annual fundraising dinner — and it’s looking to the Limestone County community for a boost.

Neighborhood Bridges-Ardmore will hold its 2026 fundraising dinner and silent auction on Saturday, April 18 at Old South Clays (27246 Wooley Springs Road, Athens, AL 35613), which has offered its venue space for the event since its 2025 debut.

As the Ardmore-based chapter of the national 501(c)(3)

Stephanie Bryan, Tribal chair and CEO of the Poarch Creek Indians, was selected as a Power & Influence Top 50 honoree by Alabama’s Yellowhammer News, according to a release.

The list, created as part of Yellowhammer News's 15th anniversary celebration, recognizes Alabama's most influential leaders in government, politics, and business.

articles.title: Dorman J. Thomas
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Mr. Dorman Jamie Thomas, 70, of Poarch, passed away on April 7, 2026.

Jamie was born on November 19, 1955, in Tyler Town, Miss., to Dorman Charlie Thomas and Nelda Stogner Thomas.

He is survived by his loving wife of 46 years, Shada Thomas of Poarch; his mother, Nelda Faye Stogner Thomas of Slidell, La.; his sons, Josh Thomas (Jennifer) of Poarch and Jake Thomas of Poarch; his brother, Randy Thomas of Picayune, Miss.; sister, Gwen Brady (Rodney) of Slidell; grandchildren, Brantlie Thomas, Blai

articles.title: Where History Lives: Exploring Alabama’s African American Heritage Sites
articles.body:
Compiled by Javacia Harris Bowser |The Birmingham Times

Alabama is home to a host of historic sites, monuments and museums dedicated to African American history. These places serve as powerful reminders of the resilience that has shaped the region and the nation and help to ensure that the stories of struggle, resistance, creativity, and triumph are not lost. Visiting them offers more than a history lesson; it provides an opportunity to connect with the people and movements that transformed Ame

articles.title: UAB Nursing Professor Shares How to Balance Nutrition for Long-Term Health
articles.body:
In 2024,Centers for Disease Control and Preventiondata showed that 38.9 percent of Alabama adults were obese, placing the state at fourth highest in the nation.The American Diabetes Associationprojects that this rate could climb to 58 percent by 2030, reiterating the urgent need for targeted nutrition and wellness strategies statewide.

Pamela G. Bowen, Ph.D., an associate professor at theUniversity of Alabama at BirminghamSchool of Nursingwho serves on the board of theAlabama Wellness Alliance,

articles.title: Chilton man sentenced to life in child sex case
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
On April 8, 34-year-old Charles Davis was sentenced to two life sentences for two counts of first-degree Sodomy after being found guilty earlier this year. Davis was sentenced by Circuit Judge Joy Booth who handed down a life sentence without parole for one count of first-degree Sodomy, and one life sentence for the other count.

“Charles Davis is another disgusting individual who was a danger to our communities, and his history of crimes against innocent children has now been stopped forever,”

articles.title: Fifth annual Pinwheel Parade brings hope to children of Chilton
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Butterfly Bridge Children’s Advocacy Center in Clanton held its most intimate event on April 3 with its fifth annual Pinwheel Parade through downtown Clanton. The event kicked off National Child Abuse Prevention Month for BBCAC in Chilton County and welcomed the entire community out for a moment of awareness, hope and reflection.

“The entire purpose of the month is to bring awareness and to help with prevention,” Brittani Ellison, Butterfly Bridge Children’s Advocacy Center’s Mission Advancemen

articles.title: JHS Blue Regiment band hosts first car show
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
Jemison High School’s Blue Regiment Band held its first car show fundraiser in Olde Town Jemison to raise money for a new wrap for its band trailer.

“I think we liked it, felt good about it and its potential, so we’ll try to do it for other fundraisers too,” said Jemison High School band director Dakota Bromley.

The car show was hosted on Main Street in Jemison in front of Olde Town Scoops. They ended up raising over $1,000 with 18 cars showing up.

“We had a lot of help from the business owne

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL: Big first inning, strong pitching by Drummond & Wallace helps Vinemont get past Locust Fork in area matchup, 7-4, plus Friday scoring recap
articles.body:
VINEMONT, Ala. –April has been very kind to the Vinemont Eagles so far, as they are 3-1 in their first four games of the month. They looked to keep that momentum going at home against Locust Fork on Fridaynight in a huge area game. Both teams went back and forth to start out the game, and Vinemont grabbed a lead that they wouldn’t relinquish the rest of the game, as they ended up knocking off the Hornets, 7-4.

Carson Drummond got the start for Vinemont and struck out the first two batters he fa

articles.title: Local students, leaders honored with statewide Farm-City Awards
articles.body:
Farm-City grows Alabama through service, student success

AUBURN, Ala.–Service and community involvement took center stage as farmers, leaders and students came together for the Farm-City Banquet April 9 in Auburn, recognizing achievements and embracing this year’s theme, “Grow Alabama.”

The theme was a callback to “Agriculture & Forestry Grow Alabama: An Economic Contribution Study” released last year by Alabama Agribusiness Council, Auburn University and Alabama Extension.

Alabama Farm-City

articles.title: Obituary: Gerald “Squirrely” Eugene Campbell
articles.body:
Was born July 20, 1943, in Alabama and passed away April 8, 2026, at the age of 82. Gerald was a devoted and well-known presence to many, remembered with deep respect for the life he lived and the people he valued. After this introduction, “Squirrely,” will be remembered as a steady, dedicated, and respected man whose influence reached far beyond his immediate circle. He lived in Cullman, Alabama.

“Squirrely,” was preceded in death by his mother, Lawasel Rivers Campbell, and his father, General

articles.title: Obituary: Alexander Scott Morton
articles.body:
Alexander Scott Morton, known to most as Alex, passed away on April 9, 2026, at the age of 27. Alex was born in Jefferson County, Alabama, on January 30, 1999.

Alex is preceded in death by his grandmother, Teresa Hollins; great-grandparents, Bill and Julia Worley, and Marcella and Earnest “OB” Breakfield.

Those left to cherish the memory of Alex include his mother and step-father, Cindy and Wes Hall; biological father, Grady Morton; grandfather, Kenneth Hollins; aunts and uncle, Patti Tuckwill

articles.title: Obituary: Rebecca Ann Lynn
articles.body: A Service celebrating the life of Rebecca Ann Lynn, age 85 of Cullman, will be at 6 p.m. on Saturday, April 11, 2026, at Cullman Heritage Funeral Home; burial will be Sunday at Cullman City Cemetery. Visitation will be from 5 p.m. – 6 p.m. on Saturday, April 11, 2026, at Cullman Heritage Funeral Home.Cullman Heritage Funeral Home is honored to serve the family.Mrs. Lynn passed away on Friday, April 10, 2026, at Hanceville Nursing Home and Rehab. She was born July 19, 1940, to Marion Lovvorn and

articles.title: Obituary: Charles “Elwin” Trimble
articles.body: Charles “Elwin” Trimble, age 73 of Hanceville, passed away on Wednesday, April 8, 2026, at his residence. He was born April 25, 1952, to Clifton Coy Trimble and Eneva Campbell Trimble.Beloved Husband, Daddy and Pawpaw, Elwin served in the US Airforce from 1971-1975, and retired from the 3M Company in Decatur, AL in 2007. He loved fishing, his grand kids, watching sports and old movies.He was preceded in death by his parents; brothers, Darrell Trimble and Gale Trimble.Survivors include his wife o

articles.title: Obituary: Ronnie Paul Jarrett
articles.body: Ronnie Paul Jarrett, age 69 of Addison, passed away Wednesday, April 8, 2025.  He was born February 1, 1957, to John Paul and Thelma Hayes Jarrett.Ronnie worked as an owner-operator of a trucking company for over 50 years. He loved the freedom of the open road.Graveside service will be held Sunday, April 12, 2026, at 4 p.m. at Addison Church of God cemetery. Brother Steton Hayes and Brother Mack Watts will officiate.Ronnie is survived by his son, Damon (Robyn) Jarrett; grandson, Jeb Jarrett; sis

articles.title: Obituary: Ray Kretzschmar
articles.body: Born in Garden City, Alabama, Ray was a lifelong member of the community he loved. He will be remembered as a devoted father, grandfather, veteran, neighbor, and friend, whose kindness, presence and sense of humor touched the lives of many.Ray was a welder by trade and a cattle farmer by choice. He was known for his strong work ethic and his remarkable ability to “fix” anything that was broken. A collector of sorts, he could always be relied upon to have just what was needed—anything from sheet

articles.title: Obituary: Joel Wendal Kennemur
articles.body:
Funeral services for Joel W. Kennemur, 83, of Athens will be 12 p.m., Sunday, April 12th at Hanceville Funeral Home Chapel. Burial will follow the service at Bethlehem East Cemetery. Visitation will be Saturday, April 11th from 3 p.m. until 5 p.m. at Hanceville Funeral Home.

Mr. Kennemur was born September 11, 1942, in Childersburg, Alabama to the late Flemming & Autie Kennemur. He passed away April 9, 2026, in Athens, Alabama.

Joel worked for 30 years as an electrical engineer, a career that

articles.title: Honey: Nature’s original sweetener
articles.body:
There are many sweeteners available to us, but the most interesting and diverse is honey. Honey is a product of honey bees and the only insect-derived natural sweetener widely available. Honey also comes in many flavors and textures, making it nature’s original sweetener.

Foraging for food

Honey bees rely on flowers for food. They work hard to gather food during the summer months, when flowers are abundant, and store it for use over the long, cold winter. The honey bee forager, a worker bee ab

articles.title: First graders shine in ‘The Day the Crayons Quit’ musical at CCPS
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –First-grade students of Cullman City Primary School recently took the stage in a vibrant performance of “The Day the Crayons Quit” — a celebration of creativity, confidence and self-expression.

The show is based on the beloved book by Drew Daywalt, and the students delivered a colorful and engaging performance through storytelling, songs, playing instruments and creative movement that kept the audience smiling from beginning to end.

A special highlight of the show was a guest ap

articles.title: Alabama Extension challenges residents to move more this month
articles.body:
AUBURN UNIVERSITY, Ala.-With warmer temperatures and more sunlight in the evenings, spring is the perfect time to get up, get out and move more. That’s why Move Alabama — A Community Physical Activity Challenge runs throughout the month of April.

Move Alabama is a social marketing campaign of the Expanded Food and Nutrition Education Program, also called EFNEP. As EFNEP state coordinator and a specialist with Alabama Extension at Auburn University,Erin Reznicekoversees the campaign.

“Movement

articles.title: Lady Panthers to represent at the collegiate level
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Two of Lanett’s most talented athletes, Makayla Davis and Nakeriona Heard, signed to play sports in college on Thursday. Davis will be playing flag football for Southern Union State Community College and Heard will be playing basketball for Talladega College.

Davis is an early success story for the flag football program. The program just started this school year, and the Lady Panthers already have their first signee. She was a standout for Lanett, leading the charge on both the field and the ba

articles.title: Chambers’ Paschal signs to play softball at Enterprise State
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Chambers Academy senior Allie Paschal has committed to play softball next year for Enterprise State Community College.

“Playing ball at the next level is a dream come true of mine. Ever since I started playing, I’ve wanted to play ball in college,” Paschal told Valley Times-News.

“I chose to continue my career and education at Enterprise State because I felt I would succeed the most there,” Paschal continued. “(Enterprise State Coach Clair Goodson) was truly the best coach option for me as I w

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Loran Smith: Cotton Facts
articles.author: Loran Smith
articles.body:
Having grown up on a farm where our principal cash crop was cotton, I am always interested in what is going on in our state regarding the agriculture sector.

Agriculture is still Georgia’s leading industry, but there are always challenges in crop production—most often brought about by the fickleness of the weather.

Not only do farmers pray for abundant rain, but they also need a consistency of showers throughout the growing season. In my time, I remember one year when heavy flooding took place

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Dean Collins: The Rhythm of Forgetting and Remembering
articles.author: Dean Collins
articles.body:
Today I am struck by the human struggle to stay focused and committed to our God who is constantly with us, always sustaining us, and who lavishes us with his love.

The Psalms below illustrate moments of praise and thanks when we get it and are focused on God, quickly followed by our forgetfulness and our determination to have it our way.

It is easy to read scripture and sneer at the failure of the children of Israel saying, “I can’t believe they were so ridiculous! Can’t they see that God is

articles.title: Troup County REACH Scholars explore future at UNG Gainesville
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Seventeen students from Callaway High School, LaGrange High School, and Troup High School recently attended REACH Scholar Day at University of North Georgia Gainesville Campus. The visit provided students with the opportunity to explore the university campus, learn more about college life, and gain valuable information about academic programs and scholarship opportunities.

Throughout the day, students participated in informational sessions, connected with university staff, and experienced first

articles.title: Lady Cougars building successful season
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
The Crenshaw Christian Academy Cougars are continuing the 2026 baseball season under coach Matthew Sipper and recently defeated Edgewood Academy on Monday, April 6, 15 to 0.

“I’ve got some good players from the top to the bottom,” Sipper continued. “They are a really good team. I’ve got big hopes and big aspirations for this team. They are a dream team to have.”

Olivia Scrushy and Cheyenne Owens each had three hits and three runs batted in (RBIs) in the win. Owens also hit her second homerun o

articles.title: This week in history: 2002
articles.author: Amy Lewis
articles.body:
A look back through the pages of The Luverne Journal from this week in 2002 reflects a community welcoming state leaders, celebrating its students and looking ahead to economic opportunity. The stories this week include the promise of new industry nearby and a visit from the governor.

Governor visits Luverne, highlights education and community investment

Gov. Don Siegelman visited Luverne during a “listening post” stop, where he attended a program at Luverne High School featuring elementary se

articles.title: Opinion | Can we please stop electing unserious people to do serious jobs?
articles.author: Josh Moon
articles.body:
When did it come to be that competence is a flaw and hyperbolic ignorance an asset in public service?

Seriously. When did a certain segment of our society determine that they are far more interested in soundbites, hyperbole and inch-thin tough talk than the sound, sane ability to do serious jobs? Jobs like attorney general.

That’s the position that Jay Mitchell is seeking: The Attorney General of Alabama. A position currently held by a pandering, hyperbolic, attention-seeking do-nothing who ha

articles.title: Bill to close primaries dies without vote
articles.author: Jacob Holmes
articles.body:
A bill that would have restricted Alabama primary elections to registered voters of each party died in the Senate Thursday without a vote.

HB541 by Representative Ernie Yarbrough, R-Trinity, has been one of the top priorities of the Alabama Republican Party as it hopes to stamp out any independent or Democratic voters from influencing which candidates represent the party in general elections.

The bill also had the backing of U.S. Senator Tommy Tuberville, who is looking to be the next Republic

articles.title: Alabama House passes Ten Commandments display in schools bill
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
The Alabama House of Representatives passed a bill requiring the display of the Ten Commandments in history classrooms in grades 5-12.

Senate Bill 99, sponsored by Senator Keith Kelley, R-Anniston, and carried in the House by Representative Mark Gidley, R-Hokes Bluff would also require a display within common areas, such as cafeterias and school libraries, in every school within the district.

Opponents argue it crosses the line into religious endorsement and risks marginalizing students of dif

articles.title: Bill to limit SNAP benefits passed by House, sent to governor
articles.author: Chance Phillips
articles.body:
On Wednesday, the state House passedSenate Bill 57, a bill that will require the Alabama Department of Human Resources to apply for a waiver excluding candy and soda from the list of foods that SNAP benefits can be spent on.

“To tell you that we’re one of the most unhealthier states in the union is an understatement,” Representative Reed Ingram, R-Montgomery, said at the start of the discussion. “We’re the third worst healthiest state in the country, with obesity, diabetes, and heart disease, a

articles.title: Bill to allow ALEA takeover of Montgomery police dies in House
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
The Alabama House failed to pass legislation on the final day of the 2026 session that would have mandated minimum staffing levels for certain police departments.

Senate Bill 298, sponsored by Senator Will Barfoot, R-Pike Road, would have required Class 3 municipalities to meet minimum staffing requirements. Under the bill, municipalities that fail to meet the requirements within a five-year grace period may face intervention from the state through the Alabama Law Enforcement Agency.

The bill

articles.title: Alabama Senate sends bill to limit data center tax breaks to governor
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
The Alabama Senate unanimously passed legislation on Thursday to rein in tax incentives available for Alabama data center facilities.

House Bill 399, sponsored by Representative Leigh Hulsey, R-Helena, would limit the maximum period for tax abatements available to data processing centers to 20 years, unless they meet certain community investment thresholds, beginning January 1, 2027.

Under current Alabama law, the largest data centers are eligible for abatements for up to 30 years.

The curren

articles.title: House passes bill to prohibit vaping in public spaces, rename Alabama Clean Indoor Air Act
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
The Alabama House of Representatives passedSenate Bill 9on Thursday, legislation that would restrict vaping in public spaces and rename the Alabama Clean Indoor Air Act.

Sponsored by Senator Gerald Allen, R-Tuscaloosa, the SB9 would add vapes—or “electronic nicotine delivery systems”—to the listed forms of smoking that are currently prohibited in enclosed public areas under Alabama law.

The bill would also rename the Alabama Clean Indoor Air Act to the “Vivian Davis Figures Clean Indoor Air Ac

articles.title: Virginia secures birth control access as other Southern states eye abortion pill manufacturers
articles.author: Elisha Brown
articles.body:
A giant inflatable intrauterine device is displayed outside of the Virginia Capitol in Richmond on Feb. 7, 2025. Democratic Gov. Abigail Spanberger signed legislation Wednesday that secures the right to birth control in the state and requires insurers to cover contraception. (Photo by Charlotte Rene Woods/Virginia Mercury)

Virginia’s Democratic Gov. Abigail Spanberger signed a bill Wednesday that ensures the right to contraception in the commonwealth — legislation that cleared the General Assem

articles.title: Gas prices soar by 21% as government inflation figures reflect Trump’s war on Iran
articles.author: Ashley Murray
articles.body:
An Indianapolis gas pump shows prices over $4 a gallon on Tuesday, April 7, 2026. (Photo by Niki Kelly/Indiana Capital Chronicle)

WASHINGTON — Spikes in energy prices caused by the U.S.-Israeli war in Iran drove up inflation for Americans in March, according to the latest consumer price index figures released Friday.

Costsjumped0.9% in March compared to the previous month — that’s up from the 0.3% increase in February.

Prices for all items together, including food, energy, shelter and other c

articles.body:
Members and staff of the Alabama Senate posing for a photo on the final day of the 2026 Regular Session on April 9, 2026 in Montgomery, Alabama. Thursday was also the final day in the Alabama Statehouse before the Legislature moves into a new building behind the current building. (Anna Barrett/Alabama Reflector)

Here are the bills that passed the Alabama Legislature this week.

Tuesday, April 7

House

HB 662, sponsored by Rep. Ritchie Whorton, R-Owens Cross Road, alters the boundary lines and

articles.title: Selma police chief placed on administrative leave
articles.author: Briana Wilson
articles.body:
In a 5-1 vote, Selma Police Chief Kenta Fulford was placed on administrative leave by the Selma City Council during a special called meeting on Friday, April 10.

The move came at the recommendation of Selma Mayor Johnny Moss III who said there have been ongoing issues within the Selma Police Department for several months. Moss cited multiple written directives with clear expectations, and said he provided repeated opportunities for corrective action and improvement.

Moss said his directives ha

articles.title: Unearthing History: Unique 1860s gravesite restored at Beulah Cemetery
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
This week, the Pike County Historical, Genealogical and Preservation Society completed a restoration project at Troy’s Historic Beulah Cemetery that has brought a unique lost gravesite from the 1860s back to life.

Beulah Cemetery, previously known as the “Old City Cemetery,” was Troy’s first cemetery founded in 1831. Unfortunately, a number of graves were lost when Three Notch Street was widened in the 1960s, which took part of the old cemetery. Also, many of the gravesites were unmarked and ma

articles.title: U.S. Senate may be tightest race in GOP primary
articles.author: Huck Treadwell
articles.body:
Political columnist and analyst Steve Flowers said Tommy Tuberville looks to have the Republican nomination for governor locked up, but the high-profile U.S. Senate race was a different story.

According to the Alabama Poll, the U.S. Senate race is a three-way contest between U.S. Rep. Barry Moore, Alabama Attorney General Steve Marshall and political newcomer Jared Hudson. Those three candidates have risen to the top of a six-man field that also includes Seth Burton, Dale Shelton Deas, Jr., and

articles.title: Troy University to host Association for Computing Machinery Southeast conference April 23-25
articles.author: Huck Treadwell
articles.body:
Troy University will host the Southeast’s premier computing conference, bringing in computer science professionals from across the nation and from international locations.

The Troy Department of Computer Science will host the Association for Computing Machinery Southeast conference April 23-25. Organizer, Dr. Suman Kumar, said the conference will be a significant event for the university and the community.

“In order to host this conference, we had to go through a bidding process,” Kumar said.

articles.title: Cruise-in and Quilt Show Saturday in Ramer
articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
Every now and then, there’s a Cruise-In that’s a car show and a good bit more.

That’s what visitors around Ramer will appreciate and enjoy at the “Memories from the Past Cruise-in & Quilt Show” from 11 a.m. until 2 p.m. on April 11, with an April 19 rain date.

The entry fee is free.

The men can enjoy the Cruise-In, while the women will be “hanging” around the Quilt Show. That’s not to say there won’t be a lot of going back-and-forth between the two.

Those who plan to enter the event should d

articles.title: Pike County Cattlemen's Auction set for Saturday
articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
The Pike County Cattlemen Association’s Annual Equipment Auction is set for Saturday, April 11, at Cattleman Park on Highway 231 south of Troy from 8 a.m. until.

Cattleman Dick Barnett said this year’s Equipment Sale will include farm equipment, tractors, trailers, vehicles, surplus items, antiques, housewares, wagons, cars, trucks and much more.

Vehicles from the City of Troy, the Pike County Sheriff’s Department and the Pike Commission will also be up for bid.

The vehicle auction will begin

articles.title: Benton Brothers remember Ol Rex
articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
Just how old Alex and Lee Benton were when they first met “Mr. Rex” Locklar, the brothers don’t remember.

“But, we’ll never forget him,” Lee Benton said. “Alex and I wanted to play the guitar and sing and Mr. Rex took a genuine interest in us. We had a lot to learn and he took up a lot of his time, ‘trying’ to teach us to play the guitar and sing…both at the same time. “Along the way, we grew to love bluegrass music…and Mr. Rex.”

Lee said Ol’ Rex was a positive influence in the brothers’ lives

articles.title: Memorial for Rex Locklar Saturday at Rex's Pickin' Park
articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
Bluegrass music will fill the air this weekend in the usually sleepy community of Henderson in rural Pike County,

The Rex Locklar Memorial Bluegrass Jamboree honors the memory and legacy of Rex Locklar, who for 60 years and more, hosted the Henderson Bluegrass Festival on the grounds of the old Henderson schoolhouse.

The Rex Locklar Memorial Service is set for 12-noon Saturday. Stage music will follow at the pavilion until 9 p.m.

Admission to the jamboree is free. Donations are appreciated.

articles.title: Pike County senior Vivian Holland signs with Montevallo
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
On Friday, Pike County High School senior cheerleader Vivian Holland officially accepted her cheerleading scholarship with the University of Montevallo at a signing ceremony.

While Holland said Montevallo wasn’t actually her first choice of schools, it grew on her after learning more about it.

“Montevallo wasn’t my first choice but it was the first scholarship offer I got,” she said. “I looked more into the program and it was such a beautiful town. It’s a small town and I’m really not ready fo

articles.title: Pike Lib sweeps Zion Chapel in baseball, softball action
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Pike Liberal Arts baseball and softball teams both picked up area wins over Zion Chapel High School on Thursday.

The Class 2A No. 9-ranked Pike Lib Patriots (15-11) earned an 11-0 win over Zion Chapel on the baseball diamond. Jackson Bowen led PLAS at the plate, going 2-for-4 with a double, two stolen bases, two RBIs and two runs, while Jack Baggett went 2-for-3 with two doubles and two RBIs. Bradeny Shirley scored three runs and Chase DeBoer collected two hits along with driving in a run a

articles.title: Troy University Naturalist Club to host Gopher Tortoise Day today
articles.author: Savanah Weed
articles.body:
On Friday, April 10, the Troy University Naturalist Club will present Gopher Tortoise Day at theTroy University Arboretum, a family-fun event designed to teach students about gopher tortoises and their environmental significance.

Sponsored by the Gopher Tortoise Day Grant from the Gopher Tortoise Council (GTC), the event will feature face painting, an interactive gopher tortoise burrow, a design-your-own gopher tortoise craft and a gopher tortoise fact game. Additionally, children will receive

articles.title: Alabama Extension Week highlights lifelong learning with events, webinars
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
From school classrooms and college campuses to the career field, family life and retirement, the Alabama Cooperative Extension System provides educational opportunities for every stage of life.

To promote that impact, Alabama Governor Kay Ivey has proclaimed April 12-18 as Alabama Extension Week in the state.

Alabama Extension Week Proclaimed

Alabama Extension DirectorEve Brantleyand Interim 1890 Extension AdministratorMajed Dweikjoined the governor for the proclamation signing.

“Thanks to A

articles.title: PREP ROUNDUP: West Limestone downs Ardmore for 3rd straight county softball title
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
West Limestone’s softball team added to its trophy case on Saturday.

Ali Blair hit a two-out, go-ahead single in the sixth inning, Katie Lyn Kyle pitched a beauty, and the Wildcats beat Ardmore 2-1 to clinch their third straight county tournament title.

Briley Jo Lawhorn put the Tigers ahead 1-0 in the second inning with an RBI single, but West Limestone answered in the bottom half of the frame following Blair’s run-scoring double.

The score remained tied until Blair’s sixth-inning heroics.

articles.title: Escambia Academy’s Cameron Perkins wins AISA Class 1A triple jump title at state track meet
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Escambia Academy’s track and field team competed well at the AISA Class 1A state track meet this week.

The Cougars were led by Cameron Perkins, who finished as the state champion in boys triple jump.

Other state results included:

• Girls 4x100-meter relay - Ensley Boykin, Kimorah Goldsmith, Brilee Handley and Camille Nichols, 10th place

• Boys 4x100-meter relay - Perkins, Jett Tillery, Kai Bartholomew and Tripp Lassiter, ninth place

• Girls 200-meter run - Goldsmith, 10th place

• Boys 200-

articles.title: Atmore Rotary Club holds annual fundraiser Saturday at Bushy Creek Clays
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
The Atmore Rotary Club held its annual Benefit Clay Shoot April 11 at Bushy Creek Clays in Perdido.

The clay shoot is held each year to help raise funds for the club's community projects.

Pro and amateur teams competed in the annual shoot.

Winners by category included:

Pro

• Team of Shirley D. Darby - first place

Amateur

• Team of United Bank - first place

• Team of First National Bank and Trust - second place

• Team of Huxford Pole and Timber - third place

articles.title: Lady Chiefs defeat Destin, Fla. 15-0 on senior night Friday
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Northview’s Lady Chiefs defeated Destin, Fla. 15-0 on senior night Friday in Bratt.

The Lady Chiefs scored two runs in the first inning, one run in the second, four runs in the third and eight runs in the fourth.

Daviona Randolph went 3-for-4 with two RBIs and runs each to lead the Lady Chiefs. Kylee Langham went 2-for-3 with a triple, and three RBIs and runs each. Addysen Bolen went 2-for-2 with three runs and an RBI.

Peyton Womack struck out six and allowed no earned runs on three hits in 4

articles.title: Hurricanes defeat Highland Home 15-5 on senior night Friday
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Flomaton’s Kam Martin recorded three hits to help lead the Hurricanes to a 15-5 victory over Highland Home on senior night Friday.

Martin went 3-for-4 with three runs and an RBI in the effort.

The Hurricanes scored eight runs in the fifth inning during the win. Brayer Chavers hit an RBI on a ground out; Chaz Odom hita two-run single; Michael Wedgeworth hit an RBI single; Riley Harrison hit an RBI single; and Landon Kelley hit a two-run single in the eighth.

FHS went ahead 3-0 in the first inn

articles.title: COLUMN: I’ve never been to Oklahoma, but I’ve been to Spain
articles.body:
Yes, I know the songs says, “Well, I’ve never been to Spain, but I’ve been to Oklahoma,” but the opposite is now true for me. I have now visited Spain and every state on every side of Oklahoma, but not Oklahoma.  Eventually, I’ll get to Oklahoma and Delaware to complete my 50 states. I must say that Spain was special and spectacular.

I know we’ve heard that the rain in Spain falls mainly on the plains. We spent most of our first week in Spain traveling along the Mediterranean coast in the south

articles.title: Files from Yesteryear: April 11, 1946
articles.body:
From the files of April 11, 1946

To and Fro

By EAGLE EYE

“I’m a school boy, age nine. I’m interested in the Strawberry Festival parade and would like to know if I could ride my bicycle in it” writes Bresee H. Vest to the parade chairman, Mrs. Henry F. Arnold. “Sure, Bresee, get your bicycle decorated, and tell your pals to get theirs decorated, too, and be ready for the big event on May 16th,” says Mrs. Arnold. Bresee was the first individual to announce his intention of entering the parade,

articles.title: FAITH: Cullman’s Rachel Hoffman discusses her faith and being a role model for the youth
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –Cullman senior basketball player Rachel Hoffman is so grateful to have God in her life, and He has changed her life in so many ways.

“God means a great deal to me, because He loves me, protects me, and most importantly, He believes in me. I’ve always known God has been with me through the good times and the bad times, and He will continue to be by my side, no matter what I go through. That’s why He means so much to me. People will come and go in your life, but the one true person

articles.title: Sacred Heart holding fundraising walk from Hanceville to Cullman April 12
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Sacred Heart School at Sacred Heart of Jesus Catholic Church is sponsoring a fundraising walk from the church’s mission church, St. Boniface in Hanceville, to the main church and school in Cullman on Sunday, April 12. The walk will begin at 2 p.m., and shuttles will begin leaving Sacred Heart for Hanceville at 12:45 p.m.

Sacred Heart Pastor Fr. Linus Klucsaritz told The Tribune, “Over 100 walkers have collected sponsors and raised money to support the school endowment, improve sc

articles.title: Wallace State hosts Jill of All Trades event
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– Wallace State Community College hosted the first-ever Jill of All Trades event in Alabama on Friday, April 10. Hosted across the campus, the one-day event was a hands-on career exploration program designed to introduce young women to high-demand skilled trades such as manufacturing, construction, transportation and more through a mix of instruction and interactive experiences.

Wallace State Regional Workforce Director and Director of Work-Based Learning Audrey Webb said, “The

articles.title: Hanceville Council talks online youth sports registration
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala. –The Hanceville City Council at is April 9 meeting addressed online registration for youth parks and recreation league sports.

Council members voted to use Jarvis Recreation Management registration management software as a way to make signups more efficient.

In comparison, there were three in-person signups this year. The council said using online registration would make it easier for parents and caregivers, who would normally have to be at a sign-up location by 4:30 p.m.

The

articles.title: Diversicare accepting formal wear for senior prom
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Diversicare of Lanett is seeking donations of formal wear for a senior prom for their residents this year.

Many of their seniors are unable to afford formal wear, and Diversicare wants each of them to feel confident and ready for the prom. Diversicare is accepting donations of new and gently used formal dresses, suits and other dress attire until Friday, May 15. Donations can be dropped off at the nursing home, located at 702 S. 13th St., Lanett. Diversicare is open to accept donations from 8 a

articles.title: Ugandan children’s choir to perform benefit concert in Lanett
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
The Imani Milele Choir, an acclaimed children’s choir from Uganda, is embarking on its 2026 U.S. tour and will make a stop next month in Chambers County. The tour will have a benefit concert on May 17, starting at 7 p.m. ET at Happy Valley Baptist Church, located at 1623 49th Ave. SW, Lanett.

The Lanett stop will be one of a few rare stops for them in the state of Alabama this year, with the choir planning to tour Alabama more in the future. The Imani Milele Choir will be performing a free conc

articles.title: Thrower seeks coroner’s seat
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
Helping and consoling people in emergency situations has been a big part of Sam Thrower’s adult life. That’s why he’s seeking the office of coroner in the upcoming Republican Primary. He’s one of five candidates who will be on the ballot in the May 19th election.

Thrower, 51, is a native of the Lakeview community and a 1992 graduate of Lanett High School. He graduated from Southern Union State Community College in 1994 and earned certification as a paramedic. He then went to work for Lanett Fir

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - L. Michelle Moore: Choosing our Futures
articles.author: L. Michelle Moore
articles.body: As the Artemis II returns to Earth after a 10-day voyage during which its crew travelled further than any human has before, I’ve been reflecting on what our return to space will mean to the future. NASA has been forthright about its intent to commercialize the moon. Others are exploring the promise of unlimited space-based solar power to help keep energy abundant and affordable. Responding to pressure that AI data centers are putting on our land, energy, and water resources, some tech companies

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - John Tures: Should We Be Bombing The Iranian People Back To The Stone Age?
articles.author: John A. Tures
articles.body:
A few weeks ago, we saw scenes of Iranian people in the streets of Tehran and elsewhere in the country, protesting their government. We all hoped that they would succeed, bringing down their cruel regime. But last night, we learned our policy would be to unleash “impending strikes” that “would take their country ‘back to the Stone Age,’” according to the Wall Street Journal.

Will our attacks manage to avoid those young people who protested, who hate their regime, who crave economic and politica

articles.title: Lindsay talks about International Sports Chaplains
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
Dennis Lindsay talked about a recent trip he took to Italy at Monday’s meeting of the Valley Lions Club. He was with the International Sports Chaplains (ISC) on a mission to share the word of God and the message of Jesus Christ through the Jesus Pin.

This ministry began more than three decades ago at the Lillehammer Winter Games in Norway and has continued at each Olympics since then. This year, ISC chaplains including Lindsay were in two locations, at the coastal city of Milan and in the Alpin

articles.title: Update on West Point’s Fairfield Inn
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
The West Point Development Authority has moved forward on the proposed Fairfield Inn & Suites by Marriott on land once owned by Coca-Cola at 1700 10th St. in West Point. The development authority held a meeting on April 8 to review a conveyance agreement to move the project forward.

During the meeting, West Point Economic Development Director Meghan Richardson explained to the rest of the development authority that the conveyance agreement is part of the project’s “hotel development process tim

articles.title: Extra Mile announces grand opening in Valley on April 24
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
KB, owner of the new Extra Mile, is proud to announce the grand opening of their location at 377 Fob James Dr., Valley, on Friday, April 24. The official ribbon-cutting ceremony, held with the Greater Valley Area Chamber of Commerce, will begin at 11 a.m. ET.

To celebrate joining the Valley community, Extra Mile is offering a massive two-hour Special Fuel Pricing event (minimum 20-gallon purchase per customer). Additionally, customers can enter to win the ultimate summer perk: free coffee for t

articles.title: Highland Home seniors awarded SAEC scholarships
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body: Each year South Alabama Electric Cooperative (SAEC) offers $1,000 scholarships to graduating seniors from each school in its service area who will be attending a 4-year university, 2-year community college or technical trade school. To qualify, the student’s parent(s) or legal guardian must be a member of SAEC. This year’s scholarships for Highland Home High School will be awarded to Cody Elliott and Nataleigh Myrick.

articles.title: Seeing and believing a risen Savior
articles.author: Dunford Cole
articles.body:
He is risen; He is risen indeed! I hope you and your family enjoyed a wonderful Holy Week and a meaningful Easter Sunday. This season reminds us of the greatest event ever—the resurrection of our Lord and Savior, Jesus Christ.

This Sunday, we focus on the story of Thomas and when Jesus appeared to the disciples in the upper room. Imagine how they must have felt. Only days before, they walked with Jesus, talked with Him, and saw His miracles. Then they watched Him suffer, die on the cross, and b

articles.title: Two very different extractions
articles.author: R.A. Mathews
articles.body:
It will be remembered as a high-stakes, daring plan, next to impossible to accomplish — the rescue of the injured airman who ejected from an F-15E Strike Eagle shot down over mountainous territory deep within Iran. The officer’s parachute and seat alone announced his location to enemy forces, and the Iranian government reportedly offered $60,000 for his head.

The U.S. mounted a massive campaign to retrieve him. Everyone knew the airman would be extracted one way or another — alive by us or dead

articles.title: A new gold rush: States stockpile bars, encourage gold-backed debit cards
articles.author: Kevin Hardy
articles.body:
Gold bars are photographed at the Texas Bullion Depository, the nation’s first state-run depository for precious metals. More states are eying legislation on gold to hedge against inflation. (Photo courtesy of Texas Comptroller’s Office)

More states are piling up gold bars, or encouraging residents to use gold-backed debit cards, to hedge against inflation.

Several states, including Western ones with rich mining histories, have been stockpiling gold as part of their broader state savings. And

articles.title: Alabama’s iconic ‘Go to Church’ billboard reimagined in Coachella Valley
articles.body:
An iconic Alabama roadside sign known for its blunt message and unmistakable imagery is finding new life in an unexpected setting, as singer-songwriter Ethel Cain reimagines the landmark billboard for a national audience as a form of advertisement for the singer’s current tour.

The original“Go to Church or the Devil Will Get You” sign, a familiar sight along Interstate 65 near Prattville, has long been one of the state’s most recognizable roadside landmarks.

Now, a variation of that message ha

articles.title: Suspected hand grenade found in topsoil delivery in Morgan County; bomb squad responds
articles.body:
A suspected hand grenade discovered in a load of topsoil prompted a response from law enforcement and a bomb squad inMorgan Countythis week.

According to the Morgan County Sheriff’s Office, deputies were called to a residence on Husky Mountain Private Drive near Lacey’s Spring after homeowners reported finding the device while going through recently delivered topsoil.

“Deputies have responded to a residence on Husky Mountain Private Drive in Lacey’s Spring where a possible hand grenade was une

articles.title: Michael J. Brooks: Is the church after your money?
articles.body:
Jim came to see me with a message from the finance committee he chaired. The group met when I was with another group, so I missed their meeting. I’d begun to use cards for Sunday guests that were actually envelopes. The simple message on the envelopes was “Please use my gift as an expression of my love for Christ.”

My predecessor schooled the church on a doctrine of the “contemporary” church; namely, no visitors should be asked for money. The argument is that many people thought the church was

articles.title: Alabama’s ‘Lulu’s Law’ shark alert system now in effect for Gulf Coast beaches
articles.body:
A newshark alert systemdesigned to notify beachgoers of confirmed attacks along Alabama’s Gulf Coast is now officially in effect.

The Alabama Department of Conservation and Natural Resources (ADCNR) announced the rollout of the system under “Lulu’s Law,” signed byGovernor Kay Ivey in 2025, establishing new safety protocols for Baldwin and Mobile counties.

The system will send alerts to mobile devices and first responders when a confirmed unprovoked shark attack occurs near the shoreline, using

articles.title: Alabama’s Willy Wilcox returns to Masters in new role after overcoming addiction
articles.body: Willy Wilcox’sgolfjourney has been anything but straightforward. Once aPGA Tourplayer earning millions, he stepped away after years of battling addiction. Now, he’s back at the game’s highest stage—the Masters—this time as a caddie.

articles.title: Racing Honda Odyssey wins 2026 ‘Coolest Thing Made in Alabama’ title
articles.body:
The Racing Honda Odyssey has been named the 2026 “Coolest Thing Made in Alabama,” following the conclusion of Manufacture Alabama’s annual Manufacturing Madness competition.

The winning product was announced Tuesday during an awards ceremony at the Center for Commerce in Montgomery, where Alabama Department of Commerce Secretary Ellen McNair presented the honor.

The statewide competition drew more than 50,000 votes and featured entries from a range of industries, including aerospace, automotiv

articles.title: Steve Flowers: Several state court seats up this year
articles.body:
We have a nine-memberState Supreme Court. The U.S. Supreme Court is also comprised of nine members. The nine U.S. Supreme Court Justices are appointed for life. When one retires or dies, which is seldom, the President of the United States makes the appointment to fill the rare vacancy.

Our Alabama Supreme Court does not have this “appointment for life” luxury. They are not appointed, they are elected. However, their terms are for six years. Once they are elected, they seem to never get opposed

articles.title: David Rainer: Cooper gains redemption at Governor’s One-Shot Turkey Hunt
articles.body:
Luke Cooper had to wait two long years for an opportunity for redemption at the AlabamaGovernor’s One-Shot Turkey Hunt, which showcases Alabama’s great outdoors and southern hospitality every two years. Landowners from across the state host hunters from diverse corporate and industry backgrounds to enlighten the participants about the opportunities available in Alabama.

In 2024, Cooper was hunting with Justin Rock of Riverview Farms near Tuscaloosa when, unfortunately, Cooper’s shot was off tar

articles.title: Limestone Child Advocacy Center to honor child abuse victims with April 15 Save Jane observance
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
The Limestone Child Advocacy Center (LCAC) will hold its first annual Save Jane observance on Wednesday, April 15, 2026, at 10:30 a.m. on the east steps of the Limestone County Courthouse in downtown Athens. The ceremony is a solemn and dignified occasion honoring every child reported as a victim of child abuse in Limestone County over the past year.

To protect the identity and privacy of victims, each child will be represented by the name “Jane Doe” or “John Doe” throughout the reading. Their

articles.title: Ivey announces record CHOOSE Act applications for upcoming Alabama school year
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Alabama Governor Kay Ivey announced in April that Alabama’s education savings account program — the CHOOSE Act — had a record number of applicants in its second year. The application officially closed at midnight on March 31 with almost 49,000 students applying.

The 2026-27 CHOOSE Act application deadline closed with 29,341 applications representing 48,927 total students. Of these, there were 18,832 new applications representing 29,986 total students with 10,509 applications for renewal represe

articles.title: ALDOT installs dynamic message signs on I-65 in Limestone County
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Drivers along Interstate 65 in Limestone County may have recently noticed a new roadside feature that soon will add a helpful public safety boost to travel along the busy highway.

This week, contractors with the Alabama Department of Transportation (ALDOT) erected a pair of new dynamic message signs (DMS) on I-65 in north Alabama, part of a $2.5 million advanced corridor management project from Exit 334 at State Route 67 in Priceville to Exit 354 at U.S. Highway 31 in Athens. The project, says

articles.title: Sheriff Joshua McLaughlin completes National Sheriffs’ Institute leadership course
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Limestone County Sheriff Joshua McLaughlin recently completed the 124th session of the Leadership Development course offered by the National Sheriffs’ Institute (NSI) — the nation’s only executive leadership program designed specifically for sheriffs.

Held in Quantico, Virginia, the no-cost course selected McLaughlin alongside 23 other nationwide sheriffs “to deeply assess their individual leadership strengths and define a clear leadership direction tailored to their unique roles as elected off

articles.title: Miles College Establishes Abascal Student Success Center, Advancing Student Achievement
articles.body:
FAIRFIELD –Miles College recently dedicated the Abascal Student Success Center on the fourth floor of the C.A. Kirkendoll Learning Resources Center. The event brought together the Suco and Abascal families, college leadership, students, faculty, and staff to honor a legacy defined by courage, sacrifice, and the transformative power of education.

The Abascal Student Success Center is a comprehensive hub that supports student achievement and persistence. It integrates academic advising, tutoring,

articles.title: In Annual Letter, JPMorganChase CEO Jamie Dimon Shares Plans for State of Alabama
articles.body:
By Jamie Dimon |JPMorganChase

Investing in Alabama

For over 50 years, JPMorganChase has helped drive economic and job growth, support businesses of all sizes and put the American Dream within reach for more Alabamians. It’s clear that Alabama’s future is bright, and we are excited for what’s next.

We’ve been working hand in hand with local governments, businesses and community partners to understand how we can best use JPMorganChase’s full range of resources to complement their efforts. I was

articles.title: BOOK REVIEW: ‘The Incredible Winston Browne’ by Sean Dietrich
articles.body:
This novel by Sean Dietrich has a dual storyline. The first is about Sheriff Winston Browne and the residents of Moab. The second is about a young girl who is being held captive in a cult.

Set in the 1950s, “The Incredible Winston Browne” is a heartwarming novel. Winston has never married and his still friends with the high school classmates. Early in the novel the reader learns that Winston has terminal lung cancer. He is keeping his illness a secret, even from his best friend Jimmy.

Jimmy ha

articles.title: 1 dead in 2-vehicle wreck in Walter
articles.body:
WALTER, Ala.– One person died in a two-vehicle wreck Sunday evening in the Walter community in Cullman County.

The wreck involved an 18-wheeler and an SUV and occurred on Alabama Highway 91 near County Road 781.

Cullman County Coroner Jeremy Kilpatrick confirmed one fatality.

More information will be shared as it becomes available.

articles.title: ARCHERY: Vinemont Elementary takes 2026 NASP State Championship behind top ten marks from Stracqualursi and Phillips
articles.body:
MONTGOMERY, Ala. –Teams from across Alabama met at the Cramton Bowl Multiplex in Montgomery on Friday for the 2026 NASP Alabama State Championship. After finishing second in the 3-D State competition behind East Elementary in March, Vinemont Elementary’s team got to celebrate with gold medals, taking first place with 3127 team points.

Leading the Eagles’ boys was Ethan Stracqualursi, who nailed 11 tens on the way to a personal best 276 points. On the other side, Madelyn Phillips hit the bullsey

articles.title: FAITH: Get to know Cullman Christian School
articles.body:
With the 2025-2026 school year coming to a close, registration for next year is now open at Cullman Christian School. The school welcomes families to learn more about enrolling, as the school continues to grow and expand its campus.

Principal Heath Yearwood explained that the school’s foundation is a Christ-centered approach to education, which he believes makes it unique.

“What I think that is a little different about Cullman Christian is our Christ-centered education first,” Yearwood said. “

articles.title: A moment with new UWCC Executive Director Tyler Hooper
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Tyler Hooper officially stepped into his role as executive director of the United Way of Cullman County on March 2, 2026.

Hooper said the community has played a significant role in shaping who he is, so having the opportunity to now serve it in this capacity is both an honor and a responsibility he takes seriously.

“I’ve had the opportunity to engage with United Way events and initiatives over the years, including through Youth Leadership Cullman County and as a former board mem

articles.title: Tomberlin's hometown show returns to Luverne
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
Bobby Tomberlin, a Nashville song writer and country music artist is making his way back to Luverne for a hometown show on Saturday, April 18. The show is to take place at the Luverne United Methodist Church DEI Center at 6 p.m.

Produced by Sesa Productions, the Bobby Tomberlin & Friends Show will also feature Linda Davis, Lang Scott and Tomberlin's longtime girlfriend, Tess Frizzell.

“It’s been three years since I’ve done a hometown show,” Tomberlin said. “A lot has happened in those three ye

articles.body:

I’d begun to use cards for Sunday guests that were actually envelopes. The simple message on the envelopes was “Please use my gift as an expression of my love for Christ.”

articles.title: Before it is too late
articles.author: Dean Kelly
articles.body:
We had an extremely busy weekend, the weekend before last. We had grandchildren come. Then on Sunday we had a family Easter egg hunt with about 25 people at the house. It was wonderful, but very hectic. And I forgot — until I was too late.

I need to have these articles in by Monday during the day. In all the busy-ness of the weekend I forgot. For the first time in all the months I have been sending in articles to print, I did not have one in the papers last week. I was too late!

I thought of t

articles.title: Opinion | Stop treating those dogs like we treat humans!
articles.author: Josh Moon
articles.body:
As state Representative Patrick Sellers sat in his seat in the House chambers last week, listening to the raging, hours-long debate over a bill to protect chained dogs, he found himself a bit dumbfounded.

The debate followed a couple of weeks of intense lobbying by thousands of Alabamians of all different political stripes. They were mostly dog lovers and their passion for the cause was obvious.

They emailed. They called. They texted. They showed up in droves to get their point across.

They w

articles.title: The V: Alabama session ends quietly as power shifts and GOP tensions rise
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
This week onThe Voice of Alabama Politics,the Legislature wraps its 2026 session—but the real story isn’t just what passed, it’s what didn’t. From a session defined by caution over bold reform, to a quiet but consequential power shift inside the Alabama Senate, and growing fractures within the Republican supermajority, the dynamics shaping Alabama’s future are coming into sharper focus.

Bill Britt, Susan Britt and Josh Moon break down the biggest moments: who held power, who gained more of it,

articles.title: Sen. Britt touts increased funding for Mobile Bay dredging projects
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
U.S. Senator Katie Britt, R-Ala., recently announced a $34 million increase in the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers’Fiscal Year 2026 Work Planfor projects in the Mobile Bay. According to the Corps, the additional funds will be used to continue dredging efforts in the Bay to give large cargo ships access to the Mobile Ship Channel.

Britt celebrated the new funding—a 60 percent increase from the Corps’ 2025 budget—in a recent statement.

“It’s Alabama the Beautiful for a reason,” Britt said. “Mobile

articles.title: Opinion | Gov. Ivey’s steady leadership has made Alabama stronger
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
Nine years ago, on April 10, 2017, Kay Ivey took the oath of office under circumstances few governors ever face.

“There are moments when history moves quickly,” she said at the time. “Most governors have three months to prepare; I had three hours.”

It was a line that captured both the urgency of the moment and the uncertainty facing Alabama. The state had been rocked by scandal, trust in government was fragile, and stability—not ambition—was the immediate need.

Now, nearly a decade later, Ala

articles.title: Panel hosting state advocacy groups recaps major bills of 2026 session
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
During a forum convened last week to recap the 2026 session, leaders of advocacy groups from across Alabama shared their views on some of the year’s most impactful bills.

Thepanelwas convened by the Birmingham-based citizen activist groups Make a Difference in Alabama and H.I.V.E. Alabama, marking the sixth annual Alabama Legislative Review hosted by Make a Difference.

The event saw discussions of legislation advanced and passed during the 2026 session from leaders representing the League of W

articles.title: Governor Ivey marks historic nine years in office
articles.author: Staff
articles.body: Governor Kay Ivey on Friday marked nine years since being sworn in as the 54thgovernor of the state of Alabama. The governor’s historic tenure includes major achievements. Under Ivey’s leadership, Alabama has seen “record investments and job creation, nationally recognized education gains, transformational progress in the state’s roads, bridges and digital infrastructure, increased public safety measures, improvements to the state’s correctional system, reforms to state government, protection of

articles.title: Gov. Ivey signs into law stronger penalties for terrorist threats in schools and other public spaces
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Friday signed into law House Bill 7, which strengthens criminal penalties for those who make terroristic threats in schools and other public spaces.

“If Alabama is to remain the best place to live, work and raise a family, we must ensure all of our classrooms are the safest environments for learning,” Ivey said. “Terroristic threats made against our schools are meant to create fear among students and their parents and undermine security. The wellbeing of Alabama’s children

articles.title: Gov. Ivey signs bill mandating Ten Commandments displays in some schools
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Friday signed Senate Bill 99, related to the display of the Ten Commandments in schools. The bill, sponsored by Senator Keith Kelley and Representative Mark Gidley, requires the display of the Ten Commandments, along with their historical significance, in certain public schools, subject to the availability of donations.

The governor issued the following comment:

“In Alabama, we proudly remember that we live in one nation under God. The Ten Commandments–like the Declaration

articles.title: Mobile pediatrician becomes new president of Medical Association of the State of Alabama
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Nina Ford Johnson, M.D., a Mobile pediatrician, was sworn in as the new president of the Medical Association of the State of Alabama during the Association’s annual meeting on Friday.

“A physician’s first responsibility is always to our patients. I am honored to lead an organization that is dedicated to supporting physicians and advocating for policies that allow us to provide the best possible care to our patients,” said Johnson said.

Originally from Indiana, Johnson has been practicing as a

articles.title: Alabama Legislature 2026: A ‘get in, get out’ session that wasn’t

articles.body: A group of senators and personnel gathered around a lectern on the floor of the Alabama Senate on April 7, 2026. Clockwise, left to right: Sen. Vivian Davis Figures, D-Mobile; Jess Skaggs, chief of staff for Senate President Pro Tem Garlan Gudger; Sen. Arthur Orr, R-Decatur; Sen. Jack Williams, R-Wilmer; Senate President Pro Tem Garlan Gudger, R-Cullman and Sen. Gerald Allen, R-Cottondale. articles.title: The terrible lows (and handful of peaks) in the 2026 Alabama Legislature
articles.author: Brian Lyman
articles.body:
(Left to right) Alabama House Speaker Nathaniel Ledbetter, R-Rainville; Alabama Lt. Gov. Will Ainsworth and Alabama Senate President Pro Tem Garlan Gudger, R-Cullman, preside over a joint session of the Alabama Legislature honoring veterans on March 19, 2026 at the Alabama Statehouse in Montgomery, Alabama. (Brian Lyman/Alabama Reflector)

The Alabama Legislature has seemed discombobulated in recent years.

It’s not just what happenedon Thursday. The last day of a session is always frantic. Bill

articles.title: Immigration-related bills stall in Alabama Legislature’s 2026 session
articles.author: Ralph Chapoco
articles.body:
The Hernandez Family stands in solidarity with other non violent protesters against anti immigration bills in Congress in Birmingham, Alabama, on Saturday February 22, 2025. At least five bills targeting immigrants were introduced in the 2026 regular session of the Alabama Legislature, but none passed. (Andi Rice for Alabama Reflector)

Immigration consumed the Alabama Legislature in 2025. Not this year.

Lawmakers considered a total of seven bills that would have affected immigrants in several

articles.title: Ten Commandments in classrooms, Planned Parenthood out: ALCAP President applauds Alabama legislative session wins
articles.body:
The Alabama Citizens Action Program (ALCAP), a group that represents close to 3,500 churches as Moral Advocates in the state, is lauding the work of Republicans in the Legislature for passing bills that reflect the Christian values of the Yellowhammer State.

During the final days of the 2026 legislative session, lawmakers passed a piece of legislation that requires schools to display the Ten Commandments, and another piece of the legislation that requires abstinence only teaching, also called “

articles.title: Four dead, three critical after weekend of bloodshed in Montgomery – Chief Graboys says ‘public safety’ belongs to ‘the legislature’ and ‘judges’
articles.body:
Four people are dead and three others are clinging to life after a wave of shootings tore through Alabama’s capital city between Friday afternoon and Sunday morning, Montgomery Police Chief Jim Graboyssaid at a Sunday press conference.

Officers responded to seven separate shooting incidents in roughly 48 hours.

The weekend pushes Montgomery’s homicide count to 14 for the year.

In his press conference, Graboys didn’t keep the blame inside his own department. He told reporters his officers keep

articles.title: Madison County DA op-ed: Mitchell misfires in attack on Attorney General’s Office
articles.body:
I read with interest Mr. Mitchell’s editorial entitled “Alabama’s War on Good Cops Must End”. As the District Attorney of Madison County, I was struck by the piece for a couple of reasons.

The first, and most glaring, is the perpetuation for political gain of the myth that there exists a war on cops in this State; and secondly, Mr. Mitchell’s confusion as to who the prosecuting authority is in these cases.

Mr. Mitchell attributes the prosecution of these two cases to the Attorney General’s Off

articles.title: AARP Alabama: Thank you lawmakers for cracking down on crypto kiosk fraud
articles.body:
Fraud is one of the fastest-growing crimes in Alabama, and it causes real harm to real people every day. That is why AARP Alabama thanks the members of the Alabama Legislature and Governor Kay Ivey for making House Bill 303 law.

We especially thank State Rep. Russell Bedsole (R-Alabaster) for sponsoring this legislation. This important new law will help protect Alabamians — especially older adults — from devastating financial scams.

House Bill 303, the Cryptocurrency Kiosk Fraud Prevention Act

articles.title: New Birmingham organization tackles ‘capital cliff’ facing mid-sized businesses
articles.body:
In Alabama and across the South, many growingbusinessesreach a familiar turning point. Revenue is steady, demand is there, and the foundation is strong—but growth begins to stall. It’s not for lack of effort or potential, but because the next level of capital, connections, and strategic guidance is often out of reach.

That gap is exactly whatThe Capital Collectivewas built to address. Founded in 2024 by Emily Halpern and Tamika Tyson, the Birmingham-based organization focuses on what they call

articles.title: CK Cattle named Alabama’s 2026 Farm of Distinction
articles.body:
TheAlabama Farmers Federationannounced that a Lowndes County cattle operation has been recognized for its impact on agriculture and community engagement statewide.

CK Cattle in Hope Hull was named Alabama’s 2026 Farm of Distinction during the Alabama Farm-City Awards Luncheon held in Auburn on April 9.

The three-generation operation is managed by Chuck and Katie Madaris alongside their family. The farm produces Angus, Chiangus and SimAngus seedstock cattle, as well as commercial cattle.

articles.title: The Stories We Forget: 5 Ways to Keep Family History Alive
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body: It usually happens without anyone noticing. At the dinner table someone begins to tell a story of how they met their wife, how the town used to look several years ago, something that has happened back in the day. People are smiling, perhaps laughing, perhaps they have heard it. Then the moment passes. Nobody writes it down. Nobody records it. And further on, the details are more difficult to remember or the story is forgotten completely. If you’ve ever thought “I should save this somehow,” you’r

articles.title: Dustin Earl Holcomb Obituary
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Dustin Earl Holcomb

Dustin Earl Holcomb, age 27 of Andalusia passed away unexpectedly. Funeral services will be held at 2 p.m., Tuesday, April 14, 2026, at Keahey Funeral Home Chapel. The family will receive friends from 12 noon until time of the services. Pastor Aurther Hornsby and Pastor Rocky Pierce will officiate. Interment will follow in Stone Lake Gardens. Serving as pallbearers will be Josh King, Matthew Cook, Brad Tillman, Myles Cook, Travis Cooper and Joseph Jones.

Left to cherish his

articles.title: City council approves upcoming events; heard from department heads, citizens
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
The Atmore City Council approved a couple event requests and a proclamation during its regular meeting on Monday.

The council approved Danny Rolin’s request to have the annual Sharon Brown Car Show at the State Line Pawn Shop on Sat., April 25.

The car show, in memory of Brown, will be held on the commercial part of First Avenue.

Additionally, Rolin, a former WWE wrestler, will be wrestling his final match during the event.

In other business, the council:

• approved Women of Distinction’s r

articles.title: Statewide fire alert issued
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
With worsening drought conditions across the state and low relative humidity, State Forester Rick Oates has issued a statewide Fire Alert effective immediately.

Over the past weekend, 50 wildfires burned more than 2,300 acres of forestland across the state. This includes a 500-acre wildfire in Mobile County, one for 412 acres in Coosa County, one for 382 acres in Choctaw County, one for 260 acres in Washington County, and another one for 105 acres in Escambia County.

In the last 30 days, 387 w

articles.title: Dorothy Pierce Oatman
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Dorothy Pierce Oatman died from a prolonged illness on January 5, 2026 in New York City, N.Y.

She was born January 24, 1933 in Northport, the only child of Melodee Green and James Elexander Pierce.

Dorothy was part of the extended family of the Joe Lewis Pierce family from Poarch.

Dorothy spent her childhood in Alabama with many visits to family farms scattered across south Alabama.

Dorothy was always interested in learning whether it was formal education or life skills. She became a nurse i

articles.title: Midweek Magic: How Sloss Furnaces Brings History to Life for Students
articles.body:
If you think Sloss Furnaces is only a place to visit on special occasions, here’s a little insider knowledge: Every Wednesday, the historic site comes alive with the energy of students discovering Birmingham’s roots in a hands-on, unforgettable way.

Learning Where It Actually Happened

There’s something powerful about standing in the very place where history unfolded. For students visiting Sloss Furnaces on field trips, this isn’t just another lesson from a textbook, it’s a full-on experience.

articles.title: HBCU Basketball Association Hosts Free Pro Tryouts in Birmingham, Bringing Elite Talent and Community Together
articles.body:
HBCU Basketball Association

The HBCU Basketball Association (HBCUBA) will host a free professional open tryout on Sunday, April 19, from 3:30 to 6:30 p.m. at Holy Family Cristo Rey Catholic High School.

Athletes from across the Southeast will compete for placement on one of 10 teams in the league’s inaugural summer season. The event is free and open to the public, inviting families, youth, and community members to experience high-level basketball at no cost.
