articles.title: Xander fever —  Xander Whitfield goes viral on TikTok
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
Xander Whitfield, a 14-year-old from Thorsby, has recently seen five videos of himself singing go viral on TikTok, catapulting him into local-celebrity status.

“One of the videos has gotten over eight million views in a week; that was kind of shocking, but I am really just proud of him,” said Adina Hines, Whitfield’s mother. “When he goes in town, people are starting to recognize him, and that’s the part that’s crazy.”

After starting TikTok in December, Whitfield posted one video in January th

articles.title: Column: The Five Pillars of the Reformation: Solus Christus
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
By Hank Walker | Pastor at Peach City Fellowship

In the chorus of the Protestant Reformation, one clear and unwavering note rings out: SOLUS CHRISTUS—Christ Alone. This third pillar of the Reformation affirms that salvation is

accomplished solely through the person and work of Jesus Christ, apart from any human

mediator, merit, action, decision, or institution. No tenet of the Christian faith can compete with this central truth in which we exult this time of year (Easter/Resurrection), or eve

articles.title: The Next Step — Monuments honoring fallen Chilton service members moved to new courthouse
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
The next big step to set the stage for the grand opening of the new Chilton County Courthouse was taken on April 8 as the Chilton County memorial monuments for fallen soldiers were moved. The monuments were taken from downtown Clanton to the new location on Lay Dam Road where they were installed throughout the week.

The morning was spent moving two monuments that honor soldiers from Chilton County who never made it back home. The first was the Chilton County Veterans Memorial monument that hono

articles.title: Column: Everyone needs a skip day
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
One of my favorite movies of all time is “Ferris Bueller’s Day Off,” not only because it’s a great movie, but because I love the concept of a skip day. When I was little, my mom, once a year, would wake me up for school, but then tell me it was a skip day. And I think it was a great parenting method.

She would take me to the Georgia Aquarium, the Atlanta Zoo, a museum or she would have my Dad take me fishing. Now, I want to first off state that I think the best way to learn is to go to school a

articles.title: ‘It’s a loss for the town’: Early morning fire destroys Historic Stiefelmeyer Building
articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
A structural fire has likely destroyed one of the city of Cullman’s most historic locations, according to officials working to contain the fire at the Stiefelmeyer Building on 1st Avenue East.

Chief Darren Peeples with Cullman Fire and Rescue said a smoke alarm from a neighboring business, Margo’s Antiques and Gifts, dispatched units at 3:55 a.m. on Monday, April 13. He said flames were visible from the exterior of the building and thermal imaging showed the fire had already spanned the length

articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
Service and community involvement took center stage as farmers, leaders and students came together for the Farm-City Banquet April 9 in Auburn, recognizing achievements and embracing this year’s theme, “Grow Alabama.”

The theme was a callback to “Agriculture & Forestry Grow Alabama: An Economic Contribution Study” released last year by Alabama Agribusiness Council, Auburn University and Alabama Extension.

articles.title: Wallace State Jazz Band hosting annual Big Band Dance
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
Wallace State Community College will host the annual Big Band Dance on Friday, April 17, at 7 p.m., in the Burrow Center for the Fine and Performing Arts.

The event will feature the Wallace State Jazz Band and the WSCC alumni Jazz Band. More than 20 former Wallace State Jazz Band musicians plan to attend and perform with the group, representing alumni from the late 1970s to more recent graduates from the Class of 2025.

“We are celebrating the 50th anniversary of the Wallace State Jazz Band,” s

articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
Cullman Fire Rescue is responding to a fire at historic Stiefelmeyer building. Due to the high volume of smoke, the Cullman Police Department has closed 2nd Ave. NE, between 1st Street NE and U.S. 278 and are asking the public to avoid the area.

articles.title: April 11, walking tour strolls along First Avenue East
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
The April walking tours continue Saturday, April 11, with a stroll along First Avenue East.

Local historian Robin Sterling discussed the viaducts, the Ratliff family warehouse and the historic Stiefelmeyer building,now lost to a Monday morning fire.

The 2011 Burgermeister, Michael Sullins, who joined the walk, offered insights along the stroll.

Roberta McClellan, Education Coordinator for Traditions Bank, will be the April 18, tour guide. Representatives from the Cullman County Historical Soc

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL: Addison notches fourth straight win with 9-0 shutout of Athens Bible
articles.body:
ADDISON, Ala. –In a matchup of two of the top 1A squads, the sixth-ranked Addison Bulldogs hosted the seventh-ranked Athens Bible Trojans. Addison dominated and came away with a solid 9-0 victory. The teams will play a double header tomorrow at Athens Bible to continue the rivalry.

Addison sent their ace, Kiah Lake, to the mound and he didn’t disappoint. After two outs in the first a Bulldog error followed by Trojan Gavin Butler hit a hot smash down the third base line put runners on second and

articles.title: ‘We lost a piece of our history’: Historic Stiefelmeyer Building burns
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Firefighters worked for hours Monday to contain a blaze at the historic Stiefelmeyer Building in downtown Cullman, with crews remaining on scene as of print time, late evening. Cullman Fire Rescue Chief Darren Peeples said the building was “not salvageable.”

Cullman Fire Rescue crews began responding to the fire around 4 a.m., with law enforcement securing the surrounding area and traffic rerouted through portions of downtown.

articles.title: Cullman EMS ambulance involved in accident
articles.body:
BETHEL, Ala.– A Cullman EMS ambulance was involved in a motor vehicle accident early Monday morning on U.S. Highway 278 West.

The Jones Chapel Volunteer Fire Department was paged to assist at approximately 5:13 a.m. following a crash involving the ambulance and a pickup truck hauling a trailer at the Bethel intersection.

Jones Chapel VFD Chief Clint Thrasher said multiple Cullman EMS units were already on scene upon their arrival.

“Upon our arrival, numerous Cullman EMS ambulances were alread

articles.title: ALEA launches new online marine event permit submissionto streamline service
articles.body:
MONTGOMERY, Ala.– The Alabama Law Enforcement Agency’s (ALEA) Marine Patrol Division is enhancing its service to the public with the launch of a new, streamlined online submission form for marine event permits.

The updated system replaces the previous paper-based process, which required applicants to download, print and scan forms prior to submission. With the new digital platform, individuals and organizations can now complete and submit applications entirely online, saving time, reducing pape

articles.title: Pulling for Survivors tractor pull returns during Alabama Strawberry Festival – April 25
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The annual Pulling for Survivors tractor pull will return Saturday, April 25, at 9 a.m. as part of the Alabama Strawberry Festival. Hosted in partnership with Cullman Fire Rescue, Cullman Parks, Recreation & Sports Tourism and TriGreen Equipment, which will be providing the John Deere tractor, the event will benefit The Willow Path, a United Way partner agency serving as Cullman County’s domestic violence resource center.

The Willow Path (formerly known as Victim Services of Cull

articles.title: Harbin found direction at Wallace State Community College
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– Wallace State Community College Psychology Instructor Casey Harbin said he did not intend to become a teacher. After earning degrees in history and psychology from Auburn University, he planned to take the LSAT to become an attorney. Instead, he found his way into education via coaching.

He started as a substitute teacher while coaching the Cullman High School varsity boys’ soccer team, leading them to the final four in 2010 and 2012 before claiming a state championship in 201

articles.title: Sheriff’s Rodeo back for 30th year
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
The Chambers County Sheriff's Office is preparing for its 30th annual Sheriff’s Rodeo on May 1 and 2 at the Chambers County Agricultural Arena, a longstanding tradition started by former Sheriff Sid Lockhart and continued by Sheriff Jeff Nelson. It is set to begin at 7 p.m. CT/ 8 p.m. ET. The Chambers County Agricultural Arena is located at 1200 AL Hwy. 77, LaFayette.

The rodeo will have plenty of competition and fun, with riders from different regions competing for prizes and pride. The catego

articles.title: Terry seeks a return to Chambers BOE
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
Huguley resident Mary Terry is seeking election to the Chambers County Board of Education for District 4. She is running against the Republican incumbent, Jody Jennings, in the May 19 election. A devoted housewife and grandmother, Terry’s primary focus is on being an advocate for children and parents across her district.

Terry graduated from Chambers Academy. Terry previously served on the Chambers County Board of Education from 2002 to 2018 and since remained an active member of the community.

articles.title: Valley’s McDonald commits to being a Friend of Battle
articles.author: By Chad Martin
articles.body:
Valley senior Bryce McDonald had a big moment on Friday, April 10, as all of his hard work and crazy antics as Valley’s Rambo culminated in him signing with Southern Union as a Friend of Battle in a signing ceremony at Valley High School. Bryce expressed much gratitude to the people that have supported him along the way and an unconventional person for his outlook on life.

“I would like to thank my cheer coach, Mrs. Bunn, for all of her support, and of course my amazing mom who is an absolute s

articles.title: Heroes Golf Tournament returns this Thursday
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
The Troup County First Responder Crisis Fund will host its second annual Heroes Golf Tournament on Thursday, April 16, starting at 10 a.m. at The Field Golf Club located at 257 S. Smith Rd., LaGrange.

Proceeds from the tournament support the Troup County First Responder Crisis Fund, which was created to support first responders and their families in Troup County, LaGrange, Hogansville or West Point. The fund helps first responders in legit emergency crises that take them away from their job, wh

articles.title: County Extension office celebrates Extension Week
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Chambers County Extension is celebrating Extension Week alongside other Extension offices across the state of Alabama this week.

Extension Week is held each week to highlight the programs, services and dedicated staff serving people and communities statewide through Extension. Each Extension office does its own thing to celebrate Extension Week, and this year Chambers County Extension invites the community to an Open House all week to learn what they’re all about.

The Open House will be held t

articles.title: Dozier Affordable Treasures serves up something for everyone
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
Dozier Affordable Treasures has opened its doors in the cafeteria of the old Dozier School, now serving up something for everyone in Dozier and the surrounding area.

“We have 32 acres outside of Brantley where we operate a nursery, but we just wanted a change,” said owner, Debra Howell. She and her husband Darrell moved to Crenshaw County four years ago from Florida.

“This has always been one of my dream bucket list things I’ve wanted to do,” Howell said. “I’ve been looking all over and was lo

articles.title: Navigating the pet predicament
articles.author: Amy Lewis
articles.body:
Dear Amy,

My husband wants to get a dog for our young kids. He keeps talking about how great it would be for them — responsibility, companionship, family memories. I am not anti-dog, but I am very aware of how these things usually go.

The kids will be excited for about two weeks. My husband will help… occasionally. And I will somehow become the primary caretaker of a living creature I didn't ask for.

How do I handle this without being the person who “ruined the dream”?

— Already Walking the

articles.title: Alabama rejects tougher water toxicity standards
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
The Alabama Environmental Management Commission heard public comment earlier this year on proposed updates to the state’s water toxicity standards, drawing differing perspectives from environmental advocates and industry representatives.

The proposed changes, developed by the Alabama Department of Environmental Management, would have revised human health and aquatic life criteria for 12 toxic substances, six with updated cancer potency factors and five with updated oral reference doses. The cha

articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
A public clash betweenPresident Donald TrumpandPope Leo XIVescalated this week, promptingArchbishop Mark S. Rivitusoto join U.S. Catholic leaders in defending the pope following criticism from the president.

Rivituso issued a statement Monday aligning himself withArchbishopPaul S. Coakley, president of theUnited States Conference of Catholic Bishops, after Trump publicly criticized the Holy Father.

“I echo the statement issued today by Archbishop Paul S. Coakley … and join my brother bishops i

articles.title: Gov. Ivey signs the Alabama Post-Election Audit Act into law
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
On Monday, Alabama Governor Kay Ivey signed into law House Bill 95, theAlabama Post-Election Audit Act. The legislation, sponsored by state Representative Joe Lovvorn, R-Auburn, will require the probate judge of each county in Alabama to order a post-election audit after every countywide and statewide general election.

articles.title: Sen. Katie Britt backs bill to close child exploitation loophole
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
U.S. Senator Katie Britt, R-Alabama, highlighted bipartisan legislation on Monday seeking to ensure the prosecution of individuals who produce sexually explicit content featuring children.

The Child Predators Accountability Act, was filed last month by U.S. Senator John Cornyn, R-Texas, with Britt cosponsoring the legislation alongside U.S. Senator Amy Klobuchar, D-Minnesota, and U.S. Senator Chuck Grassley, R-Iowa.

The bill would amend current law to clarify that a child’s passive engagement

articles.title: Rep. Sewell announces winner of Alabama’s 7th District art competition
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
U.S. Representative Terri Sewell, D-Alabama, announced the winner of the 2026 Congressional Art Competition for Alabama’s 7th Congressional District.

Sewell made the announcement Saturday afternoon during a reception at Birmingham’s Ramsay High School celebrating 36 entries submitted by students from across the district. Eden Smith, a ninth-grader at Paul W. Bryant High School in Tuscaloosa, won first place for her submission, “Baptist Church.”

“Baptist Church” by Eden Smith, a ninth-grader at

articles.title: Gov. Ivey announces 14 appointments, including Marion County district judge
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Monday announced 14 appointments to positions across Alabama, including John Hunter Fikes as district judge in the 25th Judicial Circuit in Marion County.

Fikes will fill the vacancy created by the retirement of longtime District Judge Mark Hammitte, which took effect April 1, 2026.

Fikes graduated from the University of Alabama in Tuscaloosa in 2010 and from Samford University’s Cumberland School of Law in 2014. He has served as an attorney and owner of Fikes Law Firm in

articles.title: Target’s car seat trade-in event returns
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Families across Alabama will soon have a chance to save on essential baby gear while supporting a more sustainable future as Target brings back its car seat trade-in event this month.

The biannual program runs from April 19 through May 2 and allows customers to bring any used or outgrown car seat to participating stores in exchange for a 20 percent Target Circle bonus toward new baby items. The initiative is now in its 10th year and has become a regular option for parents looking to reduce the

articles.title: Accessible playground opens at Joe Wheeler State Park
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The Alabama State Parks Division announced Monday that a new accessible playground has opened at Joe Wheeler State Park, creating a new destination for families visiting the park.

State Parks staff and community leaders marked the opening with a ribbon-cutting ceremony Friday, April 10. Officials said the playground is designed for visitors of all abilities.

“This new playground at Joe Wheeler State Park represents more than just a place to play—it’s a place where families can connect, childre

articles.title: Freedom isn’t something everyone wants
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Freedom isn’t comfort. It demands judgment. It demands doubt.

So what happens when people stop carrying that burden?

In this week’s “This Matters,” Bill Britt examines how certainty, loyalty and belonging can quietly replace independent thinking—and what that means for the future of freedom.

Subscribe to “This Matters with Bill Britt” onYouTube,Spotify,Appleor anywhere you get your podcasts.

articles.title: Controversial bills die on verge of passing Legislature

But there were a handful of other bills that were also teed up for final passage and awaiting only one final floor vote to be sent to Gov. Kay Ivey.

“We immediately became decentralized” when the magazines were suspended, Gabrielle Gunter told APR. Gunter was the editor-in-chief ofAliceat the time it was suspended and stepped into the role of editor-in-chief ofSelene.

Books on display in the teen section of the Fairhope Public Library on March 2, 2026. A bill that would have allowed local authorities to fire library board members failed to pass during the legislative session. (Ralph Chapoco/Alabama Reflector)

Alabama State Health Officer Dr. Scott Harris speaking at the State Committee on Public Health meeting on April 9, 2025, in the RSA Regions tower in Montgomery, Alabama. The U.S. Capitol in Washington, D.C., on Monday, April 15, 2024. (Photo by Jennifer Shutt/States Newsroom)

Swalwell’s announcement came just one day after he suspended his campaign for governor over allegations of sexual assault.

President Donald Trump speaks to reporters before boarding Marine One on the South Lawn of the White House in December 2025. (Photo by Tom Brenner/Getty Images)

articles.title: Selma makes a comeback against Keith
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
Selma rallied from an early deficit and used a big second inning to defeat Keith 8-6 on Friday, April 10.

Selma used aggressive base running and timely hitting to erase a 4-1 first inning deficit, highlighted by key contributions from Tay Huntington, Kaleb Wilson and Venetris Eaton throughout the game.

Selma struck first in the top of the first inning when Huntington doubled and later stole home for a 1-0 lead. Ryan Johnson walked and Eaton added a single, but Keith escaped further damage with

articles.title: Valley Grande sets all-day celebration for youth sports opening ceremony
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
VALLEY GRANDE – The Valley Grande City Council announced Monday, April 6, that the city’s Parks and Recreation department will host its annual baseball and softball opening day ceremony on Saturday, April 18. The day-long event will be headlined by a ceremonial first pitch from AHSAA Hall of Famer Earl Griffin.

The festivities are scheduled to begin at 9 a.m. With a processional to ensure the day is personal for every participant, local announcer Mike Irwin has been tapped to call out each play

articles.title: Selma makes deep appearance in tennis sectionals
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
MONTGOMERY – The Selma High tennis program demonstrated significant grit at the AHSAA Class 4-5A, Section 3 tournament, April 9-10, led by a deep postseason run from standout America Salas.

Competing at O’Conner Tennis Center in Montgomery, the Saints faced a challenging field featuring powerhouses like Loveless Academic Magnet Program (LAMP) and Montgomery Catholic. While the Saints saw several hard-fought matches across both the boys’ and girls’ brackets, the tournament was highlighted by ind

articles.title: Troy Public Library hosts April 23 meet-and-greet with new director Stephenie Kelly
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Troy Public Library will be hosting a special meet-and-greet with new Library Director Stephenie Kelly on Thursday, April 23.

Kelly was recently named the new library director and comes to the Troy Public Library with more than a decade of experience as a librarian in the area with Troy City Schools.

Kelly is a graduate of Goshen High School and Troy University, where she earned a degree in accounting. Being a librarian, however, had always been her dream job. It was at Goshen High School

articles.title: City of Troy Queens crowned
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Recently, the Miss Troy Pageant was held and the City of Troy Queens were crowned.

Julianna Knight was crowned Miss Troy, while Everly Lloyd McLendon was crowned Little Miss Troy. Both McLendon and Knight will represent the City of Troy at the National Peanut Festival Pageant in Dothan later this year. McLendon and Knight are also currently working on their community service initiatives.

McLendon’s community service is called Sparkle with Art, in which she is raising awareness on how art can m

articles.title: Blake Carnley’s family keeps his memory alive through giving
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
Since the passing of Blake Carnley, his family has worked to keep his memory alive through various charity and community events. This year will be no different as the family has partnered with Pike County DHR to donate to its adult services program.

Carnley passed away in 2001, at the age of 14, due to an automobile accident. In past years, the family has given to scholarships at Troy University, held teen driving events to educate drivers of all ages of the dangers of distracted driving and he

articles.title: Troy earns conference series win over Texas State
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Troy Trojans earned a 3-2 Sun Belt Conference series win over the Texas State Bobcats this weekend, sweeping the final two games.

The series win moves Troy into a five-way tie for third place in the conference along with Southern Miss, South Alabama, ULM and Marshall.

In game one on April 10, Texas State took an early series lead with an 8-5 win. Troy actually led 5-4 going into the bottom of the ninth when the Bobcats tied things up on an RBI single from Jackson Cotton. Then, Dawson Park

articles.title: Statewide Fire Alert issued
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
On Monday, Alabama State Forester Rick Oates issued a statewide Fire Alert effective immediately after worsening drought conditions across Alabama.

Over the past weekend, 50 wildfires burned more than 2,300 acres of forestland across Alabama, according to a statement from Oates. These fires included a 500-acre wildfire in Mobile County, a 412-acre wildfire in Close County, a 328-acre fire in Choctaw County, a 260-acre fire in Washington County and a 105-acre fire in Escambia County. In total, 3

articles.title: Beulah Cemetery hosting inaugural Guided Veterans Tour April 25
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Beulah Cemetery Committee will be hosting its inaugural Guided Veterans Tour at Historic Beulah Cemetery in Troy on April 25.

Last October, the Beulah Cemetery Committee hosted its first ever guided tour during Halloween. Beulah Cemetery Committee President David Dewberry said the Halloween History Tour was a big success and many of the visitors asked for a daytime tour centered on the numerous military veterans buried in Troy’s oldest cemetery.

Dewberry said that the tour would feature hi

articles.title: CRAWFISH IS OFF THE TRAX
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Off the Trax hosts Crawfish Cookoff on March 28

Off the Trax Bar and Grill held a crawfish boiling competition on Saturday, March 28, as a way to provide fun for the community as well as strengthen community involvement.

Owner Seth Stewart was pleased with the turnout and looks forward to growing this event in the future. “One of my food vendors has been participating in this event for the last three or four years and they asked if we were interested in hosting one here because they liked the

articles.title: Guardian Angel Christian Academy athletes bring home State Championships
articles.author: Sarah Kate Rhodes
articles.body:
Guardian Angel Christian Academy sent 2 athletes to the Alabama Independent School Association (AISA) State Track & Field Championship where the duo secured numerous state championships in GACA Athletic Department’s inaugural year.

GACA began their athletic program in the 2025-2026 school year, and the school can be proud of Chapman and Campbell Bowers performances at the AISA Championship. The pair competed in many events, and both placed on the podium in multiple races.

Chapman competed in t

articles.title: Opp City Council extends support of Mizell
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Clerk not named; board members appointed; chamber use agreement denied

The Opp City Council held a meeting on April 6.

To begin the meeting, the council approved the appointment of the following individuals to the Planning and Development board: Bill Stocke was approved as chairman; Buddy Pyron approved as Vice Chairman; and Belinda Dunn was approved as Secretary/Treasurer. The members of the Planning and Development Board include Danny Bess, Paul Norris, Ricky Boothe, John Bartholomew, Debbie

articles.title: APL to celebrate alongside Archibald
articles.author: Sarah Kate Rhodes
articles.body:
Andalusia Public Library is going to Celebrate the Love of Libraries this month with special guest John Archibald.

The month of April is National Library Month and the week of April 19 through 25 is 2026’s National Library week, so the team at APL decided that was the perfect time to invite the renowned Archibald to speak at their library celebration. National Library Week was originally established in the late 1950s to encourage readers to support their local libraries during a period of decli

articles.title: Trooper Barton honored by ALEA for heroic actions
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Twelve individuals were honored Thursday, March 26, during the Secretary of Alabama Law Enforcement’s Life-Saving Awards Ceremony at Headquarters in Montgomery, recognizing extraordinary actions taken to protect and preserve life across the state.

The recipients, representing multiple divisions within the Alabama Law Enforcement Agency (ALEA), were recognized for going above and beyond the call of duty in critical moments, delivering lifesaving aid, preventing tragedy and demonstrating the Agen

articles.title: Andalusia Ballet receives grant
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Andalusia Ballet Association has received a $4700 Creative Places Arts Facilities grant from Alabama State Council on the Arts to support the purchase of a new cyclorama backdrop and a new Nutcracker Kingdom of the Sweets backdrop for use in Andalusia Ballet’s performances.

The Creative Places Arts Facilities program strengthens Alabama communities by providing grants for the construction, renovation, restoration, or enhancement of facilities where the public can participate in arts programming

articles.title: Bulldog baseball seeks to host first round playoffs
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
After defeating Dothan on April 7 and losing to Geneva on April 10, the Andalusia varsity baseball team has reached the final stretch before playoffs. Their Tuesday, April 14 game against Charles Henderson determines their initial playoff placement.

April 7’s home game against Dothan ended in a 7-6 victory for the Bulldogs. Closely matched by Dothan’s Wolves, the Bulldogs spent much of the game trailing behind Dothan before making a sudden comeback in the seventh inning. Inning one saw each tea

articles.title: Pleasant Home softball wins home invitational
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
The Pleasant Home Eagles hosted their softball invitational on Saturday April 11. A three-team jamboree was held with Pleasant Home, McKenzie, and Highland Home competing.

The Eagles defeated McKenzie in the first game 7-3 and went on to defeat Highland Home 5-4 in the second. The Eagles are currently 6-8 on the varsity season.

In game one against McKenzie, Aubree Moseley went 2 for 3 with 1 run batted in. Scarlett Smith batted 3 for 3, and Bailey Parker went 1 for 2 with 1 run batted in. Harl

articles.title: Straughn Bass Fishing’s Copeland lands a big tournament win
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
On Saturday, April 11, the Straughn Fishing Team set out for their last tournament of the East Alabama High School Bass Trail on Lake Eufaula. A cool morning greeted the anglers as 48 boats idled in anticipation of the blast off. Four of the six Straughn Fishing teams set out on a mission to take on Lake Eufaula in hopes of bringing in strong catches.

After a long and challenging day on the water, only two of the four teams were able to weigh in fish. Despite tough conditions, the anglers demon

articles.title: Johnson brothers inducted into Red Level School’s Hall of Fame
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
The Red Level High School Class of 1971 is being honored in a special way, as brothers McKinley “Ken” Johnson and Ray Johnson—both standout student-athletes—have been inducted into the school’s Hall of Fame.

After the closing of Bethune Jr. High School, where they were part of the “Yellowjackets,” the brothers moved to Woodson High School during the 1969-1970 school year following full integration. When Woodson later closed, they entered another new chapter at Red Level High School, where they

articles.title: Covington County Schools accepts retirements; Driver’s set for June 30
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
The Covington County Board of Education addressed a range of personnel, facilities, and operational matters during its April 7 meeting.

The board began by approving the meeting agenda and minutes from its March 3 session before recognizing the Florala High School boys’ varsity basketball team for advancing to the Sweet 16 under head coach Monty Russell.

In personnel actions, the board approved a leave of absence for Patsy K. Carroll, a custodian at Pleasant Home School, under the Family and Me

articles.title: Andalusia High School releases May schedule
articles.author: Sarah Kate Rhodes
articles.body:
Andalusia High School has released its schedule for the upcoming graduation/finals season.

On April 28, AHS will be hosting an academic banquet, and on April 29, seniors will get their cap and gowns for graduation. April 30 will be an All Sports Signing Day for any student athletes signing with a college.

Friday, May 1 will be an AHS pride day to kickoff the month. On Monday, April 4, seniors will walk through Andalusia Elementary School in the morning and have their Senior Class Night that ev

articles.title: Andalusia Junior High School welcomes Benjamin as new Band Director
articles.author: Sarah Kate Rhodes
articles.body:
The Andalusia City School’s Board of Education called a special meeting earlier this month to address personnel changes.

Andalusia Junior High School’s band director Sam Nolen, turned in his resignation. Although Nolen started at AJHS in June of 2025, his resignation will take effect on May 25, 2026, not even a full year after being named band director. ACS is proud to welcome JaCorey Benjamin who is stepping into Nolen’s role as of August 3, 2026. Benjamin previously served Troy University’s S

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
David Corbell

David Corbell, age 66, passed away on April 8, 2026. He was born on March 7, 1960, in Aberdeen, Mississippi to Billy Joe Corbell and Lalia Lavelle Walker.

Memorial services will be held at 2 p.m. on Wednesday, April 15, 2026, at Keahey Funeral Home Chapel. Keith Allen will be officiating the service.

David proudly served his country in the National Guard. He enjoyed spending his time playing golf, poker, visiting the casino, and hunting. He will be remembered for the joy he foun

articles.title: Letter to the Editor: Community helps family through tragedy
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
To the Editor,

We would like to say thank you to everyone that has helped our family during a difficult time of the passing of our daughter Rebecca S. Noon. Many people may say that they dislike small town life, but we can tell you from everything we have experienced and witnessed over the last two weeks. We would choose no other place to call home. These people and businesses went above and beyond, helping us navigate these uncharted waters, and we will never be able to repay the kindness and

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Email community announcements tonewscouriersoundoff@gmail.com.

Earthworms flyer April 2026

Limestone County Historical Society to meet Sunday, April 19

Local student Ava Slinkard will share her interest in WWII with the Historical Society the afternoon of April 19 at the Rodgers Center of First Presbyterian Church on the square in Athens. A French Resistance Historical Reenactor, Ava will present her research into the service of five Limestone County WWII soldiers. Especially interested in ed

articles.title: Athens-Limestone Hospital seeks feedback via Community Needs Assessment survey
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Athens-Limestone Hospital is conducting a Community Needs Assessment, and any community member with an internet connection is invited — and encouraged — to take part.

The short survey is anonymous and open to everyone — including area residents, hospital patients, patients’ families, hospital employees, and hospital visitors.

The feedback that Athens-Limestone Hospital receives from the survey, said organizers, “will play a critical role in shaping how we improve and expand healthcare services

articles.title: PREP ROUNDUP: Elkmont rallies for 12-11 win over Whitesburg Christian
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Elkmont scored six runs in the seventh inning to rally past Whitesburg Christian, 12-11, on Tuesday night.

The Red Devils trailed 11-10 with two outs before an error on a Bentley Mitchell grounder allowed the home team to plate two runs and score the walk-off victory.

Jeffery Barnes (single) and Layton Schrimsher (walk) provided RBIs in the decisive frame. Elkmont also scored two additional runs off another error by Whitesburg Christian.

The Red Devils improved to 21-7 following the triumph.

articles.title: Alabama Forestry Commission issues statewide Fire Alert
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
With worsening drought conditions across the state and low relative humidity, Alabama State Forester Rick Oates issued a statewide Fire Alert on Monday, April 13. The new alert takes effect immediately and is set to expire no earlier than April 20.

Over the past weekend, 50 wildfires had burned more than 2,300 acres of forestland across Alabama. The burned acreage includes a 500-acre wildfire in Mobile County, one for 412 acres in Coosa County, one for 382 acres in Choctaw County, one for 260 a

articles.title: Relay for Life: Notable locals wait tables for a cause on April 16 in Athens
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Ahead of next month’s Relay for Life on May 8, locally famous volunteer waiters are set to dish up some early support in the fight against cancer inan event that’s become an annual Athens tradition.

On Thursday, April 16, local celebrities — including a cancer survivor and also a caregiver — will switch into their best service mode at Applebee’s in Athens, serving as celebrity waiters in a bid to raise tip funds that support the American Cancer Society (ACS).

“The night is about raising money

articles.title: Ribbon cutting held to show improvements at Little River State Forest, Claude D. Kelley Recreational Area Tuesday
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Soon, children and adults alike will be enjoying the Little River State Forest and Claude D. Kelley Recreational Area.

Escambia County, Monroe County and state dignitaries, along with passionate park goers were on hand Tuesday for a ribbon cutting at the state park to unveil improvements at the historic state forest and recreational area after several years of closure.

Alabama Assistant State Forester Will Brantley said this is an exciting time for the park, adding that it had been a while sin

articles.title: Randy L. Martin
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Randy Lee Martin, 60, of Canoe, passed away on April 11, 2026.

He was born on December 27, 1965, to Bernard Martin and Nettie Pearl Moye Martin.

Randy is preceded in death by his parents; and his grandchildren, Andromada and Harlyn.

He is survived by his loving spouse, Kimberly Bailey Martin; his children, Megan Martin, Rebecca (David) Merrill, Melissa (Robby) Pitman, Holly (Kevin) Dunn, Kyle (Bridget) Gunn and Jeremy Gunn; his brother, Andy Martin; six grandchildren, Annberly, Kaylyn, Linley

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Charles William “Pete” McGhee, 76, of Mobile, passed away on April 8, 2026.

He was born July 8, 1949, to Cobie McGhee and Mattie Lou Phillips McGhee.

Pete was preceded in death by his parents; and his sisters, Lizette Wiggins, Betty McGhee and Ellen Faircloth.

He is survived by his brothers, Mickey (Joyce) McGhee, Paul (Beverly) McGhee and Bobby McGhee; his sisters, Polly Rayborn and Jeannie (Jimmy) Holmes; his brother in law, Curtis Wiggins; and many nieces, nephews and extended family.

Fun

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
James (Jimmie) Paynes, 90, of Brewton, passed away on April 9, 2026 at West Florida Hospital in Pensacola, fla.

A funeral service will be held on Sat., April 18, 2026 at 2:30 p.m. at Baptist Hill Church. Burial will follow at Baptist Hill Cemetery with Christian Memorial Funeral Home of Atmore directing.

articles.title: Devin Backus
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Devin Backus, 29, of Atmore, passed away on March 31, 2026 at USA Medical Center.

A public viewing will be held Fri., April 17, 2026 from 3 p.m. until 6 p.m. at the funeral home chapel.

A funeral service will be held on Sat., April 18, 2026, at 11 a.m. at Spirit and True Tabernacle Church in Flomaton.

Burial will be held in Fairview Cemetery in Atmore with Christian Memorial Funeral Home of Atmore directing.

articles.title: ACH awarded $50,000 from GP's Brewton Containerboard mill
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Atmore Community Hospital was awarded $50,000 from Georgia-Pacific’s Brewton Containerboard mill for some much-needed financial support, according to a release.

According to the Alabama Hospital Association, 84 percent of rural hospitals in the state are in the red.

The Brewton mill also donated $225,000 to D.W. McMillan Hospital in Brewton. Additionally, another facility, Alabama River Cellulose, donated $225,000 to Monroe County Hospital. The $500,000 donation was a part of a corporate donat

articles.title: Birmingham City Council Approves New Medical Waste Facility for Kingston Neighborhood
articles.body:
A new non-hazardous medical waste facility is coming to Birmingham’s Kingston neighborhood after receiving approval from the city council Tuesday.

The facility will treat non-hazardous waste — including PPE, pharmaceuticals and single-use medical devices — using eco-friendly technology. City leaders said the project will transform a vacant lot into a resource that gives back to the community.

There are more than a dozen hospitals in Birmingham but currently no local option for processing non-h

articles.title: Jefferson County Probate Court to Host First Senior Summit for Older Citizens, Caregivers
articles.body:
Special to The Times

On Tuesday, April 21, the Jefferson County Probate Court will host its inaugural Senior Summit. The event, which will be held from 10 am to 2 pm, is designed to provide valuable resources and services to senior citizens throughout Jefferson County.

Attendees will hear from Presiding Judge Yashiba Blanchard and representatives from the Birmingham Fire Department, Elections Divisions, United Way of Central Alabama, Jefferson County Health Department and more.

“Events like t

articles.title: Meet Geno Gardner: The Servant Leader Who Teaches Birmingham Financial Growth
articles.body:
By Jordyn Davis |The Birmingham Times

Geno Gardner isn’t your typical Community Manager at JPMorganChase. While he clocks in every weekday, his focus extends far beyond personal or corporate gain. Gardner builds trust and teaches financial education in underserved communities to show up for Birmingham. And while most workforce traits are developed within a company, Gardner got his start at an early age when he enlisted in the Army.

Gardner attended Proviso West High School in Illinois, and whi

articles.title: GP makes healthy hospital donations
articles.body:
Most rural hospitals in Alabama are operating at a loss as costs continue to outpace payments. According to the Alabama Hospital Association, 84 percent of rural hospitals in the state are in the red.

To provide some much needed financial support, Georgia-Pacific’s Brewton Containerboard mill donated $225,000 to D.W. McMillan Hospital in Brewton, and $50,000 to Atmore Community Hospital in Atmore. Another facility, Alabama River Cellulose, donated $225,000 to Monroe County Hospital, all part of

articles.title: Foresters issue statewide fire alert
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
With worsening drought conditions across the state and low relative humidity, State Forester Rick Oates has issued a statewide Fire Alert effective immediately. Over the past weekend, 50 wildfires burned more than 2,300 acres of forestland across the state. This includes a 500-acre wildfire in Mobile County, and another one for 105 acres in Escambia County. In the last 30 days, 387 wildfires have burned approximately 15,850 acres of forestland across the state.

The Central Alabama Arts Alliance is proud to spotlight wood artisan Jake Jones as the April 2026 Artist Spotlight, celebrating a craftsman whose work transforms raw wood into living, breathing art.

For Jones, the journey into wood art didn't begin with power tools, but with pen and paper.

articles.title: Maplesville posts nine podium finishes at 4H Round-up
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
Maplesville High School had nine top-three finishes at the 4H Round-up, including three first-place finishes.

“Congratulations to the MHS students who competed and placed at the 4H Round-up,” said a statement on Maplesville High School’s Facebook.

Aubrey Wallace took home first place in the Creative Endeavors category. She also won second place in the Freestyle showcase, bringing home two separate certificates.

Wallace was not the only Red Devil to bring home multiple pieces of hardware, as A

articles.title: Three arrested in Clanton business shooting, two suspects released
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Three suspects were arrested following a shots fired incident at Kumi Manufacturing Alabama (KMA) on Seventh Street South in Clanton on April 10.

Clanton Police responded to the call of shots fired at 3:10 p.m. and arrived at 3:15 p.m. to find that both parties involved in the shooting had left the scene. Patrol officers made contact with both parties a minute later near Exit 205 on Interstate 65, where they were subsequently arrested.

Lashundra Howard, 24 years old of Marbury, Keyontay Jones,

articles.title: Strawberry Festival hits half-decade in Thorsby on April 18
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
From Whitney Barlow | Director of Chilton County Strawberry Festival

The Chilton County Strawberry Festival is returning to Thorsby on April 18, 2026, bringing a full day of family-friendly fun, local flavor, and community celebration.

Held at Richard Wood Park, this annual event highlights the best of Chilton County — especially its famous strawberries — while supporting local farmers, small businesses, and artisans. With thousands of attendees expected, the festival continues to grow as one

articles.title: PREP ROUNDUP: Cullman earns area sweep of Hartselle
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Cullman’s varsity soccer squads swept Hartselle in Class 6A, Area 13 action on Tuesday night.

The boys clinched a second straight area title under coach Will Drake following a 2-1 road win that featured goals from Carlos Medina and JD Ellerbee.

The girls, meanwhile, picked up a 3-1 home victory thanks to goals from Ruby Martinez, Maddy Henry and Lily Lambert. Henry also added two assists for the Bearcats, who can secure an area title of their own with a win over Mortimer Jordan on Thursday.

“

articles.title: ‘FOREVER GRATEFUL’: Cold Springs’ Dickerson caps resilient career as Class 2A Player of the Year
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Ella Dickerson’s surprise quickly gave way to immense appreciation.

Appreciation for a family that stood by her side every step of her career.

Appreciation for the Cold Springs community, whose support never waned.

Appreciation for teammates who pushed her to be the best she could be.

So when the Alabama Sports Writers Association named Dickerson the Class 2A Girls Player of the Year on Thursday — she beat out North Sand Mountain’s Kayden Reyes and Pisgah’s Campbell Barron for the prestigiou

articles.title: 42nd Annual Bloomin’ Festival set for April 18-19
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
Arts and crafts will be on display for two days in April when St. Bernard Abbey and Prep School hosts its annual Bloomin’ Festival. Set for April 18-19, 2026, the outdoor event, now in its 42nd year, features the handcrafted works of talented artists and craftsmen, music and festival food and is expected to draw more than 25,000 visitors in search of fresh air, unique arts and crafts, demonstrations and family-friendly entertainment in a picturesque setting.

During Bloomin’ Festival, more than

articles.title: Bremen man sentenced to 325 years
articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
James Michael Doss, 67, from Bremen, was sentenced to 325 consecutive years in prison for nearly a dozen crimes against children after signing a blind guilty plea agreement in February.

Doss was arrested in 2020 after multiple victims came forward with sexual abuse allegations. Investigators with the Cullman County Sheriff’s Office at the time determined that the crimes had been committed over the course of many years.

Cullman County District Attorney Champ Crocker thanked those same victims d

articles.title: Local attorney offers solution to Good Hope, Dodge City property dispute
articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
Attorney Andy Herron, representing local business owner Scotty Hooper, offered to work toward a solution to rectify what he described as a “roadblock” in a disputed property annexation between the city of Good Hope and the town of Dodge City during the latter’s council meeting on Thursday, April 9.

The dispute centers around roughly 45 acres belonging to Hooper, which was annexed into Good Hope in the summer of 2025. Herron told the Dodge City Council on Thursday that his client had requested t

articles.title: For more than a century, area landmark was a long-standing part of Cullman history
articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
The iconic Stiefelmeyer Building located on 1st Ave. in Cullman’s Warehouse District was constructed in 1892 after a fire, which started in the boiler room of the Dreher Furniture Store, consumed the entire city block.

articles.title: West Point’s Alaina Riggs ‘Best of Show’ in 2026 4th Congressional District Art Competition
articles.body:

articles.title: Obituary: Leo Madonna Swann
articles.body:
Leo Madonna Swann, age 91, was born to Clayton and Clarycie Williams on August 7, 1934. She married Mitchell R. Swann on Oct. 31, 1953. She was a member at Harmony Baptist Church all her life!

To know Leo was to love her! She was known for her quick wit, her loving and forgiving nature, and her resilient spirit. She loved the Lord with all her heart and drew strength from His Word her whole life. She persevered through life with amazing grace and lived the best she could one day at a time. She

articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The Uniform Initiative, next to Monograms Plus, was created to serve the area’s growing medical community and anyone looking for stylish, practical workwear. Stephanie King, who worked in health care for over 30 years, said she opened the store because she wanted a new challenge but still wanted to help meet the needs she saw during her career.

“I knew there was a need in the community, and I just started exploring options and just dove in headfirst,” King said.

articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Adoration Home Health offers in-home health care services help patients stay independent and makes the move from hospital to home easier by offering caring, high-quality support in a familiar setting.

The company chose Cullman for its new location because the area is growing and more people need home-based health care.

The team provides skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy and speech therapy. These services help patients recover, manage long-term health issues

articles.title: 30th LaFayette Day celebrated in style
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
The weather was great for the 30th and final LaFayette Day for Valley Haven School. There was a steady turnout throughout the day on Saturday, and an estimated $10,000 was raised for the Hike/Bike/Run.

“It wasn’t the best LaFayette Day we’ve ever had, but it was a good one, and we want to thank everyone who took part in the motorcycle ride, the car show, all our vendors, those who performed on stage and everyone who came out to take part in it,” said Valley Haven Executive Director Craig Brown.

articles.title: Springwood track and field sees four medal at state
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Springwood had a strong showing at last week’s AISA State Track & Field meet at Mickey Miller Blackwell Stadium in Gulf Shores.

At the end of the meet, Springwood had several state medalists, who placed in the top three in the state in their respective events.

Senior Kori Key placed second in a tight race for the 400m dash with a time of 54.98. Senior Clarence Moore placed second in the 800m run with a time of 2:12.35. Senior Madison Robinson finished strong, medaling in two events. She placed

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Kristy McCormally: I Wish We Still Taught Cursive. And How to Host.
articles.author: Kristy McCormally
articles.body:
Somewhere in the last thirty years, we made a decision about what mattered enough to teach.

Cursive didn't make the cut. Neither did hosting. And here we are.

I think about this more than I probably should — two skills that look like they're about penmanship and centerpieces, but are actually about something else entirely. They're about the discipline of showing up for other people. About making something by hand and presenting it as a gift. About saying, with your time and your effort and you

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Michael Brooks: Lord, Where Have You Been?
articles.author: Michael J. Brooks
articles.body: I’m unsure why her statement never struck me before as it did in preparing for Easter. I used the final of the seven “I Am” statements of Jesus in John’s gospel. John especially wanted us to understand the nature of Christ. At Lazarus’ graveside, Jesus said, “I am the resurrection and the life. The one who lives and believes in me will never die.” This seems a perplexing word to speak in a graveyard with a corpse nearby. But the mourners in Bethany were about to witness one of Jesus’ most well-k

THS clubs, organizations and sports teams were invited to select candidates who exhibit high academic standards, leadership and community involvement. Preliminary judging consisted of contestants preparing an application to be scored by off-site adjudicators based on their school and community service, GPA and extracurricular

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body: Emma Sankey and Olidia Arriaga of Luverne, were both awarded a scholarship by the Poarch Band of Creek Indians (PBCI) for their participation in Alabama’s livestock industry on Sunday, March 22 at a ceremony held in coordination with the Southeastern Livestock Exposition (SLE) Rodeo. The PBCI has a proud history of supporting youth in livestock. Each year, young people from each of Alabama’s 67 counties are awarded scholarships from the Tribe to aid in future participation in youth livestock eve

articles.title: Getting your way... the easier way
articles.author: Andy and Renie Bowman
articles.body:
You want your way, and that is normal. But getting others to want to do what you are asking sometimes just is not very easy. Forcing them usually doesn’t make you a winner in their eyes or in your own. A normal person will usually admit they don’t enjoy having to use bad tactics to win. They just can’t figure out another technique. And they are right; there are other means to get your way.

For example, you may be bigger and stronger than a child in your care and can physically force them to com

articles.title: Opinion | The ALGOP battle to do nothing for you
articles.author: Josh Moon
articles.body:
If there is one thing the 2026 Alabama legislative session taught us, it is this: The Alabama Republican Party has gerrymandered itself into a nightmare.

Throughout this past session—a session that was supposed to be relatively quiet and mundane as lawmakers prepared for elections—there was almost constant fighting. And all of it was of the intra-party variety.

Oh, sure, there were a handful of R vs. D dustups, but nothing particularly nasty or noteworthy. Instead, the real fireworks came from

articles.title: Governor signs act ensuring athletic eligibility for CHOOSE Act students
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
Alabama Governor Kay Ivey signed a bill into law on Tuesday to help ensure students who receive CHOOSE Act funds may participate in school athletics programs.

The Let Kids Play Act, sponsored by Senator Clyde Chambliss, R-Prattville, was proposed by the governor following a lawsuit that Ivey and House Speaker Nathaniel Ledbetter brought last year against the Alabama High School Athletic Association. The suit challenged regulations barring CHOOSE Act students from participating in athletics for

articles.title: Consumer Reports urges Ivey to veto Alabama Personal Data Protection Act
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
Consumer Reports—the independent, nonprofit member organization focused on marketplace transparency—is urging Alabama Governor Kay Ivey to vetoHouse Bill 351, the Alabama Personal Data Protection Act, arguing that the legislation does too little to protect consumers.

“Governor Ivey should veto HB351 and ask the legislature to significantly strengthen it,” said Matt Schwartz, policy analyst at Consumer Reports, in a recent written statement. “This legislation adopts a lowest-common-denominator a

articles.title: UAB Medicine expands care with new clinics in first quarter of 2026
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
UAB Medicine said this week it is expanding specialty care in the Birmingham area with several new and enhanced clinic locations opening in early 2026.

Thehealth systemsaid the expansion is aimed at improving access to specialty care across central Alabama.

UAB Medicine opened new specialty clinic space at UABSt. Vincent’s East, where patient care began January 12. The site offers gastroenterology and urology services for patients in eastern Birmingham.

articles.title: Gov. Ivey signs Alabama Work Zone Safety Act
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Tuesday signed Senate Bill 341, the Alabama Work Zone Safety Act, just ahead of National Work Zone Awareness Week.

The law, sponsored by Senator Josh Carnley and Representative Mike Kirkland, creates a pilot program authorizing the procurement, installation and operation of a photographic speed enforcement system in interstate highway work zones.

“Currently in Alabama, there is roadwork ongoing in all 67 counties, including on our major roadways. It is just critical that w

articles.title: ALDOT, ALEA remind drivers to stay alert while driving in work zones
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The Alabama Department of Transportation, or ALDOT, the Alabama Law Enforcement Agency, or ALEA, and states across the country are urging motorists to observeNational Work Zone Awareness Weekfrom April 20-24 by slowing down in work zones and watching for roadway workers.

This year’s theme is “Safe Actions Save Lives.”

“The number of crash fatalities in work zones in Alabama continues to decrease year after year, and the 2025 numbers are the lowest it has been in a decade,” ALDOT Drive Safe Ala

articles.title: Sen. Britt’s Stop the Scroll Act passes through Senate committee
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
U.S. Senators Katie Britt, R-Alabama, and John Fetterman’s, D-Pennsylvania, legislation, theStop the Scroll Act, which would create a mental health warning label requirement for users under the age of 18 on social media platforms, passed the Senate Commerce Committee on Tuesday. This bipartisan legislation would also ensure minors are aware of the potential mental health risks posed by social media usage and are provided access to mental health resources.

articles.author: Staff
articles.body:

articles.title: New NFIB survey shows small business optimism fell in March
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index fell 3.0 points in March to 95.8, below its 52-year average of 98.0. The last time the index was below its historical average was April 2025.

The NFIB Uncertainty Index rose 4 points from February to 92, well above its historical average of 68.

articles.title: Anchored in Truth | Not defined by yesterday
articles.author: The Observer Staff
articles.body:
OPINION —

Philippians 3:13 — “Forgetting those things which are behind and reaching forward to those things which are ahead…”

There are some things in life we wish we could forget.

Words we said.

Choices we made.

Moments we wish we could take back.

Even after we’ve grown, matured and even changed… those memories have a way of lingering around and reminding us of past failures. And with those memories, often comes a quiet voice that says, “That’s who you really are.”

That voice, is called

articles.title: Altar Call | Standing on the promises of God
articles.author: The Observer Staff
articles.body:
BY MATT ALBRITTON

OPINION —

How many times have you joined others in singing the great hymn, “Standing on the Promises?” How many times have you been comforted by its words? My favorite verse is verse two.

Standing on the promises that cannot fail, when the howling storms of doubt and fear assail, by the living Word of God I shall prevail, standing on the promises of God.

This verse always reminds me of the storms of life. Here’s the thing about storms: You have just gone through one, you ar

articles.title: Kids Talk About God | How can Christians be unified?
articles.author: The Observer Staff
articles.body:
OPINION —

“If Christians were on a basketball team, they’d have to pass the ball instead of hog it,” said Ethan, age 10. “Jesus would probably be the coach and the MVP.”

That’s a great way to start thinking about unity. Jesus prayed in John 17 that His followers would be “one” just like he and the Father are one. That’s a pretty big request, especially when we sometimes struggle just to share the remote.

“I do not pray for these alone, but also for those who will believe in me through their w

Abrams, a lifelong Opelika native, was recognized by the Lee-Russell Council of Governments as part of its centenarian program, which highlights individuals who have reached the milestone age of 100. articles.title: Sean of the South | A dog and his burger
articles.author: The Observer Staff
articles.body:
OPINION —

“Otis!” says the girl working the fast-food drive-thru window. “Gimme a kiss!”

I am in my truck, buying hamburgers. The drive-up cashier’s name is Shawnda. She is a notorious Otis lover.

Otis (alleged Labrador) crawls over the steering wheel to greet Shawnda, and his prodigious canine butt is wagging in my face. His tail is swinging like a Louisville Slugger. I think I’m going to have a black eye.

Shawnda scruffs his hair. “Can I give him some French fries?”

“Why not. He’s a growi

articles.title: Understanding Online Loans: What Borrowers Should Know in 2026
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
Online loans are digital lending products that allow borrowers to access funds through web- or mobile-based platforms. They have grown in popularity due to the speed, convenience, and accessibility they offer compared to traditional lending. Borrowers can apply anytime using a phone or computer, receive decisions quickly, and in some cases access funds the same day. This ease of use aligns with modern expectations for fast, on-demand financial services.

In 2026, online loans continue to evolve,

Selma City Schools is gearing up for a summer of growth and exploration with the launch of two major student initiatives: the Enhanced Learning Program and the Summer Enrichment Camp.

Selma Mayor Johnny Moss III has designated Chief Michael Perry to serve as interim police chief of the Selma Police Department, effective immediately.

This designation is a temporary administrative measure to ensure continuity of leadership and the uninterrupted operation of the Selma Police Department during an ongoing personnel process.

Ivey on Monday signed into law House Bill 95, the Alabama Post-Election Audit Act, a measure designed to strengthen voter confidence and ensure the accuracy and reliability of Alabama’s election processes.

The funding through the Site Evaluation and Economic Development Strategy (SEEDS) program will be used to acquire about 70 acres within the Barton Riverfront Industrial Park in Colbert County, creating a 131-acre contiguous site.

articles.title: John Roberts op-ed: Following Senator Katie Britt’s lead making Alabama the most family-friendly state in America
articles.body:
Tax Day is not something most families look forward to. But this year, it gave me a reason to reflect.

My wife and I are raising two young boys. Like so many parents, our days are full of balancing work, family, and the responsibility of building a good life for our kids. As we sat down to file our taxes this year, we saw something different. For the first time in a while, we saw policy that actually felt like it was built with families like ours in mind.

Opening Day. Shea Stadium. April 6th.

articles.title: Mama, Bluegrass and Ol’ Rex
articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
Mama loved country music.

One of my favorite memories of Mama is waking to the sound of her voice singing right along with the country music playing on the radio.

Daddy said it sounded like caterwauling.

To me, it was the prettiest sound in the world.

So, many years ago, as I sat of the running board of a pickup truck at my first Henderson Bluegrass Festival, it was like Mama’s voice coming from the kitchen early in the morning.

It was rather chilly that night so I went inside the old Hende

articles.body:
This will be a great political year, with some fantastic, interesting, and close contests. However, there will be only one hotly contested open congressional seat this year in the Heart of Dixie. The other six incumbent congressmen will coast to reelection, either unopposed or with token opposition.

articles.title: Wallace State Men Win ACCC Tennis Championship; Gautier Named Player of the Year
articles.body:
MILLBROOK –Wallace State won the ACCC Men’s Tennis Tournament championship at 17 Springs in Millbrook, earning the conference title and advancing to the NJCAA National Tournament in Plano, Texas, scheduled for May 11-15.

In singles play, Wallace State won titles at No. 1, No. 5 and No. 6 singles.

At No. 1 singles, Angel Gautier defeated Coastal Alabama South’s representative 6-1, 6-2. Luka Uzelac won the No. 5 singles championship with a 7-5, 6-4 victory over Guyot. Tom Köpf captured the No. 6

articles.title: Obituary: Betty Jean Wood
articles.body: Betty Jean Wood, 91, of Cullman, Alabama, passed away peacefully at Woodland Village Nursing and Rehab on April 15, 2026, with her family by her side.Betty Jean was born August 25, 1934, in Maxton, North Carolina, to the late Luther Thomas “Red” and Lena Walters English. The youngest of 3 siblings, she was raised in Maxton, NC where she graduated from Maxton High School. She lettered as a varsity cheerleader and was crowned Miss Maxton. Once graduated she attended Presbyterian Junior College in

articles.title: Wallace State Women Capture ACCC Tennis Championship; Standifer Earns Player of the Year Honors
articles.body:
MILLBROOK –Wallace State won the ACCC Women’s Tennis Tournament championship at 17 Springs in Millbrook, finishing with victories in five singles finals and all three doubles finals against Coastal Alabama South. With the win, the Lady Lions earned a trip to the NJCAA National Tournament in Sumter, South Carolina, scheduled for May 2-6.

In singles play, Wallace State claimed titles at No. 1 through No. 5 singles.

At No. 1 singles, Madison Standifer defeated Walter of Coastal Alabama South 6-4,

articles.title: Obituary: Frankie Lee Jones
articles.body:
Frankie Lee Jones, 78, of Cullman, Alabama, passed away on April 13, 2026. Born May 30, 1947, in Alabama, Frankie lived a life marked by steady devotion, diligent work, and a generous willingness to serve others—qualities that shaped his family, his career, and his community.

Frankie’s sense of duty and perseverance were evident across decades of service. He dedicated 22 years to the United States Air Force, rising to the rank of Senior Master Sergeant through hard work and steadfast commitment

articles.body:
Sebrenia D. Waldrep, age 64, of Vinemont, passed away on Wednesday, April 15, 2026, at her home. She was born on September 25, 1961, in West Point, Mississippi, to James and Joan Sansing Anderson.

Sebrenia was a devoted member of the Kingdom Hall of Jehovah’s Witnesses, Cullman Congregation. She will be remembered as a loving daughter, sister, wife, mother, and grandmother who cherished her family deeply.

She was preceded in death by her son, Mickey “Clay” Waldrep; her parents, James and Joan

articles.title: Obituary: Minnie Jo Thomas
articles.body:
Mrs. Minnie Jo Thomas passed away Tuesday, April 14, 2026, at her residence at the age of 90.

A Celebration of Life Service will be Saturday, April 18th, 2026 at 11 a.m. at Vinemont Christian Church. Interment will follow at Mt. Zion Cemetery.

The family will receive friends at the church from 9-11 a.m.

Jo was born in Birmingham, AL, on February 11, 1936.  She was the daughter of Homer Wiley Blankenship and Grace Estelle Rutherford Blankenship. Jo was a hard worker all her life. She attended

articles.title: Brooklyn Merriman crowned 2026 Miss Alabama Strawberry Festival
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The Miss Alabama Strawberry Festival Pageant continues to play a key role in one of Cullman’s most anticipated annual events. This year’s pageant was held Saturday, April 12, at the Desperation Church Student Center. Fairview High School’s Brooklyn Merriman took home the crown.

The pageant has been part of the festival for decades. While it was once held during festival weekend, organizers now host it in advance so Miss Alabama Strawberry Festival can take part in festival activi

articles.title: Wallace State Jazz Band hosting annual Big Band Dance April 17
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– Wallace State Community College will host the annual Big Band Dance on Friday, April 17, at 7 p.m., in the Burrow Center for the Fine and Performing Arts.

The event will feature the Wallace State Jazz Band and the WSCC alumni Jazz Band. More than 20 former Wallace State Jazz Band musicians plan to attend and perform with the group, representing alumni from the late 1970s to more recent graduates from the Class of 2025.

“We are celebrating the 50th anniversary of the Wallace S

articles.title: EDITORIAL: As cameras go up, questions follow
articles.body:
What is Flock Safety? What does it do? What does it mean for residents and visitors?

They are small, often mounted high on poles or along roadways, easy to miss unless you know what you are looking for.

But for many communities across the country, Flock cameras have become a growing part of modern policing.

The systems are operated by Flock Safety, a private company that provides automated license plate reader technology to law enforcement agencies, cities and even private neighborhoods.

The

articles.title: Rams soccer wins three straight
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
The Valley High School boys soccer team continued its strong run of form this week, earning three consecutive victories over Lanett, Chattahoochee County and Horseshoe Bend in an impressive stretch of play.

The Rams opened the streak with a hard-fought 2-1 win over Lanett. Eddy Leon delivered the decisive moment, scoring the game-winning goal with less than two minutes remaining. Matias Gutierrez assisted on the play, while Jose Diaz added a penalty kick goal before halftime. Goalkeeper Khristi

articles.title: Point University lacrosse knocks off No. 4 SCAD on Senior Day
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
The seniors of the Point University Skyhawks lacrosse team made their last game at River Bowl Stadium arguably their best one, upsetting the No. 4 Savannah College of Art and Design (SCAD) Bees behind the game-winning goal from Malyke Sillers with 1:21 left in regulation.

The Skyhawks closed the game on a fourth-quarter rally after trailing by three entering the final period.

Point struck first on a goal by Shaun McLaughlin, and Rodney Oakley added another early in the first quarter. SCAD resp

articles.title: Springwood soccer has chance to make history
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Both Springwood’s girls and boys soccer teams could come away with a state championship title this Friday in Montgomery.

Both teams are headed to the Final Four this Thursday, playing at the Emory Folmar Soccer Complex in Montgomery. The girls will take the field against Evangel (Alabaster) at 11 a.m. CT/noon ET, followed by the boys against Fort Dale at 1 p.m. CT/2 pm ET. The Lady Wildcats made it to the Final Four after a dominant Elite Eight showing against East Central on Monday, winning 5-

articles.title: Springwood’s Moore signs to play basketball at Huntingdon
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Surrounded by family, friends and coaches on Wednesday, Springwood senior Clarence Moore signed to play basketball for the Huntingdon Hawks.

“It’s truly a blessing,” Moore said about getting to play at the collegiate level. “I am glad I got signed to go to the next level to play college basketball. It’s always been a dream of mine.”

Moore is ready to play ball for the Hawks.

“The first time I went on a visit it felt like home,” he shared. “There was a good environment and I talked to the coac

articles.title: Ruby Clarice McCarley Hendrickson
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Ruby Clarice McCarley Hendrickson, 93, of Hixson, TN, passed away on Sunday, April 12, 2026, surrounded by her children. She is now reunited with her family and dear friends. We are comforted knowing that she is free from dementia and the physical ailments she persevered through without complaint since 2019. She truly is of The Greatest Generation.

Born on July 22, 1932, to the late Willis Floyd McCarley, Sr. and Ruby Greer McCarley, she was a 1950 graduate of Valley High School in Fairfax, AL,

articles.title: West Point hears updates about West Point Elementary
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
West Point Elementary aims to make kids learners and productive members of society. Working with stakeholders, school leaders want to provide a safe, supportive and encouraging learning environment for West Point’s children to make them smarter and overall better people.

Principal Keneithia Cook spoke to the West Point City Council on Monday about how the school is promoting that environment.

She said the school is part of the statewide Positive Behavioral Interventions and Supports (PBIS) sys

articles.title: Valley hears resident’s concern about sign removal, presents proclamations
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
A Valley resident addressed the city council on Monday about some signs she had placed in her yard that were removed without explanation. Double Drive resident Travanique Sellars told the council that the two signs promoted local businesses, one a CPA firm and another a business that had done some work on her driveway.

“Give me a way to correct my ways,” she said, explaining that she felt disrespected by the way things had been done. “Just let me know, and I will take care of it.”

Council Memb

We have finally stepped into a much more sophisticated age where being connected globally is just the baseline. This whole shift is really about meeting a new demand for high-quality interaction and transitions that don't feel like a chore.
