articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Habitat for Humanity looks to rebuild local momentum

The Covington County chapter of Habitat for Humanity is undergoing a transitional phase and is in need of volunteers. The chapter was founded in 2009 under the leadership of Dale Pancake. “We began exploring the possibility of starting a chapter in 2007,” Pancake said. “We became affiliated in 2008 and built our first house in 2009. The mission of Habitat for Humanity is to build safe affordable houses for deserving families. To qualify, the

articles.title: Bowers to join Mizell’s Clinic 1
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Mizell Memorial Hospital in Opp, AL is pleased to announce that Katie Bowers is joining the provider team at Mizell Clinic 1 located on the campus of Mizell Memorial Hospital.

Bowers holds a Bachelor of Science in Nursing and will graduate with a Master of Science in Nursing with a Family Nurse Practitioner track, in May 2026. She began her nursing career in the Emergency Department at Mizell Memorial Hospital where she “gained strong clinical skills and experience caring for a diverse patient

articles.author: Sarah Kate Rhodes
articles.body:
Andalusia City Schools recently set into motion their plans to add an Early Education Center on Andalusia Elementary School’s campus for their Pre-K and Kindergarten programs, and this development is evidence of ACS’ commitment to investing in the “district’s youngest learners.”

“The new facility will provide a modern, safe and engaging learning environment specifically tailored to meet the developmental needs of early learners,” stated Superintendent Dr. Daniel Shakespeare. The Pre-K and Kinde

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Steve Hubbard is running for a spot on the State Democratic Executive Committee (SDEC) because he believes he can promote unity among members of the SDEC, the governing body of the Alabama Democratic Party.

The 2024 Democratic nominee for House District 92, a former member of the SDEC, and the current chair of the Covington County Democratic Party, he brings experience that will help him to represent District 92 concerns and to take a long view of the direction of the state party.

He promises

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Mizell Memorial Hospital in Opp, AL is pleased to announce that Breann Green, CRNP, will be joining the Mizell family as a provider at the Elba Healthcare Clinic in Elba, AL.

Green is a native of Elba, AL and is strong advocate of “keeping primary care in Elba.” She is not a stranger to Elba Healthcare as she did most of her clinical training with Dylan McCullough, CRNP, during his tenure with Elba Healthcare. She is an athlete “at heart” and has a special interest in sports medicine but truly

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Lurleen B. Wallace (LBW) Community College will welcome Deputy Chancellor of Instruction, Research and Development Dr. Chris Cox of the Alabama Community College System as the commencement speaker for its 2026 graduation ceremony on May 8.

Dr. Cox brings more than 30 years of experience in education leadership, spanning both K-12 and higher education, including a previous role as interim president at LBW Community College.

“Dr. Cox understands the transformative power of community colleges bec

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Covington Electric Cooperative (CEC) recently contributed a combined $15,000 to two organizations dedicated to supporting and protecting children across Covington County. Through match funding provided by CoBank, a national cooperative bank serving electric cooperatives and other rural infrastructure providers, the impact of these contributions was doubled to a total of $30,000.

CEC donated $5,000 to the Covington County Child Advocacy Center, matched by CoBank to provide $10,000 in total suppo

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Lurleen B. Wallace is hosting the 2026 Student Art Exhibit featuring Diverse Media until April 30, and all are welcome to come see the unique, impressive works of LBW’s student body.

The exhibition will include an impressive collection of cardboard sculptures, pastel drawings, creative lamps, and graphite works, offering visitors a rich and engaging artistic experience. This year’s show promises a unique blend of styles and materials, highlighting the versatility and innovation of LBW’s student

articles.title: National Day of Prayer celebration set for May 7
articles.author: Sarah Kate Rhodes
articles.body:
Thursday, May 7 is the 2026 National Day of Prayer, and many of Covington County’s local churches are planning to gather at the Covington County Courthouse at noon to join together in prayer.

Created in 1952 through a joint resolution of the United States, Congress and President Harry Truman, the National Day of Prayer is annually observed on the first Thursday of May. This is an opportunity for all Americans to pray for the nation they love. Whether praying for the US government, military, and

articles.title: Amazing Grace to open for Mother’s Day
articles.author: Sarah Kate Rhodes
articles.body:
The Amazing Grace Cafe closed in 2025, but owner and operator Patricia Grace has not yet sold the restaurant’s building. So, this Mother’s Day, Grace plans to reopen her cafe for one day only.

The Amazing Grace will begin serving at 10 a.m. and continue service until supplies run out. Grace expressed her excitement for the limited reopening, and she is looking forward to seeing many familiar faces on May 10. She is opening to help serve her community, as she understands planning meals can be di

articles.author: Jan White
articles.body:
Crossover Ministry will hold its Annual Banquet on Tuesday, May 5, at 6:30 p.m. in the Fellowship Hall of the First Baptist Church in Opp. The guest speaker for the evening will be Benji Jordan.

Benji Jordan is a graduate of Crossover Ministry and has dedicated 13 years to serving as the pastor at Antioch Congregational Church, demonstrating a deep commitment to his community and faith. articles.body:
A Bible-reading marathon began on Monday, April 18, and will continue through today, Saturday, April 25. In connection with the 250th anniversary of the United States, this special event, “America Reads The Bible,” has been held at The Museum of the Bible in Washington, D.C.

articles.body:
Local law enforcement is alerting Limestone County to a current phone scam that preys on residents’ fear of neglecting to pay important court and enforcement penalties.

The Limestone County Sheriff’s Office cautioned the public to be on the lookout for the scam, which can come in the form of predatory text messages or phone calls, in which the sender or caller falsely claims that the recipient “owe[s] money for outstanding traffic violations, unpaid tolls, parking tickets, or missed court appea

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
We have art everywhere here in our Tennessee River Valley. Did you know people come from all around to share their artwork at our various shows? We Owls only need fly by the license plates of cars at art shows to know that’s true. Consider two well-known exhibits as Art by the Lake in Guntersville, or the Bloomin’ Festival in Cullman at the Benedictine Monastery. All manner of artwork, from paintings to figurines to jewelry to carvings, and on and on, are on display. What is going on?

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL PLAYOFFS: Athens falls to Hazel Green, 7-6, in Game 3 tiebreaker
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Athens’ season ended in heartbreaking fashion on Friday night.

After splitting a pair of matchups with Hazel Green on Thursday, the Golden Eagles fell short in a Game 3 tiebreaker, 7-6, to end their season at 19-16.

Hazel Green jumped out to an early 5-0 advantage in the Class 6A playoff series, but Athens answered with six consecutive runs to take a lead.

The Trojans, though, added two runs in the fifth inning that proved the difference.

Game 3

Hazel Green 7, Athens 6

Hudson Marks:2-for-3

articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
East Limestone delivered a knockout performance in the opening round of the Class 5A playoffs.

Behind a superb pitching effort, the Indians swept Arab 4-0 and 13-1 in a home doubleheader on Friday to punch their ticket to the next stage of the postseason.

East Limestone (23-8) will play Lincoln next week.

East Limestone 4, Arab 0

articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Athens Bible rode a potent offensive performance to an opening-round series win on Friday night.

The Trojans racked up 17 hits against No. 6 Lynn, and Luke Murrell struck out 15 batters across six strong innings en route to a 16-4 victory in the Class 1A playoffs.

Athens Bible (18-11) will duke it out with No. 8 Skyline or Waterloo in the second round next week.

articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
A traffic stop in northern Limestone County resulted in the recent arrest of an Elkmont man, who faces a charge in trafficking stolen identities as well as an array of drug possession allegations.

John Sprain, 42, of Elkmont was arrested after deputies with the Limestone County Sheriff’s Office (LCSO) conducted a stop for an alleged traffic infraction just south of Ardmore. articles.title: Tanner woman charged with ‘multiple’ financial crimes in Thursday arrest
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
A Tanner woman has posted bond following her arrest Thursday after an investigation begun by the Limestone County Sheriff’s Office (LCSO) in February into suspected financial crimes.

LCSO arrested Angela Sides, 51, of Tanner on Thursday (April 23) and charged with five counts of identity theft and 1st degree theft of property, according to the sheriff’s office. Sides posted a $17,500 bond and was released pending further court proceedings.

The arrest stems from an investigation into allegation

articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Want to get to know your local first responders just a little bit better? Today’s the day to meet and mingle with law enforcement and more response personnel in Ardmore, where everyone is invited to come out and meet the men and women behind the badge (and the fire trucks, the ambulances, and first-response lifesaving equipment).

articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Elkmont will carry momentum — and bragging rights — into the next stage of the postseason.

Behind an offensive barrage, the No. 10 Red Devils earned a pair of wins over county foe Clements on Thursday to advance to the second round of the Class 3A playoffs.

Layton Schrimsher and Lincoln Donerhomered in the opener to help Elkmont(24-10) secure an 8-5 victory.

articles.body:

Arthur Orr, provides an income tax deduction for qualified overtime compensation, up to $1,000. The legislation also suspends the state’s portion of the grocery tax for two months beginning in May.

The ceremony will be held at 8:15 a.m., right before the start of Mayfest.

Ullysses McBride was born on November 27, 1933, in Atmore.

After graduating from Escambia County Training School, McBride earned his bachelor’s degree from Knoxville College, master’s degree from Indiana University and doctorate from Auburn University.

He completed post-doctoral studies at Troy University, Clark University (Worcester, Mass.), and the University of New York. He was a member of Alpha Kappa Mu and Kappa Delta Pi (national honor societies).

articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Dale Harrison, 65, of Atmore, passed away on April 17, 2026.

He was born on May 9, 1960, to James Leonard and Marion Lorraine Galloway Harrison.

He is preceded in death by his parents; and his brother, Jimmy Harrison.

Dale is survived by his brother, Wayne (Barbara) Harrison of Atmore; his nieces and nephews, Jim Harrison, Chris Harrison, Glendon Harrison, Travis Harrison, Dustin Harrison, Michael Harrison, Lynn Thurber, Lesley Ann Harrison and extended family and friends.

articles.title: Sloss Metal Arts Summer Youth Apprenticeship Applications Open
articles.body:
birminghamal.gov

Applications are open through April 28 for the annual Summer Youth Apprenticeship at Sloss Furnaces.

The Summer Youth Apprenticeship was founded on the importance of passing on the history of Sloss and the many techniques and traditions of metal art. This paid, 8-week program employs 10-15 high school students, aged 16-19, to work and study under professional artists for 30 hours per week.

articles.title: Alabama State University Names New Director of Bands After 30 Years
articles.body:
Alabama State University has named Dr. Derrick Smith as its new Director of Bands, marking the beginning of a new chapter for one of the most recognizable HBCU marching band programs in the country.

The Mighty Marching Hornets announced the hire on social media, welcoming Smith back to campus as a proud alumnus and former trumpet section leader of the renowned Mighty Marching Hornets.

articles.title: CCA hosts second annual fishing tournament
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
Chilton Christian Academy hosted its second annual fishing tournament on earlier in April, seeing an excellent turnout of 44 boats participating.

“This was our second annual tournament; we had 44 boats, and we just continue to grow,” said Katie Mitchell, who helped organize the event. “We had some people approach us who want to be a bigger part of it next year.”

All of the funds raised from the fishing tournament go towards CCA athletics, providing opportunities to students they would not have

articles.title: Best of Strawberry winners announced from fifth annual festival
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
The 2026 Chilton County Strawberry Festival featured Best of Strawberry contests that showcased the best strawberry-themed artwork, dishes and strawberries from the area.

In the Best of Strawberry Art Contest, Kinzely Price was named the Overall Best of Berry for her artwork. articles.title: PREP BASEBALL PLAYOFFS: No. 3 Cullman splits with Southside-Gadsden to set up tiebreaker; Fairview, Vinemont, West Point eliminated
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Cullman’s playoff fate will be decided in a winner-take-all tiebreaker.

The No. 3 Bearcats knocked off Southside-Gadsden 10-3 on Friday before falling in the nightcap 17-8 to set up a decisive Game 3 on Saturday at 5 p.m.

Jonah Inman and Knox Mauldin each homered in the opener to help Cullman earn an easy victory.

Carson Stancil continued the momentum with a first-inning homer in Game 2, and Cullman (25-10) built a 6-0 cushion before Southside-Gadsden stormed back to eventually flip the scrip

articles.title: PREP BASKETBALL: Fairview hires Meyer as varsity boys coach
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Fairview has hired Bobby Meyer as its new varsity boys basketball coach.

Meyer, who has previous stops at Cullman and Vinemont, spent the past two seasons as the varsity boys coach at West Morgan, where he led the Rebels to consecutive Elite Eights.

articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –The Cullman Bearcats kicked off their 2026 playoffs with a first round matchup against the Southside Panthers on Friday night.

In game one, the Bearcats handled Southside with ease in a 10-3 victory. Jonah Inman and Knox Mauldin both homered, and starting pitcher Michael Shadeck went 5.2 innings, giving up six hits and three earned runs while striking out five batters.

articles.body: BOAZ, Ala. –The West Point Warriors took a road trip over to Boaz to take on the Pirates in the first round of the Class 5A state playoffs. Game one was a pitcher’s duel through four innings, and Boaz held a 3-1 lead after six innings. The Warriors went on a late rally to take the lead, but the Pirates tied it up to send the game into extra innings. Boaz ended up scoring on an error in their final at-bats, as they would go on to take game one, 5-4. Game two was all Pirates, as they jumped out to

articles.body:
Jeanean Carolyn Taylor, 83, of Crane Hill, Alabama, passed away on April 22, 2026. Born on February 2, 1943, Jeanean’s life was marked by a steady devotion to her home and family, and by a quiet pride in the work and care she gave to what she loved most.

articles.title: Zoning board approves Auto Zone expansion
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –The City of Cullman Zoning Board of Adjustments & Appeals at its April 21 meeting approved an expansion for Auto Zone.

Auto Zone, located at 210 Second Ave. NW, will expand from 9,000 square feet to 16,000 square feet. The board said the expansion will improve the location for customers as well as neighboring businesses. It will allow better traffic flow for part shipments by giving delivery trucks more room so as to not disrupt surrounding roadways.

HANCEVILLE, Ala.– On Friday, April 17, Wallace State Community College marked a special milestone as the annual Big Band Dance brought together current musicians and alumni from many years of the college’s jazz program.

Led by Ricky Burks, the event celebrated the band’s 50-year history, which started with its first director, Robert Bean. Alumni from different years came back to perform and quickly formed a strong group, even though many had never played together before.

articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –The Cullman City Board of Education at its April 21 meeting recognized student award winners.

Cullman High School Junior Grayson Gay was recognized for recently being named a 2026 Alabama Public Television Young Hero. Gay is one of only three students to be named an APT Young Hero this year. See a separate feature on Gay atwww.cullmantribune.com/2026/03/12/cullman-highs-grayson-gay-named-apt-2026-young-hero.

articles.body:
CLANTON – Friday, 7:30 am, April 24, 2026

A line of thunderstorms will move into western Alabama this evening and rapidly weaken once reaching the I-65 region between 12 am and 6 am.  A few storms west of I-65 could produce wind gusts from 40-60 mph.  Redevelopment of showers and thunderstorms is expected Saturday afternoon and evening.  A few storms could produce wind gusts from 35-50 mph.

After a relatively quiet Sunday with just a few showers and thunderstorms, a stronger system will bring

articles.title: Chambers Academy softball making a statement
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
The Chambers Academy softball teams are putting on a show this season.

Last weekend, the JV team became back-to-back state champions after defeating Bessemer Academy.

It’s a big deal for the JV Lady Rebels to be state champions again.

“It’s something that we poured into the program over the past two years. You know, with that goal in mind, to see the girls’ success and [see them] achieve that goal means a lot to all of us. So, very proud of the girls,” head coach Chase Earl said.

The JV team

articles.title: Springwood powerlifter gets state titles
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Springwood’s Marcus Lee made the Wildcats proud at the AISA Powerlifting Championship held on April 15 at Lowndes Academy. Lee won the 165-pound championship. He was also named the overall BMI champion, after lifting a total of 1,055 pounds across the squat, bench press and deadlift.

The international student from Vietnam only started getting into training for powerlifting meets this year after being introduced to the sport at Springwood. He put in the work to make the titles happen, including

articles.title: Kia Georgia officially launches solar canopy
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
On Friday, Vehicle Protection Services (VPS), Kia Georgia and Georgia Power cut the ribbon for a large-scale hail protection and solar canopy system at Kia’s plant in West Point.

VPS created the turnkey system for Kia Georgia after a 2023 hailstorm caused extensive damage to Kia’s vehicle supply. Kia wanted a way to mitigate risk and create long-term value. Their solution, the hail protection and solar canopy system, keeps vehicles safe in inclement weather, provides shelter for employees as th

articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Rosephanye Powell, a native of Lanett and a professor of voice at Auburn University, was recently commissioned to compose a new choral work for the national celebration of America’s 250th anniversary held later this year.

The work is being performed by the West Point Glee Club at the United States Military Academy located in West Point, New York. Powell was commissioned by Constance Chase during Chase’s time as director of the West Point Glee Club. Powell found out that Chase had performed seve

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Loran Smith: Mules
articles.author: Loran Smith
articles.body:
On a recent trip from Athens to Hartwell, I enjoyed driving the backroads and monitoring the fields which have or are being prepared for spring planting.

There were plenty of John Deere tractors about, which always makes my day. I remember what the old tricycle model MT meant to our family. It was the most versatile of machines. It could do things beyond its primary purpose—to use its two-row design to cultivate crops that were the staple of our survival.

We began the New Year by using a tille

articles.author: Dean Collins
articles.body:
The first thing I thought today as I read Psalm 49 was that it was in the wrong book of the Bible. Psalm 49 sounds more like it should be either in Proverbs or possibly Ecclesiastes since it is a mixture of practical wisdom and a stark reminder that whether you do good or bad, we all die.

The second thing I started thinking about is how this Psalm is a testament to God’s mercy and grace.

This Psalm is attributed to the sons of Korah. You may recall the story of Korah from the sixteenth chapter

articles.author: Brandon Ussery

The City of West Point now has another restaurant in the downtown area. La Familia had its official grand opening on Wednesday, April 22. Members of the LaGrange-Troup Chamber of Commerce welcomed the La Familia staff to the area with a ribbon-cutting ceremony.

Several city officials and neighboring business owners stopped by to show support to the new business.

Connie Hensler, president of the LaGrange-Troup Chamber of Commerce, addressed everyone in attendance to let them know about the staf

articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
The Crenshaw County Board of Education met on April 20 for its regularly scheduled board meeting. The meeting featured several recognitions highlighting student achievements and staff contributions across the district.

“Students of the month were recognized from each school,” said Crenshaw County Schools Superintendent Ashley Catrett. “These were Chaz Branum from Brantley, Brody Johnson from Luverne and Braelynn King from Highland Home. The board also honored this year’s valedictorians and salu

articles.title: Keldric Faulk picked up by Tennessee Titans in first round draft
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
Highland Home graduate Keldric Faulk was picked up by the Tennessee Titans as 31st in the first round draft on Thursday, April 23. Faulk has played defensive end at Auburn University since he graduated high school in 2023.

“It feels great,” Faulk said in a media release on  Tennesseetitan’s.com. “I saw the number come up on my phone and it said Titans on the bottom and I said, ‘Shoot, I know who it is now.’ When I first got the call, everybody started jumping and hollering. It kind of startled

articles.title: Dinner honors ministers' wives, mission leaders
articles.body: The Alabama-Crenshaw Woman's Missionary Union (WMU) executive leadership team celebrated minister's wives and missions leaders at a dinner held April 16. Hosted by South Luverne Baptist Church, the event honored women who serve their congregations and provided a fun, inspiring evening filled with games, fellowship and an encouraging message by Jackie Campbell with the Alabama Baptist Children's Home. She urged women in attendance to "Keep on," with a devotional taken from Galatians 6:9.

articles.title: Opinion | America’s guardrails are eroding, and instability is growing
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
We are living through instability, and we keep telling ourselves it’s because we no longer agree.

That explanation is too easy.

Stability was built on guardrails like rules, norms and institutional restraints that forced those in power to slow down, compromise and consider consequences before acting.

For a long time, those guardrails held, even when the country didn’t agree. Over the past decade, however, we’ve begun to remove them—not all at once, but enough to change how the system behaves.

Several states are considering bills related to mandated insurance coverage for menopause and perimenopause, as well as other proposals, saying it improves quality of life and work productivity when people can access care to mitigate symptoms. Some states have already rejected the idea this session. (Getty Images)

Transgender rights supporters and opponents rally outside of the U.S. Supreme Court as the high court hears arguments in a case on transgender health rights in December 2024 in Washington, D.C. An appeals court ruling in light of that case, U.S. v. Skrmetti, upheld West Virginia’s ban on Medicaid coverage for adult gender-affirming surgery. Experts say it could have broad implications. articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
MAPLESVILLE –Keith High School saw its postseason run come to an abrupt end Thursday, April 23, as defensive miscues and a lack of offensive production led to a two-game sweep at the hands of Maplesville in the first round of the AHSAA Class 1A playoffs.

The Bears fell 16-0 in the series opener before dropping the second contest 15-0. Both games were called after three innings via the mercy rule. The sweep eliminated Keith from the state tournament, while Maplesville advances to the second roun

articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
The Keith Lady Bears turned the Thursday afternoon of April 23 into a home-run derby, exploding for 19 runs in the first inning to overwhelm Amelia Love Johnson 27-8.

The victory showcased the Bears’ elite offensive identity, combining power at the plate with a relentless, record-setting performance on the base paths.

Keith wasted no time establishing dominance, sending the bulk of their roster to the plate in a marathon first inning. The team’s middle-of-the-order hitters dismantled the Johns

articles.title: Selma native Mary Martin Mitchell to serve as next Revenue Commissioner
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
MONTGOMERY – Governor Kay Ivey on Wednesday announced Alabama Department of Revenue (ADOR) Commissioner Vernon Barnett will retire from state service at the end of the month. The governor has selected Selma native Mary Martin Mitchell, senior attorney within the department, to assume the role.

“Vernon has been an outstanding member of the Ivey Administration from the beginning and has served the people of our state faithfully. When I took office in 2017, I promised the people of Alabama I would

articles.title: United by purpose: Crime Victims’ Rights Week highlights the cost of violence
articles.author: Briana Wilson
articles.body:
Silent tears fell in the Dallas County Courtroom as the face of a smiling boy appeared on a screen–the first face of many shown who were lost to violence. For some families in the pews, the sight was too much to bear, so they slipped out of the courtroom in tears, leaving behind a room of people united by a somber reality: behind every statistic is a name, a face and a family left picking up the pieces.

This slideshow honoring victims played on Tuesday, April 21, in the Dallas County Courthouse

City officials said the program was created to give residents, civic leaders, and community partners a firsthand look at the department’s daily operations, emergency response capabilities, and overall public safety mission.

Montgomery Fire/Rescue, which holds both an ISO Class 1 rating and national accreditation—distinctions earned by only a

With kids swinging and swishing down the slide as a backdrop, Chris Blankenship, Commissioner of the Alabama Department of Conservation and Natural Resources (ADCNR), made the long-awaited reopening ofLake Lurleen State Parkofficial last week.

articles.body:
Former Alabama Supreme Court Chief Justice Roy Moore’s $8.2 million defamation verdict was reversed Friday by a federal appeals court, which found Moore failed to prove a political ad defamed him during his 2017 U.S. Senate campaign.

The former Troy State art professor was well known for his iconic Fernware dishes, sculptural torsos, wall panels and his glass-fired “pottery.’

In the early 1940s, “Mister Walter” discovered how to fire glass in a pottery kiln. He often infused his work with found metals and experimented with all types of materials in his glass sculptures.

Noah Jonathan Green was charged with fourth-degree theft of property, possession of a controlled substance and second-degree possession of marijuana.

The first request was for the re-zoning of 0.54 acres of land located at 201 Highland Avenue from R-1: Low Density Residential Zoning to the R-3: High Density Residential Zoning District with the intention of turning the property into a rental property.

The CHHS Trojans (18-2-3) defeated Carroll by a score of 3-2 to earn a spot in the second round. Daniel Frigge earned one goal and one assist for the Trojans, while Robert Lindsey and Jordan Green scored one goal each. Jealin Atwell also tallied two assists in the Trojan victory.

The Lady Trojans (10-5-2) also throttled Eufaula by a

articles.title: As drought worsens, Alabama Extension offers insights, resources
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Consistent dry weather is causing concerns for Alabama agriculture. Planting has slowed, pastures are stressed and ponds are drying up.

The U.S. Drought Monitor released April 23 reported 100 percent of the state is now experiencing drought conditions of varying severity. Extreme drought has been reported in 46 percent of the state. That’s a 20-percent increase in that drought category since the previous week. The driest portions of the state stretch across south and into west central Alabama.

articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Athens’ Blake Elmore began his 3D printing business as a small-scale, spare-time hobbyist. Now, he’s meeting the demand for his services from across a broad swath of north Alabama with his very own brick-and-mortar storefront.

For Elmore, what started as a simple hobby has grown into 3rd Dimension Designs 3D Printing & Supply, a thriving small business rooted in family, creativity, and community. Elmore opened his new local store this month at 935 U.S. Highway 72 East, Suite E — located beside

articles.title: Sharon Brown Memorial Car Show draws nice crowd Saturday
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
The third annual Sharon Brown Memorial Car Show drew a nice crowd given the weather.

Several area residents attended the event, which included newer and classic cars, wrestling matches, drawings for giveaways and more.

Mayor Shawn Lassiter served as one of the judges of the car show.

articles.title: EMJ Outreach holds annual Autism Awareness Walk Saturday
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
In spite of the rain Saturday morning, a good crowd turned out for EMJ Outreach's annual Autism Awareness Walk.

The walkers gathered and fellowshipped under the canopy of the old Dean's Grill location, waiting out the rain.

Once the weather cleared, the participants walked all the way from EMJ's sign to Heritage Park.

articles.title: Jack Mitchell
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Jack Mitchell, 86, of Walnut Hill, Fla., passed away on April 22, 2026.

He was born on December 13, 1939, to John Mitchell and Mary Graham Mitchell.

Jack is survived by his wife, Hazel Flowers Mitchell; his daughters, Pam (Randy) Johnson of Stockton and Tammy (Danny) Sightler of Andalusia; four grandchildren, Kevin (Rebecca) Johnson, Ryan (Sarah Jane) Johnson, Danielle (Walt) Meeks and Travis Sightler; and five great-grandchildren, Brook, Madison, Macey, Kaylee and Hudson Tate.

He is also sur

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL PLAYOFFS: No. 3 Cullman ousts Southside-Gadsden to advance
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Cullman made easy work of Southside-Gadsden on Saturday, earning an 11-0 victory in Game 3 of the Class 6A playoff series to advance to the second round.

The No. 3 Bearcats (26-10) scored four runs in the first inning to take control early, and Jonah Inman capped the win with a walk-off grand slam in the fifth inning.

Landyn Henry, meanwhile, took care of business on the mound, scattering three hits and four strikeouts in the shutout.

Offensively, Cross Bowen (three), Inman (two), Jake Weathe

articles.title: (Gallery) Alabama Strawberry Festival returns to downtown Cullman
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
The Alabama Strawberry Festival returned Friday and Saturday to downtown Cullman to celebrate the area’s rich agricultural heritage.

In 2023, the Governor of Alabama officially recognized the annual strawberry celebration in Cullman as the Alabama Strawberry Festival. Now in its 87th year, the event is Alabama’s longest-running strawberry festival.

Local farmers were on hand selling their produce including fresh, locally grown strawberries under the Festhalle Marketplatz.

Along with food, car

articles.title: ‘WILL BE GREATLY MISSED’: Local sports mainstay Johnny Thornton dies
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Johnny Thornton, who began his admirable career as a correspondent withThe Timesunder former sports editor Bill Shelton and who became a fixture at sporting events across Cullman County, has died.

The man known as “The Flash” earned induction into the Cullman County Sports Hall of Fame in 2009, thanks in no small part to a steady dedication to his craft.

The Good Hope graduate left his mark on the local landscape, serving as Wallace State’s sports information director for many years and ultima

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL: Inman’s grand slam finishes off Southside, Bearcats win 11-0 to move on to round two
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –After a blowout game one win over Southside followed by a stunning loss in game two, Cullman hosted the Panthers for a series-deciding third game on Saturday afternoon. Like the first game, the Bearcats struck quickly and built a lead. In the bottom of the fifth, designated hitter Jonah Inman slapped a grand slam to center field to end the game and the series with an 11-0 win.

Taking the mound for the Bearcats was Landyn Henry, who had not previously thrown in the series. With a

articles.title: COLUMN: ‘This Little Line of Mine’
articles.body:
I am a writer. I write articles, books and songs. While I have won a few awards for some of my newspaper and magazine articles, I have never written a best-selling book or song. I have written hundreds of songs, recorded a few off them and lost or thrown away many others. My first printed book was a 184-page doctoral dissertation 31 years ago. They only printed four copies, and a dear friend actually bought one!

Not counting that dissertation, I recently finished writing my 11th book, which I’v

articles.title: Files from Yesteryear: April 25, 1940
articles.body:
From the files of April 25, 1940

Joppa News

The order of the day around Joppa this week has been land preparation and planting. Mr. Waylon Ogletree reports that he has some 40 acres of ground prepared and ready to plant. Mr. Ogletree also said that he has some 10 acres of corn already planted.

Mr. J. M. Taylor and sons have done extensive land preparation in the past few days with tractor and plows.

Mr. Woodrow Morris and family have moved back to the Joppa community. Mr. Morris is at presen

articles.title: Obituary: Jackie Wayne Needham
articles.body:
Jackie Wayne Needham, of Blountsville, Alabama, passed away on April 23, 2026, at the age of 70. Mr. Needham was born in Cullman County, Alabama, on January 10, 1956, to the late Elbert Leon and Mildred Irene Stancil Needham.

Mr. Needham is preceded in death by his parents; his beloved wife, Patricia “Starr” Needham; and his siblings, Rayford Needham, Rayburn Needham, Gearld Needham, Kenny Needham, and Pam Malin.

Those left to cherish the many memories of Jackie include his children, Alicia Da

articles.title: Obituary: Robert “Bob” Chaney
articles.body: Mr. Robert Leon “Bob” Chaney, age 79, of Cullman passed away on Wednesday, April 22, 2026, at Cullman Regional Medical Center, surrounded by his loving family. A memorial service will be held in his honor on Sunday, May 17, 2026, at 2:00 p.m. at Cullman Heritage Funeral Home. Family would like to welcome all who knew Bob to attend.Bob was born on February 26, 1947, to Mr. and Mrs. Chesley Chaney.He is preceded in death by his parents; sister, Carolyn Heatherly.He is survived by his wife, Patrici

articles.title: Obituary: Judy Yates Nelms
articles.body: Judy Yates Nelms, 72, passed on April 23, 2026. Judy was a resident of Cullman, Alabama. Judy was born in Franklin, Georgia, to the late Glenn Dewey Yates and the late Eloise Cummings Yates. Judy was raised in a home filled with lots of love and even more laughter, thanks to the many siblings, cousins, and other family members who frequented the Yates family home. It was during those years that she found a love for Jesus during family devotions every evening and endless hours spent at Happy Vall

articles.title: Alleged cockfighting operation under investigation in Cullman County
articles.body:
BAILEYTON, Ala.– Cullman County Animal Control and the Cullman County Sheriff’s Office are investigating an alleged cockfighting operation in Baileyton.

Cullman County Animal Control Director Rodney Banister said they recently took over the investigation after it was reported to the Cullman County Sheriff’s Office.

“We received the complaint and opened up and investigation,” Banister said. “It is still ongoing; no arrests have been made at this time.”

Banister said the complaint came in last

articles.title: Hanceville proclaims April Child Abuse Prevention Month
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala. –The Hanceville City Council, during the public comments portion of its April 23 meeting, heard from Cullman Court Appointed Special Advocate (CASA) Alyson Stewart on the subject of child abuse.

“It’s become a popular subject, but not decreasing in numbers, unfortunately,” she said.

April is National Child Abuse Prevention Month, and one of the ways people raise awareness and honor victims of child abuse is by wearing blue. Stewart expressed the need for change, saying Alabama

articles.title: The Tribune Debate Series returns, and the invitation stands
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The Tribune Debate Series will return in May, bringing candidates together in a format built on transparency, accountability and direct engagement with the public.

At its core, the debate series exists for one reason: voters deserve to see, hear and evaluate the individuals asking for their vote, side by side, under the same conditions, answering the same questions.

This year’s schedule reflects that continued commitment, with multiple races already confirmed and others still pe

articles.title: County Commission honors historic cemeteries, OKs road and community projects
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The Cullman County Commission at its meeting on April 21, recognized Ryans Creek Cemetery and Good Hope Baptist Church Cemetery for being listed on the Alabama Historical Commission’s Alabama Historic Cemetery Register.

According to the Alabama Historical Commission, the register is “the state’s official list of historic cemeteries in Alabama. The Alabama Historical Commission (AHC) considers historic cemeteries particularly worthy of preservation and appreciation, and therefore

articles.title: CCS Foundation celebrates 30 years, recognizes award winners
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The Cullman City Schools Foundation marked its 30th anniversary during its 15th annual Distinguished Alumni & Service Awards brunch on Tuesday, April 21, at Loft 212.

The Foundation donated $1,050 to the Cullman Middle School Piano Recital from the Tony Brock Music Fund, which helps maintain the school’s piano lab equipment.

CMS Band Director Linda Bean said the piano lab program is uncommon across the country and gives students special opportunities, including advanced study in

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - L. Michelle Moore: Cutting Energy Bills and Preserving Housing in LaGrange
articles.author: L. Michelle Moore
articles.body:
I love LaGrange, and I love its historic mill villages. My parents grew up in the Dunson Mill community where my grandparents lived all their lives, and it will always be home to me.

When you love something, you get to know it pretty well, and I will say with love that many of our community’s mill houses and lots of other homes built during that same era are very expensive to heat and cool and need a lot of repairs. For our neighbors with low- and moderate incomes – including many retired resid

articles.title: Extension champions mental health
articles.body:
People who struggle with their mental health often find it difficult to find resources in their local area. County Commissioner Debra Riley and Chambers County Extension Director Rachel Snoddy have partnered with organizations around Chambers County to spread the word about 988.

988 is the nationwide mental-health crisis hotline — a simple, three-digit number people can call or text when they’re experiencing emotional distress, suicidal thoughts, or a mental-health emergency. It’s designed to b

articles.title: CVHS to reminisce about good old days
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
The spring quarterly meeting of the Chattahoochee Valley Historical Society (CVHS) should be a fun one, and everyone is invited to take part in it via Zoom. It will start at 3 p.m. EDT Sunday, April 26. It’s going to be a “Rememberin’ When” session and anyone joining in can feel free to take part in the online conversation.

“Don’t you just love to reminisce about the good old days?” says Ron Williams, editor of The Voice. “We Southerners love to talk about them. It brings to mind lingering arou

articles.title: LaFayette Parks and Rec faces transition period
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
The City of LaFayette is aiming to revitalize the Parks and Recreation department. Councilmembers Charlene Story and Kim Langley sat with members of the board on April 22 to discuss ways to re-establish the board and confirm membership. During the meeting, Story and Langley listed several challenges facing the department, including updating the list of active board members, addressing the financial management process and funding issues.

Once the meeting was underway, Story called long-time volu

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - John Tures: Would You Recognize Your Savior By Sight?
articles.author: John A. Tures
articles.body:
Last week, liberals and conservatives were briefly united in opposition to our President reposting a picture of himself resembling Jesus Christ on social media. But such a moment gives us a chance to reflect on what really constitutes Christlike behavior, and therefore if we would recognize our savior if we actually saw him.

The level of outrange ranged from a mild rebuke by supporters to a harsh condemnation by opponents for an image designed to make the president look like the Messiah. Even c

articles.title: Kiwanis luncheon celebrates honor students
articles.author: Lanell Downs Smith
articles.body:
The Luverne Kiwanis Club hosted its annual honor-student luncheon Tuesday, April 21, at the Luverne Church of Christ. Guest speaker Walt Merrill inspired students to strive for success, and the club award eight Crenshaw County students with scholarships to help meet their higher education goals.

Scholarships awarded at the luncheon went to:

The club recognized honor graduates from each of the four local high schools. Seniors who received recognition certificates are:

The meal was catered by t

articles.title: Listening to the Good Shepherd's Voice
articles.author: Dunford Cole
articles.body:
One day, I walked into a store to grab a few things. As I went down the aisle, I heard a voice. I didn’t have to see who it was. I knew that voice right away.

I turned down the aisle, and there was my wife, Brandy, talking with a friend. Even in the busy store, with all the noise and people, I could still pick out Brandy’s voice. I recognized it because I know her.

This is what Jesus means when He calls Himself the Good Shepherd. In John 10, Jesus says, “My sheep hear my voice. I know them, an

articles.title: Christians: Watch one another’s back
articles.author: Michael Brooks
articles.body:
We grew up as high school friends and he became a music leader for church congregations. He told me the most embarrassing day was when he mounted the steps to the platform to begin worship, tripped and fell headlong onto the floor. Fortunately, he wasn’t hurt, but there are better ways to begin worship.

I thought of this lately when a conference speaker told a group of us about a famous pulpiteer from another generation who said as he mounted each stair he would pray, “Fill me. Fill me,” beseec

articles.title: Culture of silence around abortions for active-duty military intensifies, researchers say
articles.author: Kelcie Moseley-Morris
articles.body:
Caitlin Russell poses with goats during one of her two deployments to Afghanistan, in 2012, while serving on the cultural support team in the U.S. Army. Speaking with active-duty service members about their experiences with accessing abortion has become more difficult in the current political environment, researchers say. (Photo courtesy of Caitlin Russell)

Researcher Caitlin Gerdts planned to release a new study about abortion access for active-duty military service members, much like the one

articles.title: Trump uninjured after gunfire at Washington press dinner; suspect in custody
articles.author: Jane Norman, Jacob Fischler
articles.body:
Federal agents draw their guns out after an incident at the annual White House Correspondents Association Dinner April 25, 2026. According to reports, President Donald Trump, along with other government officials, were evacuated from the Washington Hilton after what sounded like gun fire. (Photo by Nathan Howard/Getty Images)

President Donald Trump safely evacuated the White House Correspondents Dinner at a hotel in Washington, D.C., on Saturday night after shots were fired by an alleged lone g

articles.title: At Virginia Giuffre memorial, friends and family urge justice for Epstein victims
articles.author: Ashley Murray
articles.body:
Amanda and Sky Roberts, sister-in-law and brother of the late Virginia Roberts Giuffre, read from her posthumous memoir in Washington, D.C., on Saturday, April 25, 2026. (Photo by Ashley Murray/States Newsroom)

WASHINGTON — Family and friends of Virginia Roberts Giuffre gathered in the nation’s capital Saturday to mark one year since her death, and to demand justice for victims of the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

On a stage across from the Ellipse, with the White House in the background,

articles.title: Wright becomes a Saint again after being drafted to New Orleans
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
The New Orleans Saints bolstered their offensive line Saturday, April 25, selecting Auburn offensive guard and Selma alumnus Jeremiah Wright with the 132 overall pick in the fourth round of the 2026 NFL Draft.

The 6-foot-5, 331-pound Wright becomes the latest interior lineman added to the Saints’ roster, bringing a physical “mauler” reputation to New Orleans. Wright’s selection followed a pre-draft process where he was a frequent visitor at the Saints’ facility and a standout participant in the

articles.title: Dr. Oz helps Alabama teen secure experimental cancer treatment after viral plea
articles.body:
Will Roberts, an Alabamateenbattling a rare and aggressive form of cancer, is now set to receive access to an experimental treatment after his emotional plea on social media gained widespread attention and prompted swift action from public figures and medical professionals.

In a post shared by his mother, the family said the breakthrough came within hours of the video spreading online, ultimately leading to direct contact with doctors developing the drug.

According to the post, the family rece

articles.title: Alabama-based BCRFA marks 30 years, invests $17 million in breast cancer research statewide
articles.body: What began as one family’s response to a diagnosis has, over three decades, grown into one of Alabama’s most impactful engines forbreast cancer research.

articles.title: 56 Alabama student-athletes placed on SEC Winter Academic Honor Roll
articles.body: Michael Brauner is a Senior Sports Analyst and Contributing Writer for Yellowhammer News. You can follow him on Twitter@MBraunerWNSPand hear him every weekday morning from 6 to 9 a.m. on “The Opening Kickoff” on WNSP-FM 105.5,available free online.

articles.title: Charles Henderson, Ariton sweep into second round
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Charles Henderson Trojans and Ariton Purple Cats earned first round playoff sweeps on Friday to advance to the second round of the state baseball playoffs.

The Charles Henderson Trojans (22-8) swept Eufaula 2-0 in the first round of the Class 5A State Playoffs at home. CHHS defeated Eufaula 8-4 in the game one. Clay Johnson led Charles Henderson at the plate, going 2-for-3 with a double and two RBIs, while Cooper Johnson scored two runs. Trenton Bryan and Wes Templin scored one run each, wh

articles.title: One person hospitalized in alleged Sunday shooting east of Athens
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
A shooting in eastern Limestone County early Sunday evening sent one alleged victim to the hospital, while law enforcement responded at the scene and took one person into custody.

According to the Limestone County Sheriff’s Office (LCSO), one person was shot in the incident, which was called in to dispatchers as a report of a domestic disturbance shortly after 5 p.m. on Sunday, April 26.

The incident occurred in the area of Lon Road east of Athens, located about one mile north of East Limeston

articles.title: Absentee voting deadline approaches for May 19 primary election
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Slightly more than two weeks remain to arrange absentee voting ahead of the May 19 primary election.

County absentee election managers in Alabama must receive mail-in absentee ballot applications by Tuesday, May 12. The deadline to return an absentee ballot application in person is Thursday, May 14. The Limestone County Probate Judge’s Office maintains online up-to-date information on guidelines for local absentee voting atvotelimestone.com/voter-information/absentee-voting.

Absentee ballots b

articles.title: Escambia county boys track takes third at 4A sectional, advances to state meet
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Escambia County’s boys track and field team finished third at the Class 4A, Section 1 meet on Saturday in Andalusia.

With the finish, the Blue Devils advance to the state meet in Gulf Shores April 30 to May 2.

Top ECHS performances from the sectional include:

• Jadon Cox (state qualifier) - 110-meter hurdles – second place; and 300-meter hurdles – first place (section champion)

• Bryson Phillips (state qualifier) - 800-meter run – second place

• Tah’Lijah Saffore (state qualifier) - 100-met

articles.title: Under the Big Top: A Celebration of Culture and Community Arrives in Birmingham
articles.body:
Story and Photos by Marika N. Johnson |For the Birmingham Times

For more than three decades, the UniverSoul Circus has brought a different kind of spectacle beneath the big top — one that blends world-class circus entertainment with the rhythms, traditions and cultural expressions of communities often underrepresented in mainstream circus productions.

The traveling production concluded its Birmingham tour stop on April 26, continuing its legacy of combining acrobatics, dance, music, and audien

articles.title: Former Players Sharpe and Saxton Lead Fairfield’s Football, Girls Basketball Programs
articles.body:
By Solomon Crenshaw Jr. |For the Birmingham Times

When young sports fans of Fairfield High Preparatory School talk about the good old days, they might only think about the beginning of boys basketball success under coach Maurice Ford. But Jazmine Saxton and Rex Sharpe know better.

They are former Fairfield Tiger athletes who experienced success in their playing days. They — Saxton as the girls basketball coach and Sharpe preparing for his first season leading the Fairfield football team — have

articles.title: New Collaborative Offers UAB Students Community-Driven Work to Help the Birmingham Thrive
articles.body:
By Matt Windsor |UAB News

Three faculty members in UAB’sJ. Frank Barefield, Jr. Department of Criminal Justicehave joined forces to give bright undergraduates the opportunity to get real-world experience while serving the surrounding community.

The Community Solutions Collaborative brings together UAB labs focused on digital forensics, law and data science “to serve the City of Birmingham,” said Jeffery T. Walker, Ph.D., University Professor and J. Frank Barefield, Jr. Endowed Chair in Communi

articles.title: From drafting tables to global missions: The journey of Shawn Doss
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– For Hanceville native Shawn Doss, the path from a college drafting lab to mission fields across the globe was anything but expected — but it has always been guided by purpose.

Doss attended Wallace State Community College from 1991 to 1993, earning an associate degree in Drafting and Design. At the time, he envisioned a future rooted firmly in civil engineering, a field he would go on to work in for 15 years. Still, the foundation he gained at Wallace State left a lasting mark

articles.body:
CLANTON – Monday, 6:00 am, April 27, 2026

An area of thunderstorms with heavy rainfall will move into the far northern portions of the state around 12 am tonight and continue southward into Tuesday morning, weakening once reaching I-20.  A few wind gusts from 40-60 mph and very heavy rainfall are possible north of I-20.

Redevelopment of thunderstorms is possible across the southern half of the state on Tuesday afternoon, and a few storms may produce wind gusts from 40-50 mph.  However, the big

articles.title: Remembering Johnny ‘The Flash’ Thornton
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Long before Johnny Thornton became known across Cullman County as “The Flash,” he was a Good Hope Raider with a love for the game, a mind built for remembering details and a voice that would one day become part of the sound of local sports.

Thornton, 64, a longtime sports journalist, broadcaster, basketball coach, official and Wallace State athletics contributor, has died, leaving behind a legacy that cannot be measured only in games called, stories written or statistics kept.

H

articles.title: Former Cullman star accepts invite to Indianapolis Colts Rookie Minicamp
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –After going undrafted in the 2026 NFL Draft, former Cullman Bearcats tight end Kaleb Heatherly accepted an invite to participate in the Indianapolis Colts’ Rookie Minicamp on May 8-9.

“The Colts had shown a lot of interest in me since I played at the Dream Bowl, and going into the weekend I was hoping I’d receive a call from Indianapolis,” Heatherly told The Tribune. He continued, “Saturday was a very long day, and I knew that was the day I’d be expecting some calls but when they

articles.title: Free Touch A Truck event returns May 2 with giveaways supporting Community Cupboard
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Cullman Electric Cooperative will host its annual Touch A Truck event on Saturday, May 2, from 9 a.m. to 2 p.m. at the Cullman County Fairgrounds. Admission is free, and this year’s event will combine family fun with an opportunity to give back.

Guests are encouraged to bring nonperishable food items or hygiene products to support Cullman Electric Cooperative’s Community Cupboard. Each item donated earns one entry into a giveaway featuring prizes such as a Cozy Coupe, water table

articles.title: Op-Ed: Alabama’s outdoors key to our state’s economic growth – Innovate Alabama is a key partner in that growth
articles.body:
From the mountains of the Tennessee Valley to the shores of the Gulf Coast, and everything in-between, our state is second to none in the country when it comes to beautiful outdoors and recreation.

Whether it’s camping and hiking across Alabama’s state parks, fishing our streams, rivers, coastlines and bays, or hunting in our state’s sprawling forests – there is truly something for everyone who has a love and appreciation for the outdoors.

But Alabama’s natural beauty is about more than just r

articles.title: Heavy Rainfall With Some Possible Severe Weather This Week
articles.body:
CLANTON – Sunday, 7:00 am, April 26, 2026

As of 630 am, a cluster of thunderstorms in central MS is moving southeastward and may produce wind gusts from 40-50 mph in far southwestern AL between 8 am and 12 pm.

An area of thunderstorms with heavy rainfall will move into the far northern portions of the state after 10 pm Monday and continue southward into Tuesday morning, weakening once reaching I-20.  A few wind gusts from 40-60 mph and very heavy rainfall are possible north of I-20.

Yet anoth

articles.title: Baptist women investigate area services, ministries
articles.author: Lanell Downs Smith
articles.body: The Alabama-Crenshaw Baptist Association hosted its spring missions meeting Monday, April 20, at Brantley Baptist Church. Beginning with a meal at 6 p.m., the service kicked off at 6:45 p.m. and featured presentations from Sav-A-Life nurse Pamela Liljeberg and Luverne's own Bailey Wilson who described services for mothers available through the nonprofit organization, Gift of Life. Wrapping up the meeting, attendees learned about volunteer opportunities through a Christmas Backpack mission trip s

articles.title: Jesus Christ and Him Crucified
articles.author: Dean Kelly
articles.body:
The Apostle Paul taught the Corinthian people and established the congregation in their city. He states that it was not words of human wisdom that he taught. He also says, “For I determined not to know anything among you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified” (I Corinthians‬ 2‬:2‬).‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬

The message of the cross is founded, first of all, in the recognition of the One True God. Hebrews 11:6 says, “But without Faith it is impossible to please God, for he who comes to God must believe that

articles.title: Let this begin with you and me
articles.author: R.A. Mathews
articles.body:
When Columbus sailed the ocean blue in 1492, he expected to hit Asia. What a surprise he had in store!

Likewise, Scripture is filled with hundreds of Messianic prophecies, yet few tell the surprise that would come. But you’ll find it in these words the angel Gabriel spoke to Daniel c.539 BC. See if you can spot the unexpected.

“From the issuing of a decree to restore and rebuild Jerusalem, until Messiah the Prince…, it will be built again, with streets and moat, even in times of distress. Then

articles.title: Alabama Arise and partners announce Alabama Workers’ Week of Action
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
Alabama Arise—in partnership with Jobs to Move America, the Alabama Coalition for Immigrant Justice and the United Campus Workers of Alabama—will host “Alabama Workers’ Week of Action” from Monday, April 27 to Friday, May 1, the organization recently announced.

The week will encompass both Workers Memorial Day (April 28) and International Workers’ Day (May 1)—an intentional choice by the organizations.

“Every year, the spring brings two holidays honoring workers. April 28 marks Workers Memoria

articles.title: Sens. Britt, Fetterman discuss operations in Iran, social media regulations
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
U.S. Senator Katie Britt, R-Alabama, joined U.S. Senator John Fetterman, D-Pennsylvania, last week for a discussion on topics including the ongoing war in Iran and bipartisan legislation aimed at curbing negative impacts of social media.

During a Fridayappearanceon an NBC News Common Ground Panel, hosted by Kristen Welker, Britt and Fetterman discussed the war, alongside youth social media use and their support for the bipartisan Stop the Scroll Act and additional proposed social media regulati

articles.title: Alabama politics shift as PAC attacks, policy gaps, voters collide
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
This week on “The Voice of Alabama Politics,” a series of developments across Alabama reveals a deeper story about power—who holds it, how it’s used and who is being left out of the process.

Questions remain about transparency and influence, especially as conflicting claims emerged about who is actually behind the campaign. At the same time, lawmakers wrapped the legislative session with a mix of meaningful but limited policy wins, leaving some of the state’s most pressing challenges unresolved

articles.title: Rep. Sewell to host shadow subcommittee hearing on voting rights
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Representative Terri A. Sewell, D-Alabama, ranking member of the Subcommittee on Elections for the Committee on House Administration, announced a shadow hearing on Monday titled “The State of Voting Rights and Elections” focused on the current state of voting rights and elections in America.

According to Sewell’s press release, the hearing will kick off a series of convenings and listening sessions the Southern Poverty Law Center will host in several cities across the country.

The hearing is s

articles.title: Gov. Ivey awards funds to south Alabama organizations assisting victims
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey announced Friday she has awarded eight grants worth about $267,000 to help fund programs that serve victims of crimes including domestic violence, sexual assault and human trafficking.

“Victims of these heinous crimes deserve a safe haven, professional care and a determined pursuit of justice,” Ivey said. “These funds will also support prevention and awareness initiatives aimed at reducing future crimes.”

The Alabama Department of Economic and Community Affairs is administeri

articles.title: Opinion | Justice and politics cannot serve the same master
articles.author: Mike Ball
articles.body:
According to scripture, Jesus said that no man can serve two masters. He will love the one and hate the other, hold to one and despise the other. We cannot simultaneously serve God and mammon.

Time and experience have proven the truth of that statement to me many times over. However, not everyone views scripture, or anything else for that matter, through the same lens. Our understanding is often cloudy and shaped by a number of factors—culture, peers and ego—that tend to narrow our perspective

Home security is no longer optional. Modern homes hold documents, cash, jewelry, and firearms. These items are hard to replace. Basic storage like drawers is not enough anymore.

The seat became open when incumbent U.S. Rep. Barry Moore (R-Enterprise) opted to run for U.S. Senate instead of seeking reelection.

articles.title: University of Alabama researchers lead sweeping flood risk study showing 17 million Americans at highest risk
articles.body:

The research, published last week inScience AdvancesandreportedbyFortuneandThe Associated Press, evaluates coastal areas stretching from Texas to Maine using a wide range of environmental and demographic data.

articles.title: Juanita J. Daily
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Juanita Johnson Daily, of Atmore, passed away on April 20, 2026 after a brief illness.

Juanita was born on July 8, 1943 in Greenville, N.C.

She moved to Atmore in the early 1960s.

After marrying Mack Dailey of Atmore, Juanita considered Atmore as her home and Mack Dailey as the love of her life. He preceded her in death.

articles.title: Column: The Five Pillars of the Reformation: Sola Fide
articles.author: Staff Reports

By Hank Walker | Pastor at Peach City Fellowship

In this continuing exploration of the Five Pillars of the Reformation, few doctrines strike at the heart of the gospel more directly than SOLA FIDE (pronounced Sola Fee-day)—justification by faith alone. This pillar addressed a central question that still echoes today: How is a sinner made right before a holy God? Is it faith PLUS works, or is it faith ALONE?

Neither team was able to hit a goal in not only regulation, but in overtime as well, and the game would be decided in a five-player penalty kick shootout. “We are excited for Dr. Stapler’s new opportunity and wish him the best,” said Cullman County Schools Superintendent Dr. Shane Barnette. “We appreciate the work he has done at Fairview and we will miss him.”

articles.title: Reimagined to celebrate re-grand opening May 2
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
Reimagined, an antique and thrift store located in the heart of Luverne, is gearing up for a re-grand opening celebration. The event is slated to take place on May 2, from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m., inviting the community in after a recent facelift.

“We’ve been closed for about nine months or so due to road construction,” said owner Kathy Smyth. “We’re back with a new facelift ready to reopen with the same fun finds and antiques we’ve had as well as adding a few new goodies and a little market section.”

articles.title: For better or worse, even at dinner
articles.author: Amy Lewis
articles.body:
Dear Amy,

My wife and I are newly married, and she has recently taken an interest in cooking. I love her enthusiasm. I truly do. The problem is… the results.

Meals are either undercooked, overcooked or somehow both. I’ve eaten things that I can't even identify. I don’t want to hurt her feelings, especially since she’s trying so hard, but I also don’t know how much longer I can pretend that this is all delicious.

How do I handle this without ruining her confidence or my stomach?

— Chewing Car

articles.title: Opinion | The fight now isn’t left vs. right. It’s forward vs. backward
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
We once believed the future would move forward.

Not perfectly. Not evenly. But forward.

That belief shaped everything that followed. It was rooted in centuries of progress, from the ideals of the Enlightenment to the hard-won expansions of liberty that followed. Each generation built—imperfectly, unevenly, but persistently—on the last, expanding who belonged and what freedom meant.

In the United States, here in Alabama, and in places around the globe, a pattern is emerging. The future is bein

The exhibit features large standing displays that tell Till’s story, plus interactive elements such as a telephone that guests can pick up to hear an eyewitness account from Wheeler Parker or a touchscreen that lets users look through photos from Till’s funeral.

articles.title: Opinion | The Democratic debates have been an embarrassment. Doug Jones should keep avoiding them
articles.author: Josh Moon
articles.body:
There was a debate last Friday night between Democratic candidates running for governor.

Don’t fret if you were unaware. Most sane people were unaware. It wasn’t worth your time or your attention.

The frontrunner for the Democratic nomination, former U.S. Senator Doug Jones, wasn’t there. He has taken some criticism for his decision to thus far decline these debate invitations (Friday’s was the second “debate” among the candidates).

He shouldn’t. Because his absence is exactly the proper mess

articles.title: Figures unveils rural bill package focused on health, land-grant universities
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
U.S. Representative Shomari Figures, D-Alabama, introduced a legislative package on Monday aimed at supporting rural healthcare and workforce development.

The “Rural Futures” package filed by Figures includes four pieces of legislation centered on nutrition assistance, rural healthcare, agriculture and veterinary education.

The package is cosponsored by Representative Terri Sewell, D-Alabama, Representative Joyce Beatty, D-Ohio, and Representative Bennie Thompson, D-Mississippi.

“I’m introduc

articles.title: UAB professors receive patent for lens that could slow myopia progression in children, young adults
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
The University of Alabama at Birmingham recently announced that two professors in its School of Optometry received a patent for a lens design that could help slow or stop the progression of myopia in children and young adults.

Myopia, or nearsightedness, is a common condition typically diagnosed before age 20. It affects about 30 percent of the global population, and experts estimate that figure could rise tohalf the world’s population by 2050. Scientists now recognize that untreated myopia can

articles.title: Jeremy Devito to host town hall in Huntsville on May 4
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Jeremy Devito, a Democratic candidate for Alabama’s 5th Congressional District, announced this week that he will host “A Town Hall for Y’all of Us” on Monday, May 4, at Shenanigans Comedy Theatre in Huntsville. The event will run from 4 p.m. to 7 p.m. and is free and open to the public.

“The town hall is built around one idea: let the people talk,” Devito said in a press release.

An open mic will allow attendees to share concerns, ask Devito questions directly and speak with their neighbors ab

articles.title: Free healthcare clinic served more than 465 patients in Montgomery
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Remote Area Medical, RAM, the nation’s largest provider of free pop-up health clinics offering dental, vision and medical care, held a free clinic in Montgomery, Alabama, on April 25-26. The clinic at the Multiplex at Cramton Bowl served more than 465 patients. The value of care exceeded $280,000.

Patients received free dental cleanings, tooth extractions, fillings, general medical exams, eye exams and glasses made on-site. RAM does not require identification or insurance, and the clinic is fre

articles.title: Lt. Gov. Ainsworth endorses Rhett Marques for Congress
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Lieutenant Governor Will Ainsworth on Monday announced his endorsement of conservative Republican Rhett Marques for Congress in Alabama’s First Congressional District, as momentum continues to build with just over three weeks until the May 19 primary election.

In avideo shared on social media, Ainsworth said, “Rhett is a MAGA Trump Republican through and through and is supported by one of the most conservative Senators in Washington, Senator Katie Britt. … He has stood tall in the Alabama House

articles.title: Alabama voters are being targeted by voices they can’t see
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
When you can’t see who’s speaking, you can’t trust what’s being said.

In Alabama’s Republican primary, voters are being hit with coordinated messages, but the source remains hidden.

In this week’s “This Matters,” Bill Britt examines a wave of attack mailers targeting Republican incumbents who supported letting voters decide on gambling, and what it reveals about how influence is operating in modern elections.

This isn’t just about one PAC or one election. It’s about a system where influence m

articles.title: In potentially competitive Huntsville-area House seat, candidates look to healthcare, education
articles.author: Anna Barrett
articles.body:
Voters in western Huntsville will choose between Democratic candidate Allison Montgomery and incumbent Rep. Phillip Rigsby, R-Monrovia, in November. A second Democrat, Damon Eubanks, suspended his campaign on April 26, 2026. (photos by Anna Barrett and Brian Lyman/Alabama Reflector; graphic by Anna Barrett)

A Huntsville-area Alabama House seat could be one of the more competitive ones in the state this fall, as voters choose between incumbent Rep. Phillip Rigsby, R-Monrovia, and presumptive Dem

articles.title: Southern Poverty Law Center hosts traveling exhibit on Emmett Till
articles.author: Ralph Chapoco
articles.body:
An exhibit dedicated to Emmett Till, a child from Chicago lynched in Mississippi in 1955, seen on display Monday at the Southern Poverty Law Center Civil Rights Memorial Center in Montgomery, Alabama. The exhibit provides details on Till's life in Chicagoin Chicago and his trip to Mississippi where he was murdered after purchasing refreshments at a convenience store. (Ralph Chapoco/Alabama Reflector)

The Southern Poverty Law Center will host an exhibit on Emmett Till, whose murder in 1955 helpe

articles.author: Ariana Figueroa

In an aerial view, a immigrant family from Haiti walks towards a gap in the U.S. border wall from Mexico on Dec. 11, 2021 in Yuma, Arizona. (Photo by John Moore/Getty Images)

WASHINGTON — The U.S. Supreme Court Wednesday will hear oral arguments on the Trump administration’s efforts to strip temporary legal status from 350,000 Haitians and 6,000 Syrians, a move that could open them up to deportation.

The case has the potential to have an impact on multiple lawsuits challenging the Trump admini

articles.author: Amanda Watford
articles.body:
Survivors and loved ones of violent crime victims march toward the Alabama Capitol building in February to urge lawmakers for more robust support and services for people who have endured incidents of violent crime. Several states, including Alabama, have considered legislation this year that would support victims of crimes in various ways. (Photo by Ralph Chapoco/Alabama Reflector)

While federal efforts to strengthen victim compensation are underway, states play a critical role in determining w

articles.title: US Supreme Court weighs how far police investigations can go in using cellphone location data
articles.author: Jonathan Shorman
articles.body:
The U.S. Supreme Court on April 9, 2026. (Photo by Ashley Murray/States Newsroom

The U.S. Supreme Court on Monday appeared likely to allow law enforcement to continue seeking warrants for the location history of cellphones near crime scenes, even as the justices wrestled with how far the government must go to protect Americans’ privacy.

Some of the justices appeared to be searching for a middle groundduring oral argumentsin a case out of Virginia challenging what is known as a geofence warrant

articles.author: Robbie Sequeira

Trees bloom in early spring outside an apartment building in the Bronx, N.Y., in 2026. New York City had the highest number of Emergency Housing Voucher recipients and is scrambling to transition them as the program sunsets. (Photo by Robbie Sequeira/Stateline)

A New York City mom and domestic violence survivor felt a flashback of fear when she received a notice in March that the emergency housing voucher she and her son have relied on since 2023 will run out soon.

articles.title: What Is the FICA Tax and How Does It Impact Your Take-Home Pay
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
FICA tax is a mandatory 7.65% payroll deduction from your paycheck that funds Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%). For 2026, the Social Security tax applies only to the first $176,100 in earnings, while Medicare has no income cap.

Knowledge of FICA calculations can assist you in making budgets that are sensible within the context of your family, negotiating wages efficiently, and generating an accuratesalary estimateto think in the long term about expenses you can reasonably afford base
