articles.title: Moody ready to bring winning culture to Selma
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
For Charles Moody, the decision to leave Southside-Selma for the head coaching vacancy at Selma High School came down to a combination or prayer, resources and a new challenge for his family.

After seven years leading the Panthers to consistent postseason success, including back-to-back Class 3A semifinal appearances, Moody officially steps into a new classification and will be on the other side of a historic crosstown rivalry.

“I think it’s just an opportunity,” Moody said. “A lot of prayer a

articles.title: Harris returns home to become the next head basketball coach
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
Johnathan Harris is coming home. The Selma High School alumnus was officially introduced Monday, April 27, as the Saints’ new head varsity basketball coach. The move marks a high-profile return to his alma mater following a prolific 10-year tenure at R.C. Hatch, where Harris established himself as one of the region’s most consistent winners.

For Harris, the appointment is more than a career milestone; it is a personal mission to revitalize the program he once represented as a student-athlete.

articles.title: Selma secures $4.2 million federal grant to eliminate residential hazards
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
The City of Selma has been awarded a $4.2 million grant from the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) to launch an aggressive initiative to eliminate lead-based paint and other health hazards in local residences.

The funding will support two primary efforts, the Lead Hazard Reduction Grant Program and the Healthy Homes Production Program. Both are designed to improve living conditions for low-income families, with a specific focus on households with senior citizens and childre

articles.title: Inside Ivey’s final months: Chief of Staff says governor focused on ‘finishing strong’
articles.body:
As Ivey’s final months in office wind down, the governor’s chief of staff said the administration is focused oncementing its policy legacywhile preparing a detailed transition for whoever takes office in January.

Liz Filmore, who has served as Ivey’s chief of staff and worked for her during her time as lieutenant governor, appeared on Capitol Journal this week to discuss the session that just wrapped and what comes next.

Filmore said the governor’s approach to the 2026 session was shaped by a

articles.title: ‘Reject anti-American agendas without apology’: Jay Mitchell announces plan targeting radical Islamic extremism as Alabama AG
articles.body:
Jay Mitchell, a former Alabama Supreme Court Justice now running for Attorney General, is putting radical Islamic extremists on notice with a new “Protect Alabama Plan” unveiled Monday.

Mitchell, a former Alabama Supreme Court Justice, outlined the plan in a press release on Monday.

The proposal includes enforcing Alabama’s prohibition against Sharia Law, designating groups such as CAIR, the Muslim Brotherhood, and Students for Justice in Palestine as domestic terror organizations, and improvi

articles.title: Dr. David Albright: Alabama’s problem isn’t funding. It’s capacity.
articles.body:
The case for local control only holds if local government can govern. In too many Alabama counties that is the part we have not built.

Somewhere in Alabama right now, a county commissioner is sitting across a table from an opportunity he cannot quite reach. The program is real. The money is real. Nobody has to explain the need. What is missing is the capacity to deliver: the staff, the systems, the know-how that the job now demands.

He will not make the news. The bridge that does not get fixed

articles.title: TVA names Mike Skaggs interim CEO as board begins search for permanent leader
articles.body:
TheTennessee Valley Authority(TVA) has named longtime executive Mike Skaggs as interim President and CEO. The TVA Board of Directors announced the pick Friday and said Skaggs will run the agency while they search for a permanent CEO.

Skaggs brings more than 40 years of experience in the utility industry, including over 25 years with TVA, where he most recently served as Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer from 2018 to 2021.

“Mike was a leader at TVA for more than two decades a

Alabama Governor Kay Ivey thinks that late night ABC host Jimmy Kimmel should be fired for his recent comments about President Donald Trump.

Kimmel has received backlash afterdoing a skit on his showwhere he hosted a fake White House Correspondents Dinner speech and said, “Mrs. Trump, you have a glow like an expectant widow.”

articles.body:
Alabama gamblingenforcement will require stronger legal tools and additional investigators if the state expects the Attorney General’s office to police illegal operations statewide, Republican candidate Katherine Robertson told “Capitol Journal” recently.

articles.title: Alabama marks 15 years since devastating April 27, 2011 tornadoes
articles.body:
Fifteen years after one of the deadliest natural disasters in state history, Alabama is pausing to remember the April 27, 2011, Alabamatornadoesthat claimed about 253 lives and forever changed communities across the state.

For many Alabamians, the events of that day remain vivid — a moment when ordinary routines gave way to a statewide emergency unfolding in real time.

On that spring day, Alabama was struck by 62 tornadoes — nearly matching the state’s typical annual total of 65. The storms ca

articles.title: RESULTS: Tuberville, Hudson, Allen, Mitchell, and legislative incumbents claim victory in Jefferson County straw poll
articles.body:
The Jefferson County Republican Party held its straw poll breakfast Saturday at the Gardendale Civic Center.

This year, it drew roughly 200 of some of the most engaged GOP voters for the long-running tradition that gives Republican primary candidates an early read on Alabama’s most populous county ahead of the May 19 ballot.

For a $25 admission, attendees received breakfast, a ballot covering races up and down the ticket from the U.S. Senate to local legislative seats, and facetime with just a

articles.body:
The Charles Henderson Trojans (18-4-3) saw their season come to an end in a 2-1 loss to the Faith Academy Rams (18-4-2) in the second round of the Class 5A State Soccer Playoffs in Mobile on Monday.

Charles Henderson’s lone goal came from Daniel Frigge, assisted by Robert Lindsey. Despite the loss, and elimination from the playoffs, head coach Josh Wildermuth was proud of his Trojans for the way they competed this season.

articles.title: TPD promotes three officers to sergeant
articles.author: Huck Treadwell
articles.body:
The Troy Police Department held a promotion ceremony on Monday to recognize and pin three officers who were promoted to the rank of sergeant.

Troy Police Chief Danny Barron promoted Justin Baker, Addison Hand and Cody Henderson to the rank of sergeant. Baker was hired by the TPD in 2018 and is a field training officer, spike strip instructor and survival training instructor. Hand and Henderson are both field training officers.

“You've been promoted today, and that's a great milestone,” Barron

articles.title: TB&T announces promotions of Doug Cowart and Isha Tarver
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
On Monday, Troy Bank & Trust announced the promotion of both Doug Cowart and Isha Tarver.

Cowart joined TB&T in May of 2024 as the City President for Dothan and will now serve as Market President for the Wiregrass area, overseeing the markets of Dothan, Elba, Enterprise and New Brockton.

Cowart has more than 20 years of experience in banking, including 13 years specializing in commercial and consumer lending.

articles.title: Troy Hoops adds first transfer of Adam Howard era
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
Troy University men’s basketball coach Adam Howard announced the addition of former Old Dominion guard Robert Davis Jr. to the roster.

Davis is Troy’s first transfer addition since Howard was hired. The 6-foot-6-inch Detroit, Mich., native scored more than 1,500 career points in high school and was a Prep All-American. He originally was an All-State athlete at Led Hamtramck High School, leading his school to a state championship and averaging 22 points as a junior. He finished his prep career a

articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
On Monday, the Troy City Council unanimously approved a request to enter into a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) between the Troy Police Department and Enterprise Police Department to assist in the search being conducted for a missing child that is believed to be deceased.

The MOU would allow law enforcement officers from TPD to assist EPD in the search for Genesis Reid, a missing Enterprise child who is believed to have been murdered earlier this year.

The Enterprise Police Department began

articles.title: Stallions look for answers amid losing streak
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
Coming into the 2026 season, the Birmingham Stallions had lost seven combined games total in the past four seasons. The Stallions have now lost four straight this season alone.

After opening the season with a win over Louisville back on March 27, Birmingham has lost four straight to Houston, St. Louis, Orlando and DC. While the losses to St. Louis and Houston were close, the past two games have not been remotely close. The Stallions were shutout for the first time in franchise history against O

articles.title: Trojans edge Marshall in conference series
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Troy Trojans (22-22, 12-9) earned their fourth straight Sun Belt Conference baseball series win this weekend, taking 2-of-3 games from the Marshall Thundering Herd (19-23, 10-11) on the road.

The series win gives Troy sole possession of fourth place in the conference, behind Coastal Carolina, Southern Miss and Appalachian State. It also marked Troy’s fourth straight series win. The Sunday win also pushed Troy back to an even .500 on the season. The series didn’t get off to a great start for

articles.title: Troy walks off ULM to avoid series sweep
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
The Troy Trojans (31-19, 9-12) managed to avoid its second Sun Belt Conference series sweep of the season with a walk-off win over the first-place ULM Warhawks (34-19, 16-5) in game three on Sunday.

Troy and ULM went into extra innings in two of the three series games this weekend. ULM currently sits tied for first place in the conference with Marshall, while Troy is in eighth place. ULM took a 1-0 series lead on April 24 with a 4-2 win in extra innings. With the game tied 2-2 in the 10th innin

articles.title: Conner, Lafayette sign rookie free agent contracts
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
While no Troy Trojans were drafted in this year’s NFL Draft, defensive back Devin Lafayette and tight end Ethan Conner were both signed as undrafted free agents following the draft.

Lafayette signed with the Las Vegas Raiders. Lafayette, a 6-foot-2-inch, Georgia native, was a star player at Brunswick High School before beginning his college career at Northern Illinois. He was All-MAC as a true freshman in 2020 but suffered a season-ending injury in the season opener in 2021. In 2022, he returne

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
On April 24, Alabama Gov. Kay Ivey announced that she has awarded $267,000 in grants to help fund programs that serve victims of crimes including domestic violence, sexual assault and human traffic; this includes The House of Ruth, which supports Pike County.

The Alabama Dept. of Economic and Community Affairs (ADECA) is administering the grants made available by the U.S. Dept. of Justice.

articles.body:
Bass Agency Real Estate celebrated 80 years in business with a beautiful reception at their location in Andalusia on Thursday, April 23. Droves of local friends, customers, and city officials joined with the Bass Agency family for an afternoon of honoring the eight decade legacy the real estate company has developed.

Charles Bass Jr. incorporated Bass Agency in 1983 when his two children took on management positions. After selling the insurance side of the company, establishing multiple locatio

Two time Pulitzer Prize winner John Archibald joined Andalusia Public Library on April 23 at its event celebrating the Love of Libraries.

The event began with a welcome from APL’s Director Sara Mixon while guests enjoyed their lunch. She reminded attendees of the humble beginnings of the local library, and how it has transformed from “a meager, donated 130 books and a borrowed space” into the bustling hub of the community APL is today. articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
“Potholes... your days are numbered,” claimed a City of Opp social media post on April 2 that introduced a new service to Opp citizens: The Pothole Patrol. This initiative was started by Opp Mayor Chance McCurley in an effort to bring organization, accountability, and transparency to the process of maintaining Opp’s roads.

Dugrid deems CO2 storage safe for Covington County

An engineer with more than two decades of experience in carbon capture and storage (CCS) says the proposed Pine Hills Storage Hub is supported by strong geology and would be subject to the strictest underground safety regulations in the country.

articles.body:
Steve Roanell Dukes

Steve Roanell Dukes, age 83, passed away Saturday, April 25, 2026 at his residence in Pensacola, Florida.

Mr. Dukes was born July 29, 1942, in Columbus, Georgia, to the late Joseph Eugene Dukes and Ruby Lee Edwards. He had made his home in Pensacola, Florida since 1969 and was a member of Burgess Road Baptist Church.

Cherine Schofield

After 77 years of a life animated by love, tenderness, joy, curiosity and compassion, Cherine (Cherie) Bruce Schofield joined our Lord in heaven on Tuesday, April 21st, while surrounded by loving family and friends.  Cherie was born in Hutchinson, KS but spent most of her life in Baton Rouge, LA.

articles.title: ALDOT: Tennessee River bridge project close to completion
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
After two years of construction work, traffic flow will soon return to normal along the busy stretch of Interstate 65 that bridges Limestone and Morgan Counties where the Tennessee River divides them.

The Alabama Department of Transportation (ALDOT) last week announced that only a final few steps remain in an extended bridge rehabilitation project that has made the area an active work zone for the past two years.

articles.title: Nick Looney named new principal at Athens Elementary
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
The board of education for Athens City Schools has appointed Nick Looney as the incoming next principal of Athens Elementary School.

Looney, who enters the position after having served as the school’s assistant principal since 2021, was named the new Athens Elementary principal at the board’s April 23 meeting.

articles.title: Northview softball tops Baker to reach Class Rural District 1 final against Jay
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
UPDATE: This article was updated to include the correct time for the district championship on Thursday.

Northview’s Kylee Langham went a perfect 4-for-4 with five RBIs to help lead the Lady Chiefs to a 14-1 victory over Baker, Fla. in the semifinals of the Class Rural, District 1 tournament Tuesday night in Jay.

Langham also scored four runs for NHS in the winning effort.

With the victory, the Lady Chiefs improve to 17-5-1, and advance to play Jay in the district championship on Thurs., April

articles.title: Here is the schedule for Atmore Mayfest 2026 at Tom Byrne Park
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
It’s Mayfest time.

The annual event, which is sponsored by the Atmore Area Chamber of Commerce, is going to be held this Sat., May 2, 2026 at Tom Byrne Park.

Vendors will be set up around most of the walking path within the park.

Opening ceremonies will begin at 9 a.m. with Executive Director Addison Long speaking, a presentation of colors by the Boy Scout Troop No. 26 and a prayer by Chris McGhee.

The annual Beautiful Baby Contest will be held from 10 a.m. until 11:30 a.m. All baby contesta

articles.title: Michael R. Caraway
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Michael Richard “Mike” Caraway, 53, of Canoe, passed away on April 17, 2026, in Atmore.

He was born on December 27, 1972, to Collin “Pete” Caraway and Mary “Janie” Caraway.

He was preceded in death by a daughter, Maggie Pearl Caraway.

Mike is survived by his parents; his daughter, Girdielene Ella Wade Caraway; his sisters, Vicky (Jerry) Timothy and Sandra Caraway (David) Brown; his brother, William (Debbie) Caraway; and numerous nieces and nephews.

Private funeral services were held on April

articles.title: ‘My Thing Was That You Know When You Know … I Had No Doubt About It’
articles.body:
Jo & Charles Chambers

By Mia Watkins |The Birmingham Times

Live:Vestavia Hills

Married:August 10, 1991

Met:Jo and Charles Chambers either met in the 1960s or the 1990s, depending on who you ask.

“He says we met when we were in college.” Jo Chambers said. “I don’t remember meeting him. I remember his roommates, but I don’t remember meeting him. He says he saw me, but I don’t ever remember being introduced.”

Charles Chambers, an Auburn student at the time, was doing a co-op program with Bell

articles.title: Birmingham City Council Delays Vote Until June on Data Center Regulations
articles.body:
The Birmingham Times

Rules for future data center development in Birmingham are still being worked out, as city council members delayed a vote on updates to the city’s zoning ordinance.

After hearing from nearly two dozen concerned citizens during a public hearing on Tuesday, the Birmingham City Council voted unanimously to postpone a decision on new data center regulations. The proposed guidelines were presented by zoning and planning staff during the meeting. The delay will give officials mo

articles.title: Foot Soldiers’ 63rd Anniversary Celebration Set For Saturday in Birmingham’s Historic 4th Avenue District
articles.body:
Special to The Times

The Civil Rights Activist Committee is hosting the Foot Soldiers’ 63rd anniversary celebration from 10 a.m. to 7 p.m. on Saturday, May 2, in Birmingham’s Historic 4th Avenue District.

This year’s theme, “Honoring the Legacy – Uniting the Neighborhoods – Inspiring the Nation,” commemorates the 63rd anniversary of the Birmingham Civil Rights Movement of 1963, including the historic Children’s March. This celebration honors the courageous young people who marched for justice

articles.title: Speak Loud, Win Big: $500 on the Line at BPL’s Bards & Brews Poetry Slam on May 1
articles.body:
By Roy L. Williams |Birmingham Public Library

Birmingham Public Library (BPL) invites the community to experience some of the metro area’s top spoken word artists competing for a combined $500 in a Bards & Brews Poetry Slam happening Friday, May 1 at the Central Library downtown.

This will be the second slam of the year hosted by Bards & Brews, BPL’s high-energy monthly spoken word poetry series where poets express their voices on stage.

Ten poets will go head-to-head in a fast-paced, three-r

articles.title: E-edition for April 29, 2026
articles.body: Find out the latest news and information about upcoming events right here at home in this week's edition of The Brewton Standard.

articles.title: TRM Class of ‘74 luncheon honors Cottrells with gift
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
A regular monthly gathering of the T.R. Miller High School Class of 1974, turned into a special meeting to honor Danny and Karen Cottrell.

Cottrell, a member of the class of ‘74, was honored for the generosity of him and his wife by classmates last week when a donation was made in their name to Camille Place.

“We’re very honored,” Danny Cottrell said. “Someone who organized this asked the classmates what they could do for us. They were told that the best gift to us would be to give something t

articles.title: ECRC welding instructor surprised with $30K gift
articles.author: Lisa Tindell
articles.body:
Marty Long got a big surprise last week when he was presented with $30,000 to enhance the welding program he teaches at the Escambia Career Readiness Center.

Long was one of just three winners across Alabama in the grant competition presented by Oak Ridge Associated Universities (ORAU).

ORAU launched the Extreme Trades Makeover: Future Welders competition in mid-October to award three Alabama welding teachers with $30,000 in new equipment or upgrades for their workshops or classrooms.

Teacher

articles.title: Maplesville, Thorsby baseball advances following large Chilton contingency in first round
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
It was a historical postseason for Chilton County area high school baseball teams with all seven teams advancing to their respective state tournaments. Teams won or finished runner-up in their areas to advance to the postseason.

Two of the seven schools in action on April 22-24 advanced to the second round of the state playoffs — Maplesville and Thorsby High Schools.

Maplesville beat Keith High School on April 23 in straight games, taking game one 16-0 and game two 15-0. Aidan Abbott was 3-for

articles.title: Column: No fast food, no alcohol
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
At the beginning of this year, I decided to give up fast food until my birthday on April 27, to see how much weight I would lose and to see how much money I would save. Shortly after, my brother passed away, I decided it would be wise to also no longer drink, to avoid the numbing aspects of it that could turn me into an alcoholic. I also thought it would be interesting to see how much money I would save doing that.

The weight aspect of this was interesting because by making these two lifestyle

articles.title: Unemployment info out for Feb., continuing catch up from government shutdown
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
After a delay in unemployment information from the Alabama Department of Workforce due to a small government shutdown, information for February was released earlier in April.

Inside that information showed Chilton County’s unemployment rate was 2.8% in February, up from 2.5% in January, but down from 3% in February 2025. Those rates represent 577 unemployed persons in Chilton County in February, 514 unemployed persons in January and 614 unemployed persons in February 2025.

The number of people

articles.title: Clanton Farmers Market returning to Corner Park in 2026
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
From The City of Clanton

Clanton, AL— The City of Clanton is pleased to announce the return of the Clanton Farmers Market for the 2026 season, welcoming residents and visitors each Monday from May through August to downtown Corner Park.

The market will operate weekly from 5 p.m. to 7 p.m., offering a family-friendly atmosphere and a wide selection of locally grown produce, handmade goods and community-based vendors. The seasonal market serves as a gathering place that supports local farmers, a

articles.title: Revenue Commissioner candidates offer similar perspectives in GOP forum
articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
Cullman County Revenue Commissioner candidates Kenneth Cornelius and Mike Pitts offered similar perspectives on their vision for the office during a GOP-hosted forum on Thursday, April 23.

Both candidates have decades of business ownership experience: Cornelius, specializing in business and building development, largely focused on a successful chain of Xpress lube locations, and Pitts, who grew the Hanceville-based screen-printing business Creative Design after inheriting it from his father.

B

articles.title: CCSO addresses alleged assault in Cold Springs involving minors
articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
Cullman County Sheriff Matt Gentry released a statement on a widely discussed, alleged assault that occurred weeks ago involving minors within the Cold Springs area.

In the video statement, Gentry confirmed the incident was currently under investigation and that law enforcement has been working closely with the victim’s family and the Cold Springs community. However, due to the age of those involved, was limited on how much information he was allowed to disclose.

“Due to state law, and the fac

articles.title: Cold Springs 1936 service club receives recognition
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
Cold Springs High School was recognized on Monday, April 27, for its service-oriented organization, 1936 Club.

The Council for Leaders in Alabama Schools (CLAS) recognized 32 Schools of Distinction across the eight State School Board Districts and announced the 2025 CLAS Banner Schools, during an awards luncheon in Prattville, Alabama. The CLAS Banner School program was created in 2001 to recognize schools in Alabama that showcase outstanding programs and service to students.

Each school was n

articles.title: FHS principal to resign at end of school year
articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
Fairview High School Principal Brad Stapler has accepted a position with the Guntersville City School system and will be resigning at the end of the current school year.

Before accepting the position at Fairview High School at the end of the 2024 school year, Stapler served as the Arab High School assistant principal and career tech director. Stapler previously worked in the Morgan County school system as principal of both Brewer High School and Cotaco School.

“We are excited for Dr. Stapler’s

articles.title: Sheriff candidates weigh in during GOP forum
articles.author: Patrick Camp
articles.body:
Voters gained further insight into the two Cullman County Sheriff candidates — Tim Creel and Chad Whaley — during a forum hosted by the local Republican party on Thursday, April 23.

Creel brings more than 38 years of law enforcement experience to the table, 18 of which were spent at the Cullman County Sheriff’s Office, in addition to 20 years of overlapping military service. Most of Creel’s time in law enforcement was spent as a major crimes and narcotics investigator. After returning from a tw

articles.title: PREP FOOTBALL: Fairview’s Zayden Cruce signs scholarship offer to attend Huntingdon College
articles.body:
FAIRVIEW, Ala. –At Fairview High School this morning, Zayden Cruce signed his scholarship offer to play football at Huntingdon College in Montgomery.

After a breakout junior season in 2024, a leg injury threatened not only his senior season but his career. Cruce fought back, however, playing in the final four games of the Aggies’ 12-1 2025 season.

“‘Z’ fought his way back to the Good Hope game, the Arab game and the Jacksonville game, but I can’t help but get chill bumps when I watch the Scott

articles.title: Arley man dies after Cullman County wreck
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –An Arley man died following a two-vehicle crash that occurred at approximately 5:20 p.m. Thursday, April 23. Alabama State Troopers identified the victim as  Arlen L. Langley, 86.

Troopers said Langley was critically injured when the 2010 Chrysler 300 Touring he was driving stuck a 2025 Nissan Sentra driven by Christina R. Campbell, 37, of Cullman.

After the initial impact, they said, the Nissan left the roadway, struck a cable box and a fence. The Chrysler also left the roadway

articles.title: State of Education: Students, teachers honored
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Multiple students and educators were honored at the 2026 State of Education Breakfast community breakfast hosted by the Cullman Area Chamber of Commerce on Friday, April 24.

“We were honored to host our recent State of Education Community Breakfast, bringing together educators, students, and community leaders to celebrate the impact of education in Cullman,” the Chamber said in a statement. “We were proud to award scholarships to outstanding students and honor teachers for their

articles.title: Severe Weather Threat Update
articles.body:
CLANTON – Tuesday, 1:00 pm, April 28, 2026

Storms near and south of I-20 will continue to redevelop this afternoon with very heavy rainfall, a few wind gusts from 40-60 mph, and hail up to quarter size being the main threats.

A Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) or large cluster of storms in Arkansas and northern Louisiana at 12:30 pm will reach western Alabama late this afternoon or evening and move eastward through early Wednesday morning.  Damaging wind gusts, hail, and very heavy rainfall a

articles.title: Obituary: Sammie Audrey Jones
articles.body:
Sammie Audrey Jones, age 72, passed away on April 24, 2026. Born on February 7, 1954, in Cullman, Alabama, Sammie lived in Hanceville, Alabama.

Sammie served in the Navy as well as a carpenter, bringing steady hands and a practical dedication to the work entrusted to him. His life reflected a quiet reliability—an ability to build, repair, and provide in ways that were both tangible and lasting.

Away from work, Sammie found deep enjoyment in the outdoors and in nature, especially while fishing.

articles.title: Obituary: Allyson Helton Yoder
articles.body: Funeral service for Mrs. Allyson Helton Yoder of Cullman, is Thursday, April 30, 2026, at 1 p.m. at West Point Baptist Church, with Rev. Samuel Tucker officiating. Visitation will be held prior to the service at the church from 12 p.m. to 1 p.m. Mrs. Yoder will be laid to rest at Cullman City Cemetery.Allyson left us too soon on Monday, April 27,2026, at her residence surrounded by her loving family.Serving as pallbearers are Chuck Shikle, Carson Sanders, Jamie Braswell, Gary Braswell, Jeffery B

articles.body: Roger Dale Holmes, age 74 of Hanceville, passed away on Sunday, April 26, 2026, at his residence. articles.body:
CLANTON – Tuesday, 6:30 am, April 28, 2026

There are two separate threats of severe weather today into early Wednesday.   The first is due to the current thunderstorm activity near and north of I-20 as of 530 am.  These storms will produce a boundary somewhere near or south of I-20 by late morning.  Additional storm development is likely into the afternoon with a few storms producing wind gusts from 40-60 mph and hail.

articles.title: Hanceville students to host America’s 250th celebration Wednesday
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– Hanceville students will mark America’s 250th birthday Wednesday with a community celebration centered on patriotism, student performances and recognition of veterans and active military members.

The America’s Semiquincentennial Celebration is scheduled for 9-10:30 a.m. Wednesday, April 29, in the Hanceville High School gym.

The event is being hosted by Hanceville’s fifth-grade class and Hanceville Middle School.

“Our theme for this event is ‘What does America mean to me,’”

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Andrah P'Lonas: If God Wrote His Law on Every Heart, Would We Still Need a Constitution?
articles.author: Andrah P'Lonas
articles.body:
The prophet Book of Jeremiah wrote that God would raise up a people and write His law on their hearts and in their minds. He described a time when people would no longer have to say, “Know the Lord,” because they would already know Him. That paints a powerful picture of a world where what is right is not simply taught, but lived naturally from the inside out.

That leads to an important question. If every human on earth suddenly had God’s law written within them, guiding their thoughts, their wo

articles.title: Masonic Lodge hosts successful Jeep Fest
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
Classic car shows have been popular for some time. Much like the cars themselves, these shows evoke a sense of nostalgia, connecting people with the past and allowing them to relive the era when these vehicles dominated the roads.

Newer events such as Touch-a-Truck programs and Jeep Fests are becoming more popular these days.

The Touch-a-Truck gives youngsters a chance to be up close and to actually touch the bigger vehicles on the road such as fire trucks, ambulances, utility vehicles and mon

articles.title: Springwood softball advances to state tournament
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Springwood softball just got restarted this season after not having enough players to field a team last season and they’re already making impressive strides as a young team. The Lady Wildcats boast a 11-9 record and are headed to the state tournament, which kicks off on Thursday.

“I think it's been a fairly successful season, no matter what really happens in the state playoffs. We've restarted the program, and our growth has improved tremendously. Lots of pluses there,” said Thomas Hill who coa

articles.title: Carey signs to play soccer at Point
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Springwood senior Cameron Carey was surrounded by family, teammates, Wildcat coaches and future coaches on Monday as he signed to play soccer for Point University.

Carey shined on the field for Springwood during his high school career, helping to lead them to the 2A state championship this season. Carey credits Springwood for helping him love soccer and wanting to try harder at the sport than what he would have done playing for a rec league, making it possible for him to reach the collegiate le

articles.title: Food Truck Sundays return to West Point
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
This summer, the Downtown West Point Development Authority is bringing back Food Truck Sundays to West Point’s River Park Pavilion and Splash Pad, located adjacent to West Point City Hall at 730 1st Ave. Food Truck Sundays will be held from 11:30 a.m. to 6 p.m. on May 31, June 7, June 21, June 28, July 19, July 26, Aug. 2, Aug. 16, Aug. 23 and Aug. 30.

Food Truck Sundays is designed to act as a food truck court for the River Park and downtown West Point for residents, families enjoying the park

articles.title: Live concert comes to Point University
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
Point University’s music program will present a free community concert, “Music for the Soul,” featuring uplifting selections designed to take listeners on an emotional journey. The concert will be held at the Scotts Fine Arts Center on Point’s West Point campus (413 West 10th St.).

The concert will feature performances by Point’s three ensembles: the West and Tenth house band, the Point Singers and the Point Percussion Ensemble. The groups have spent the semester preparing selections — includin

articles.title: Springwood’s Smith named AISA Elementary TOTY
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Springwood kindergarten teacher Allison Smith has been named the Class A Elementary Teacher of the Year for the Alabama Independent School Association (AISA). She was previously named one of Springwood’s Teachers of the Year, representing the Lower School.

“Mrs. Smith brings a wealth of experience, compassion and love to school each day and consistently delivers instruction that allows her students to achieve at a very high level. Springwood School is blessed to have her on staff,” said Head of

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Kristy McCormally: Store-Bought Mashed Potatoes and Other Acts of Courage
articles.author: Kristy McCormally
articles.body:
Letters from the Truitt House

— — —

It is 6:30 on a Friday night and I am hissing at my husband over the whereabouts of the lemon zester.

The guests are coming at seven. There is a roast in the oven, a beurre blanc on the stove, rolls rising under a linen towel, two sauces I swore to myself I would make the day before, and a centerpiece I am now, somehow, also arranging. I have been in this kitchen for six hours. Brian has asked me a perfectly reasonable question about the ice and I have resp

articles.title: Chambley talks passion for turkey hunting
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
Although the wild turkey population in Alabama has been declining some in recent years, this popular game bird is still found in all 67 counties and its total population is thought to exceed 300,000.

Steve Chambley has hunted wild turkeys in Alabama for 55 years now. He bagged his first one when he was 19 and has brought home many more since then. He’s become quite an expert over the years of calling turkeys from many types of calls he’s made. He talked about his love of turkey hunting and the

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Michael Brooks: When Congregational Surveys Give Mixed Results
articles.author: Michael J. Brooks
articles.body:
It’s common for congregations to administer opinion surveys occasionally. But I’ve seen mixed results.

Baptist denominational officials report that congregations who take surveys when searching for a new pastor discover members want a 35-year old with a seminary degree. Thus he is educated, not too old, not too young, but just right.

A retired minister told me about his displeasure with their new young pastor.

“He won’t wear a suit, and he won’t use a pulpit,” he fumed, so there is that.

I i

articles.title: First Responders Dinner returns May 7
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
The second annual Law Enforcement and First Responder appreciation and awards supper is to take place on May 7, from 6 to 8 p.m. at the Tom Harbin Ag Center in Luverne. The event is sponsored by Judge Jon Folmar and the Children’s Policy Council.

"May is Law Enforcement and First Responder Appreciation Month and Law Enforcement Memorial Month,” Folmar said. “It has been a passion of mine for many years to recognize our outstanding law enforcement officers and first responders of which I was par

articles.title: This week in history: 2017
articles.author: Amy Lewis
articles.body:
A look back at pages from the Luverne Journal this week in 2017 reflects a community balancing long-term planning with hands-on local action. The stories this week include plans for a new vocational program and a competition to clean up Crenshaw County.

Board weighs vocational partnership proposal

The Crenshaw County Board of Education considered a proposal from county commissioners to lease a board-owned facility for a new vocational program but opted to seek more information before making a

articles.title: SPLC says false public statements may have tainted indictment
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
The Southern Poverty Law Center has filed two motions in federal court challenging both the basis of a recent grand jury indictment and public statements made by senior federal officials about the organization’s work.

The filings come days after the Department of Justice announced an indictment against the SPLC, charging it with six counts of wire fraud, four counts of bank fraud, and one count of conspiracy to commit money laundering. In media appearances, acting U.S. Attorney General Todd Bla

articles.title: Birmingham City Council considers, delays vote on data center ordinance
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
The Birmingham City Council heard statements for and against a proposed ordinance to regulate data centers during its Tuesday meeting.

Themeetingsaw city officials explain the draft ordinance and community members speak both for and in criticism of the proposed regulations.

Although the ordinance was scheduled for a vote during the meeting, the council ultimately opted to delay a vote on whether to adopt the regulations to consider potential amendments based on public comments heard during the

articles.title: Birmingham awards $200,000 in microgrants for violence prevention, youth intervention programs
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
The city of Birmingham recently awarded $200,000 in microgrants to 14 local organizations to expand violence prevention and youth intervention programs across the city.

The funds come from the city’s 2023 U.S. Department of Justice Community Violence Intervention and Prevention Initiative grant. They will support wraparound services connected to the Jefferson County Family Resource Center and its RESTORE program, which stands for Reduce, Educate, Support, Train, Organize, Realize and Empower.

articles.title: Opinion | North Alabama deserves better than an $85 billion rail merger
articles.author: Ben Harrison
articles.body:
Limestone and Lauderdale counties are home to limestone-derived soils just north of the Tennessee River. Row crops like soybeans, cotton and corn flourish in the red, clayey soils of the valleys, while the gravelly composition of the uplands is conducive to significant poultry and egg production.

Alabama’s competitive rail system provides agricultural laborers with cost-effective choices to move goods to the Port of Mobile, which is a gateway to participation in the global commodity market. How

articles.title: Alabama Department of Corrections Commissioner John Hamm retires
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Tuesday announced the retirement of Alabama Department of Corrections Commissioner John Hamm, saying he is leaving “following his successful and transformative tenure,” according to a news release.

Ivey also said she is appointing corrections and law enforcement veteran Greg Lovelace to lead the department through the remainder of the quadrennium.

“Running the Department of Corrections in any state is a heavy task, and Commissioner John Hamm has been exceptional for Alabam

articles.title: Gov. Ivey issues flag lowering directive for fallen officer Dallas Hinton
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
I hereby authorize flags be flown at half-staff on Wednesday, April 29, 2026, within the State Capitol Complex in Montgomery and across the state of Alabama, to honor fallen officerDallas Hinton of the Thomasville Police Department who gave his life in the line of duty, on Wednesday, April 22, 2026. Officer Hinton was 25 years old.

A native of Tuscaloosa and a resident of Thomasville, Dallas Hinton proudly served his country in the United States Marine Corps in Japan and Afghanistan. A two-year

articles.title: Alabama Realtors PAC announces 2026 House, Senate endorsements
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The Alabama Realtors Political Action Committee, ARPAC, the political arm of the Alabama Association of Realtors, announced endorsements this week for House and Senate seats in the Republican and Democratic primaries scheduled for May 19.

“The endorsed candidates are deeply connected to their districts and understand how decisions made in the Legislature impact Alabama property owners, families and communities,” ARPAC Board of Trustees Chair Sherri McCollum said.

“Candidates earn ARPAC support

articles.title: Gov. Ivey awards funding for programs assisting victims of domestic violence, sexual assault
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Tuesday awarded 15 grants totaling about $673,000 to nonprofit organizations and county governments in central Alabama to assist victims of domestic violence, sexual assault, human trafficking and other crimes.

“These funds will support programs that provide critical services to crime victims and help investigate and prosecute offenders,” Ivey said. “I am pleased to award these grants that will help victims recover and take important steps toward rebuilding their lives.”

T

articles.title: New grant program to support Montgomery’s small businesses
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The city of Montgomery said this week that it and Montgomery County will soon launch the Montgomery Thriving Small Business Grant Program, funded through the American Rescue Plan Act, to support small businesses across the region.

The program will help local businesses expand, stabilize and strengthen daily operations, with funding aimed at promoting economic growth and supporting long-term success.

Business owners can sign up for updatesonline.

The program website is scheduled to launch May

articles.title: Opinion | Very few open or contested state House races
articles.author: Steve Flowers
articles.body: Like the Alabama State Senate, the State House of Representatives will have very little turnover. Our state legislative seats are becoming analogous to congressional seats when it comes to incumbency. Over 80 percent of the legislature is unopposed. The partisanship makeup and faces will be pretty much unchanged. The House will have a supermajority Republican complexion. It will remain 75 percent Republican. There will be a strong continuity of leadership in the House of Representatives between

articles.body:
Rep. Chris England, D-Tuscaloosa, reading legislation in the House Ethics and Campaign Finance Committee on March 11, 2026, in the Alabama Statehouse in Montgomery, Alabama. England earlier this week blamed the Alabama Department of Corrections for killing a bill that would have allowed for parole applicants, victims and law enforcement to address the parole board virtually. Hamm announced his retirement Tuesday. He will be replaced by Greg Lovelace as commissioner of the Alabama Department of Corrections. (Brian Lyman/Alabama Reflector)

articles.author: Robbie Sequeira
articles.body:

A Trump administration proposal would end gender identity protections for people in federally funded housing and shelters.

articles.author: Jonathan Shorman
articles.body:
James Comey speaks onstage at 92NY on May 30, 2023 in New York City. (Photo by Dia Dipasupil/Getty Images)

The U.S. Department of Justice on Tuesday obtained a second grand jury indictment of former FBI Director James Comey, long a target of President Donald Trump’s anger for overseeing an investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 election.

articles.title: GOP candidates revive anti-Islam attacks as midterms approach
articles.author: Anna Claire Vollers

articles.title: Kids Talk About God | What tempts you to deny Jesus?

OPINION —

“If someone offered me a lifetime supply of chocolate ice cream to say I didn’t believe in Jesus, I might hesitate,” says Jackson, 9. “But then I’d remember that chocolate melts, and Jesus lasts forever.”

In John 18:12-27, we read how Peter, Jesus’ boldest follower, denied three times that he even knew Jesus. Only hours earlier, Peter had promised to die with Jesus. But when things got scary, Peter pretended he didn’t know him. It’s easy to shake our heads at Peter, but we face the s

You are 94 and your days are numbered. Does that not trouble you? No, that does not bother me. Yes, my days are numbered, but they are numbered by the Lord, and He has given me peace about his plan for me.

Have you ever had a moment in your life when something finally just “clicked?” Maybe it was something you had heard before. articles.title: Sean of the South | The room full of adolescence

I was in a hotel with a few hundred Mennonites.

I walked into the hotel at noon. At first, I was confused inasmuch as the lobby was full of cape dresses, plain suits and broad-brimmed hats. Some of the older men had beards, some were clean-shaven. The women wore head coverings.

I thought maybe I’d taken a wrong turn on the interstate.

I approached the hotel desk. “I feel a little underdressed,” I said.

articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Pike County Sheriff Russell Thomas said a Union Springs teen accused of shooting a man over the weekend turned himself in

According to Thomas, Artavious Darnell Eldridge, 18, was being sought on a first-degree assault charge in Union Springs. articles.title: Troy’s Hayden Smith named National Pitcher of the Week
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
On Tuesday, Troy junior pitcher Hayden Smith was named both Sun Belt Pitcher of the Week and the College Baseball Foundation (CBF) National Pitcher of the Week.

Smith is the third Trojan to earn both a national and conference weekly award. Jimmy Janicki and Aaron Piasecki have both been named Sun Belt Player of the Week earlier this week, while Drew Nelson and Janicki have earned national player of the week honors.

Smith threw five hitless innings against Jacksonville State last week and struc

articles.title: Softball: CHHS celebrates Senior Night with shutout win
articles.author: Josh Boutwell
articles.body:
As area softball tournaments draw near, a number of local teams picked up wins over the weekend.

The Charles Henderson Lady Trojans (12-16) celebrated Senior Night on Monday with a 19-0 win over Abbeville in three innings. The Lady Trojans wasted no time, jumping out to a 15-0 lead in the first inning.

Senior Isabella Turner stole four bases and scored three runs, while Keirstin Gamble scored three runs and Erika Young scored two. Katy Lynn Lee also stole three bases and scored three bases. Sa

articles.title: Andalusia Elementary School sets virtual learning day and moves field day
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
Attention Andalusia Elementary School's Students and Parents:

Due to the transfer of utilities to the new addition at Andalusia Elementary School, elementary students will not attend in-person school on Friday, May 8, 2026. Instead, Andalusia Elementary School only will observe a virtual learning day on that date.

Additionally, in order to accommodate this schedule change, Field Day has been moved to May 6 for grades 3rd - 6th and May 7 for grades Pre-K- 2nd.

articles.title: ALEA debuts low-cost ‘Kayaking 101’ class for beginners
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
The Alabama Law Enforcement Agency (ALEA) and Alabama Department of Conservation and Natural Resources (ADCNR) are teaming up with paddlesports leaders to offer low-cost beginner kayaking classes Saturday, May 16 at Montgomery Whitewater to kick off National Safe Boating Week.

This is the first year for Kayaking 101 in Alabama, but the multi-state initiative is in its fifth year, having helped hundreds of beginner paddlers across the Southeast safely dip their paddles into kayaking for the firs

articles.title: MTM Unsung Hero Scholarship ceremony set for May 4 in Athens
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
Local youth nonprofit MTM Educational Enrichment will hold its annual Unsung Hero Scholarship and Community heroes celebration at a free-to-attend May 4 event in Athens.

The celebration recognizes students and local leaders “who exemplify excellence, service, and perseverance across Athens and Limestone County,” according to organizers, and will honor more than a dozen local people — including five MTM Unsung Hero Scholars.

The Unsung Hero scholarship funds educational opportunities for gradua

articles.title: Alabama DPH seeks public input via Community Health Opinion online survey
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
The Alabama Department of Public Health would like to hear from state residents about health issues they believe are the most important in their communities.

All Alabamians ages 18 and older are encouraged to express their concerns and opinions in a confidential online survey.

The 2026 Community Health Opinion Survey is a component of the State Health Assessment that identifies health issues and challenges at the community level. Results will be used to guide future public health strategies in

articles.title: Sandra M. Ferguson
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Sandra Marie Ferguson, 38, of Atmore, passed away on April 27, 2026.

She was born in Norfolk, Va. to Charles and Jamie Johnson Ferguson.

She is survived by her parents; her brothers, Chris Ferguson of Virginia Beach, Va. and David (Sarah) Ferguson of Canada; her sister, Amanda (Jason) Hardy of Columbus, Miss.; and nieces and nephew, Abbriana, Kensley, Allie and Alex.

A celebration of life service will be held at a later date.

Johnson-Quimby Funeral Home is in charge of arrangements.

articles.title: Charles W. Stanton
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Charles Wade Stanton, 61, of Robinsonville, passed away on April 26, 2026.

He was born in Atmore to Jesse Sewell Stanton and Gladys Moye Stanton.

He is survived by his wife, Lisa Hudson Stanton; his son, Jesse Stanton of Milton, Fla.; his daughter, Amy (Johnny) Lacy of Bratt, Fla.; a step-son, Trey (Cierra) Hudson of Theodore; a brother, Dennis (LeeAnn) Stanton of Robinsonville; his sister, Judy (Steve) Lambert of Robertsdale; 11 grandchildren; and numerous nieces, nephews, family and friends.

articles.title: Court denies ECACH bid for preliminary injunction, forensic audit in Atmore Community Hospital case
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Escambia County Ala. Community Hospitals Inc.’s (ECACH) motion for preliminary injunction and a forensic audit were denied during a hearing April 28 at the courthouse in Brewton, according to court records.

According to the Cornell Law School’s website, to get a preliminary injunction, a party must show that they will suffer irreparable harm unless the injunction is issued. Preliminary injunctions may be granted after a hearing.

ECACH filed the motion for preliminary injunction on April 22. Fr

articles.author: Staff Reports

The sixth annual Mental Health Awareness Walk and Resource Fair will be held on Sat., May 16 at 10 a.m.

The walk will begin at Boxcar Willie and end at Heritage Park, where the fair will be held.

articles.title: 60 Years Later, A.G. Gaston Boys & Girls Club is Still ‘Finding a Need and Filling It’
articles.body:
By Mia Watkins |For the Birmingham Times

A 60-year-old legacy began with a simple observation.

Business titan and Birmingham’s first Black millionaire, Dr. Arthur George “A.G.” Gaston, was in downtown Birmingham one day during the height of the Civil Rights Movement and noticed a group of young, Black men idling. Not necessarily doing anything nefarious, but they were what we would refer to today as hanging out.

“He looked at them and said, ‘Idle hands…maybe we need to find something for them

articles.body:
Social media posts concerning the future of the East Brewton Police Department and the recent resignation of the police chief have prompted comments from city officials there.

East Brewton Mayor Joey Shell responded saying there would be no disruption in law enforcement in the city.

“I want to inform our residents that the East Brewton Police Chief has officially resigned from his position,” Shell said in a statement. “I want to assure the community that we are working closely with the county

articles.title: Coosa Currents — The State of the Coosa: Pro-bass fishing, new parks and more
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
When the weather warms up, vacationers and Chilton County residents prepare for their favorite part of summer. Whether that is sunbathing, fishing, boating, kayaking, or just enjoying a nice cold one by the water, in Chilton County, it is done on Lake Mitchell.

Lake Mitchell is a 6,000-acre reservoir that makes up the majority of the county line. It is one of seven reservoirs along the Coosa River, and it is one of the many hidden gems of Chilton County. It also provides a huge economic boost f

articles.title: Coosa Currents — Column: Off the iPad, and into the water
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body:
It is no secret among my friends that summer is my favorite season. My childhood best friend describes me as aquatic, which is definitely a summer trait. However, to be honest, my favorite part about summer is swimming and fishing. Mostly because my friends were interested in swimming in the river, pool or going fishing instead of staying inside playing video games.

Waterways like Lake Mitchell and the Coosa River or Lake Lanier and the Chattahoochee River, which I grew up on, I believe, are vi

articles.title: Traywick leads new 2026 Relay Queens to start Relay month of May
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Six new, deserving young girls have assumed the Relay for Life crowns in Chilton County following the 2026 Relay for Life Pageant on April 25. This year’s pageant was the 12th annual event and was held inside McKee Auditorium at Chilton County High School.

This year’s queens are set to lead the fight against cancer in Chilton County over their next year of reign. May is Relay for Life month in Chilton County, and every year the pageant kicks off the festivities.

articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Cullman’s varsity girls soccer team defeated Decatur 2-1 on Wednesday to advance to the quarterfinals of the Class 6A playoffs.

Eva Allen and Maddy Henry each notched goals, and Khloie Harris added a pair of assists for the Bearcats, who improved to 19-3 and will host Athens on Saturday at 5 p.m. for a spot in the Final Four.

Cullman’s varsity boys, meanwhile, prevailed in penalty kicks (3-2) against Randolph on Tuesday after the teams played to a 0-0 draw. Carlos Medina, Reed Flores and Blayc

articles.title: Pending CCBOE approval, Trett Hardman selected for Fairview High School principal
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
Cullman County School Superintendent Dr. Shane Barnette is recommending Trett Hardman as the next principal of Fairview High School. A Fairview native and graduate, Hardman currently serves as principal of the Child Development Center.

“Trett has been an outstanding leader at the CDC, and we are excited about the positive impact he will bring to Fairview,” Barnette said in a press release. “Fairview has benefited from strong leadership, with its principals moving on to larger roles in education

articles.title: Pet of the Week: Biscuit-making Coal-Man
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Coal-Man, an approximately 2-year-old domestic short hair, is The Cullman Tribune’s Pet of the Week.

Coal-Man is a gentle, quiet fellow with a soft personality and curious spirit. Some of his favorite things include making biscuits, playing with his toys and exploring the cat tower.

Though he can be shy at first, Coal-Man warms up with gentle head massages and patient attention. Once he feels comfortable, his sweet nature begins to shine.

With his calm demeanor and affectionate

articles.title: Fairview Robotics teams place in MATE ROV Competition
articles.body:
FAIRVIEW, Ala.– Fairview High School Robotics competed for the second consecutive year in the Northern Gulf Coast Regional MATE ROV Competition. The Dauphin Island Sea Lab’s Discovery Hall Programs hosted the 13th annual Northern Gulf Coast Regional MATE ROV Competition April 24-26. All three teams from Fairview placed in the competition.

Eighteen teams from four states competed with their custom-built remotely operated vehicles (ROVs). The competition is part of the Marine Advanced Technology

articles.title: 2 Hanceville women sentenced to multiple years in prison in separate cases
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Two Hanceville women were sentenced Wednesday in Cullman County Circuit Court, including one woman sentenced to 20 years in prison in chemical endangerment cases and another sentenced to 42 years in connection with a 2022 armed robbery.

Cullman County District Attorney Champ Crocker said the sentencing hearings were held Wednesday, April 29, before Circuit Court Judge Chad Floyd.

Camila Maria Moctezuma, 29, was sentenced to 20 years in prison for two chemical endangerment cases

articles.title: Alabama Strawberry Festival draws estimated 70K
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Rain concerns and an uncertain forecast did little to slow the Alabama Strawberry Festival, as large crowds filled downtown Cullman Friday and Saturday, April 24-25, for what organizers described as one of the event’s strongest weekends yet.

Festival Director Daniel Wyatt said final attendance data is still being compiled, but early estimates show the festival drew around 70,000 people over two days.

“We’re still waiting on all the data to come in, but we’re confident we hosted

articles.title: Obituary: Ruth Keese Tuck
articles.body:
Ruth Keese Tuck, 85, of Cullman passed away April 28, 2026, at her home.  Born at her family home on Indian Creek in the Simcoe community, May 14th, 1940, Ruth’s roots ran deep in the land she loved.  Remarkably, she spent her entire life within five miles of her homestead.

An avid gardener, Ruth found joy and peace among her flowers, especially her beloved roses.  She especially loved the Peace rose and the Peace and Love rose.  She delighted in the simple pleasures like feeding and watching h

articles.title: Obituary: Mildred Urilla Posey
articles.body:
Mildred Urilla Posey, 97, of Cullman, Alabama, passed away on April 27, 2026. Born September 30, 1928, Mildred’s long life was marked by steady devotion to those she loved and a quiet strength that shaped the family around her.

Mildred was preceded in death by her husband, Leon Eldon Posey; her son, Thomas “Buster” Posey; her daughter in law, Connie Posey; and her brother, Larry Woody.

She is survived by her son, Charles Posey; her daughter in law, Deborah Posey; her grandchildren, David (Nikk

articles.body:
Jannette Lynn (Leverett) Legue, 79, passed away on Saturday, April 25, 2026, in Huntsville, AL. No services will be held.

Born on January 7, 1947, in Leamington Ontario Canada, and known as “Joe” to family and friends. She lived a life of kindness and generosity and loved being around her family and cherished her grandchildren. articles.body:
Memorial service for Terry Dewayne Campbell, 61, of Arley will be at 3 p.m., Sunday, May 3rd, 2025, at Cullman Funeral Home Chapel.  The family will receive friends from 2-3 p.m. prior to the service.

Cullman Funeral Home is honored to serve the family.

Terry passed away Tuesday, April 28th, 2026, at his residence.  He was born May 4, 1964, to Duell Wayne and Carolyn Chandler Campbell.

He was preceded in death by his parents.

articles.body:

“Each student member here has gained meaningful leadership insight and perspective through the steps leading to induction,” said Kristen Holmes, vice president for students. “Along the way, you made commitments — to yourselves, to one another

articles.title: Pilot Club holds annual fashion show
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The Pilot Club of Cullman held its annual fashion show and luncheon last week, raising  money for several local organizations.

Pilot Club President Brenda Allison said one of the best parts of the day was seeing people come in and reunite with friends they might only see once a year at the event, saying the event helps create a sense of tradition and community.

articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– A local alternative education program aims to combine academics with life skills and give families more time together.

Lauren Rainwater, who founded and owns Captivate Learning Center, said the program offers a different approach than traditional schools by using a part-time schedule and hands-on learning. articles.title: Cullman teams make pulling a tractor look easy
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –On Saturday, April 25 at the Alabama Strawberry Festival, local titans united in a contest of strength, pulling a colossal John Deere tractor in support of The Willow Path, Cullman County’s domestic violence resource center.

The annual Pulling for Survivors pull event serves to raise funds for The Willow Path, which provides a wide range of services to individuals and families affected by domestic violence, including emergency shelter, counseling, legal advocacy and recovery supp

articles.title: Valley Haven prepares for Hike/Bike/Run
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
This past Friday’s Hike/Bike/Run Auction raised over $11,000 for Valley Haven School. It was the best total in several years and helped build some momentum heading into the final week for this year’s Hike/Bike/Run fundraiser.

The 2002 Lincoln Navigator was the top seller, bringing in $950. Insulation provided by Knauf was also a popular item. 2,906 square feet of R-13 and 3,168 square feet of R-13 were available. One sold for $450 and another for $425. A used Timecutter Toro Zero Turn mower bro

articles.title: Commission approves road work projects, hears updates
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
The Chambers County Commission met on April 27 for its regularly scheduled business meeting.

The commission passed a resolution to enter into an agreement with the Alabama Department of Transportation to install school speed limit signs with flashing time-of-day beacons. The signage will be installed on US 431 north and south of the intersection of County Road 425 near the entrance to Chambers Academy. County Engineer Josh Harvill explained that the cost of the signs and their installation will

articles.title: West Point considers incentive pay, issues proclamation
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
The City of West Point held a work session on April 28 to discuss agenda items ahead of the regular business meeting. During the session, they discussed several items on the agenda for the May 11 city council meeting.

The City of West Point is developing a legally compliant framework for one-time incentive pay for employees. It would cover instances where employees go above and beyond in their duties and is discussed as a way to pay for performance, retention and special circumstances, accordin

articles.title: Bradshaw Library to host Outdoor Movie Night
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
The H. Grady Bradshaw Library will bring back its popular Outdoor Movie Night event on Thursday, May 28, to kick off this year’s summer reading program, which is full of fun events for families as the library encourages children and adults to read for cool prizes all summer.

The library hosts two free movie nights each summer, one to launch the summer reading program and the other to officially close out the program. Every year, the free movie nights are a big hit, with the first movie night of

articles.title: Valley promotes four officers to corporal rank
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
A larger-than-normal crowd for a city council meeting was present Monday evening to see four Valley Police Department officers promoted from the rank of patrol officer to corporal. They include Austin Billingsley, Austin Graben, Christian Chavez and Brandon Longshore. Each was pinned with their new bars by their wife or a close family member.

Chief Stacy Shirley said he was proud of the job being done by each man and that each one richly deserved the promotion.

In department head communication

articles.title: Local nonprofit takes veterans out on the lake
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
A LaGrange man talked to the West Point Rotary Club on Thursday about the Veterans Fishing Organization (VFO) he founded 10 years ago, which has provided much-needed outdoor enjoyment for veterans of U.S. military service, especially combat veterans who have struggled with Post Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD).

Few people know West Point Lake the way Ken Bearden does. Much of his 40 years of fishing experience and 20 years of professional guiding experience has taken place there.

Bearded is bl

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Lynn Gendusa: When Grief Reaches for Hope
articles.author: Lynn Gendusa
articles.body:
I’ve never been able to accept the idea that Heaven is quiet.

When my father died, I didn’t imagine him simply at rest. I imagined him searching for the perfect pecan pie and ice cream.

Grief is a challenging experience, and there is no remedy to alleviate the pain. Losing a loved one turns our lives upside down. Most people have felt profound heartache at some point. Animals also experience grief; for instance, hearing the mournful cry of a mother elephant who has lost her young can break you

articles.title: Beloved food truck festival returns to LaFayette
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
LaFayette Main Street will be hosting its next Food Truck Festival to kick off the summer on Friday, May 8.

LaFayette Main Street hosts these festivals quarterly as a way to bring the community together, promote LaFayette and promote area food trucks. It’s become a beloved community event.

“This is to really strengthen downtown vibrancy, promote tourism and visitors to see what LaFayette has to offer, and also just create a fun gathering space for people to enjoy different varieties of food an

articles.title: Crenshaw schools advance in post-season baseball
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
Three Crenshaw County schools — Brantley, Highland Home and Luverne — reached round one of the AHSAA baseball playoffs. Brantley and Highland Home will be advancing to round two while Luverne’s season ended in a first-round loss to G.W. Long.

Brantley School

The Brantley Bulldogs under head coach Andre Parks finished the regular season 17-and-1. Then, Bulldogs faced Choctaw County in round one of the playoffs.

“We were able to defeat them 15 to 0 in game one and 16 to 0 in game two,” Parks sa

articles.title: What's Happening in Crenshaw County
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Visit our website at www.luvernejournal.com to view a live, interactive events calendar.

Elementary Play

The 43rd annual First grade play will be held in the Luverne High School auditorium on April 30 at 6:00 p.m. Advanced tickets will be on sale Tuesday, April 28 from 3:15 - 5 p.m. in front of the Elementary office and at the door on April 30 at 5 p.m.

Book Club

The Word Nerds book club will meet Thursday, April 30, at the Luverne Public Library. All adult reading enthusiasts are invited to

articles.title: Alabama mayors urge voters to expand Aniah’s Law
articles.author: Jacob Holmes
articles.body:
The mayors of Alabama’s 10 biggest cities are encouraging voters to back expansions to Aniah’s Law.

The law is named after Aniah Blanchard, an Auburn University student kidnapped and murdered in 2019 by a man out on bond on charges of another violent crime.

Alabama voters approved a constitutional amendment in 2022 dubbed “Aniah’s Law,” allowing judges to deny bond to defendants charged with serious violent crimes, including murder, kidnapping, rape and first-degree robbery.

The mayors Wednes

articles.title: Study Commission on AI and Children’s Safety holds first meeting
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
The Alabama Joint Study Commission on Artificial Intelligence and Children’s Safety held its first meeting this week, opening an effort to determine whether the state needs new guardrails as AI tools and social media platforms become more embedded in children’s lives.

State Representative Ben Robbins, R-Sylacauga, introduced House Resolution 51 to the Legislature in February. Robbins told members on Wednesday that the group’s charge is straightforward.

“The goal of this task force is to do two

articles.title: Sen. Cory Booker to attend upcoming Birmingham town hall
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
U.S. Senator Cory Booker, D-New Jersey, will attend a Birmingham town hall hosted by Blueprint Alabama on Monday, May 4, the organization recently announced.

According to the organizers, Booker will join Alabama voters for “an important conversation at a critical moment for our country.” The senator was originally scheduled to visit the Magic Citylast Novemberfor a town hall with U.S. Representative Terri Sewell, D-Alabama, and former U.S. Senator Doug Jones, but wasunable to attendfollowing a

articles.title: When money decides elections before voters do
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Most elections are decided before most voters are paying attention.

And much of the money shaping those outcomes is never fully seen and never fully understood.

In this week’s “This Matters,” Bill Britt examines how primary elections—especially in Alabama—have become the true battleground for power, and what that means for voters who only engage once the outcome is already taking shape. Because once influence operates quietly and without transparency, elections stop being a contest of ideas an

articles.title: Charles Barkley donates $25,000 to support Children’s of Alabama
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Basketball Hall of Famer and Leeds native Charles Barkley has donated $25,000 to Children’s of Alabama in support of the Regions Tradition’s charitable mission.

Children’s of Alabama is the primary charitable beneficiary of the Regions Tradition and is one of the 10 busiest children’s hospitals in the country, serving patients from every county in Alabama and across the United States.

“On behalf of our team at Children’s of Alabama, we are incredibly grateful to Charles Barkley for a $25,000 d

articles.title: NFIB Alabama PAC endorses 78 lawmakers for reelection
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The NFIB Alabama Political Action Committee announced Wednesday that it has endorsed 78 incumbents for reelection to the Alabama Legislature, citing their “strong support for small businesses.”

The PAC, made up entirely of NFIB members, endorsed 25 candidates for the Senate and 53 for the House of Representatives. All earned a 100% NFIB Voting Record by supporting policies on issues ranging from retail theft to property tax assessments.

“Small business is the foundation of Alabama’s economy,”

articles.title: Mt. Moriah Farms wins Alabama rural small business honor
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
A Wiregrass business rooted in family, agriculture and entrepreneurship is receiving statewide recognition—and Troy University announced Wednesday that it is inviting the community to celebrate and learn more about resources available to small businesses across the region.

Mia Scott, owner ofMt. Moriah Farmsin Dothan, has been named Alabama’s Rural-Owned Small Business of the Year as part of the 2026 National Small Business Week honors. The recognition places her business among a select group o

articles.title: Opinion | Ernie Yarbrough fumbles on the goal line—twice
articles.author: Angelo “Doc” Mancuso
articles.body: Representative Ernie Yarbrough walked into this legislative session with two bills that should have been touchdowns: House Bill 7 / House Bill 13—The Laken Riley Act and House Bill 541—Closed Primary Elections. These weren’t long shot policy experiments or fringe ideological statements. They were the kind of bills any Republican legislator would normallywantto carry because they’re straightforward, emotionally resonant, and aligned with the priorities of the conservative base. Both were red meat

articles.title: Opinion | Nobody cares. No, seriously, nobody cares
articles.author: Tom Greene
articles.body:
There’s a great golf course in south Georgia that carries an amazing yet simple tag line: “Nobody Cares.” It’s the kind of place where nobody keeps track of their score. My kind of place. The admonition suggests that you should be enjoying the course, your friends, and the weather. And not worrying about the score. Why? Because nobody cares…but you.

Much of golf, and life, is spent as if someone is watching, evaluating, keeping track of your score. They aren’t. The simple truth is that nobody i

articles.title: Analysis | Alabama GOP attorney general primary turns costly, bitter and unsettled
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
The Republican primary for Alabama attorney general is becoming one of the most expensive and bitter intraparty fights in recent Alabama political history.

Republican candidates Jay Mitchell and Katherine Robertson are now openly attacking each other’s conservative credentials, honesty, political loyalties and fitness for office in a campaign increasingly dominated by accusations involving DEI, foreign lobbying, Islam, Hillary Clinton and “weak frauds.”

And yet, despite months of attacks and m

articles.title: Alabama Department of Corrections, AG’s office face possible sanctions in death penalty case
articles.author: Ralph Chapoco
articles.body:
An oxygen mask. A federal judge Tuesday threatened sanctions against the Alabama Department of Corrections and the Attorney General's Office after finding officials made false statements regarding devices used in nitrogen gas execution. (Getty Images)

A federal judge Tuesday threatened officials from the Alabama Department of Corrections and the state Attorney General’s Office with sanctions after ruling they made false statements regarding the state’s protocol for nitrogen gas executions.

U.S

articles.title: Congressional Black Caucus members condemn Supreme Court’s gutting of the Voting Rights Act
articles.author: Jonathan Shorman
articles.body:
Rev. Bernard LaFayette (center, in wheelchair and cloth cap) holds his wife Kate’s hand as they are wheeled over the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, Alabama on March 9, 2025 as part of 60th anniversary commemorations of Bloody Sunday, the 1965 attack on peaceful civil rights protestors that led to the Selma-to-Montgomery March and the Voting Rights Act. LaFayette ran the Selma voting rights campaign in 1965 and survived an assassination attempt. (Photo by John Partipilo/Tennessee Lookout)

articles.author: Kevin Hardy
articles.body:
Labor unions and other supporters of an income tax on millionaire earners rallied at the Washington state Capitol in Olympia in February. A growing number of liberal states are considering raising taxes on their wealthiest residents. (Photo by Bill Lucia/Washington State Standard)

While the idea of a special tax on millionaires is hotly debated across the country, Maine state Rep. Cheryl Golek characterized her state’s new tax as a modest and reasonable step toward fairness.

articles.author: Andrea Tinker
articles.body:
Sen. Rodger Smitherman, D-Birmingham, discusses a Democratic congressional map proposal during a special session on redistricting on Wednesday, July 19, 2023 in Montgomery, Ala. Gov. Kay Ivey Wednesday said she would not call a special session for redistricting following a U.S. Supreme Court ruling that will make it significantly harder for plaintiffs to challenge maps on the basis of racial discrimination. (Stew Milne for Alabama Reflector)

Wednesday’s Supreme Court ruling significantly limiti

articles.author: Brian Lyman
articles.body:
A crowd gathering at the foot of the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, Alabama, on March 9, 2025. The U.S. Supreme Court Wednesday gutted a key portion of the Voting Rights Act, passed after law enforcement attacked civil rights marchers on the bridge on March 7, 1965, an event known as Bloody Sunday. (Anna Barrett/Alabama Reflector)

Think of the millions of Alabamians who lived, loved and died under an apartheid government, a regime that lasted nearly a century, presiding over lynchings and mass

articles.title: US Supreme Court seems to side with Trump actions to strip legal status for Haitians, Syrians
articles.author: Ariana Figueroa
articles.body:
Demonstrators chant and hold signs outside the U.S. Supreme Court on April 29, 2026 in Washington, DC. The court heard arguments challenging the Department of Homeland Secuirty's termination of Temporary Protected Status for immigrants from Haiti and Syria. (Photo by Tom Brenner/Getty Images)

WASHINGTON — The U.S. Supreme Court appeared poised Wednesday to uphold the Trump administration’s efforts to end temporary legal protections for 350,000 Haitians and 6,000 Syrians.
