articles.author: Ashley Murray
articles.body:
Kevin Warsh, U.S. President Donald Trump's nominee for chair of the Federal Reserve, testifies during his Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs confirmation hearing in the Dirksen Senate Office Building on April 21, 2026 in Washington, D.C. (Photo by Andrew Harnik/Getty Images)

WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump’s pick to lead the Federal Reserve was one step closer to the job Wednesday after North Carolina Republican U.S. Sen. Thom Tillis cast the deciding vote to advance Ke

articles.title: US Supreme Court limits use of race in congressional district remaps, diluting Voting Rights Act
articles.author: Jonathan Shorman
articles.body:
The U.S. Supreme Court on Oct. 29, 2024. (Photo by Jane Norman/States Newsroom)

Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis’ office on Monday invoked an upcoming landmark U.S. Supreme Court decision on the role of race in drawing congressional districts to justify the Republican’s proposed gerrymander.

“The use of race in redistricting should never happen,” the governor’s general counsel, David Axelman,wrote in a memounveiling a map that aims to hand Republicans four additional U.S. House seats in Florida.

articles.title: Boyfriend, arrested, charged in child porn case
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — The boyfriend of the 22-yearold who was charged with taking nude photos of a 6-year-old girl is facing 76 criminal charges, including first-degree sodomy and sexual abuse of a child less than 12 years old.

Franklin County Sheriff’s investigators issued a warrant for the arrest of Michael Clinton Chambers, 26, of Carrollton, Georgia, two weeks after his girlfriend, Abigail Roberts, was arrested on 106 counts ranging from possession of child pornography to firstdegree sodomy. Chamb

articles.title: City alters vacation sell back policy
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — City councilmembers have voted to amend the personnel policy and procedures ordinance to allow employees to sell back up to 80 hours of unused vacation time each year instead of once every five years.

Employees must notify the human resources department by Dec. 10 if they plan to sell vacation time. Previously, the deadline was May 15.

Employees who sell vacation time will continue to receive straight time pay rather than overtime rates. The payouts will be processed through reg

articles.title: Primary election: Register to vote by May 4
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — The deadline to register to vote in the upcoming primary election is approaching for Franklin County residents.

May 4 is the last day to register, according to the Alabama Secretary of State’s election calendar. The primary election will be held on May 19.

Residents may register at the county board of registrars office in the courthouse, or online through the Secretary of State’s website. Voters must bring a valid form of identification and they will be asked to choose either a

articles.title: The sky turned black, and he lived to tell it
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
PHIL CAMPBELL — By the time April 27, 2011, arrived, Rodney Smith had already grown accustomed to the warnings.

For days, sirens had gone off across Franklin County. Storms had come and gone. Like many people in the area, he had started to believe this would be another false alarm.

As time would tell, it was not.

Smith was a teacher in Franklin County, as was his wife. That afternoon, he watched a storm pass over Tharptown Elementary School, where he taught. On the drive home, he saw fields c

articles.title: EMA warns: Don’t rely on storm sirens
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Franklin County EMA Director Mary Glass said outdoor warning sirens should not be residents’ primary alert system during severe weather because electronic systems can fail, even after testing and repairs.

“We tell everybody, do not depend on these sirens,” Glass said. “They need three forms of communication — television, cell phones and a weather radio.”

Glass said outdoor warning sirens were installed years ago when weather radios and cell phones were not as common, but they ar

articles.title: Ex-principal recalls lost students, teacher
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
PHIL CAMPBELL — On the morning of April 27, 2011, Phil Campbell Elementary School (PCES) Principal Jackie Ergle was aware of the threat of severe weather supposedly coming through the area, but little could prepare her for what would soon unfold.

While her home remained relatively untouched by the EF-5 tornado, what she saw in other parts of town after the storm passed became seared into her memory.

“I don’t think anybody thought the storm would bring the devastation it ended up bringing,” Erg

articles.title: West Elementary hosts Careers on Wheels
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — West Elementary students climbed into concrete trucks, explored emergency vehicles, and learned about skilled trades during the school’s annual Careers on Wheels event.

As part of the event, students visited a concrete truck from SRM Concrete, a Russellville Fire Department fire truck, a utility bucket truck and a police vehicle. Representatives from each spoke to the students and guided them through their encounters.

Russellville City Schools career coach Alissa Moore said gett

articles.title: Cultura Garden Club spotlights pollinators
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Bees, butterflies and plenty of garden talk filled the room as Cultura Garden Club members gathered at North Highlands Church of Christ in Russellville for the club’s April meeting.

As a member of the club, I always enjoy meetings that mix gardening, learning and fellowship, and this month’s focus on pollinators brought all three together.

Barbara Sage, program chair, led the meeting’s program on bees, butterflies and the important role pollinators play in gardens and food production. Members

articles.title: State should broaden its readiness definition
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
Families across Alabama are asking hard and necessary questions about what’s next for their high school students. What’s the right path for my child? What if my student isn’t excited about enrolling in a traditional, four-year college program? And how can our schools better prepare students for a future that’s changing faster than ever before?

At the heart of this conversation is how we define and measure post-high school readiness. For too long, the nation has relied on a definition of readine

articles.title: ‘If you see something, … say something’
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
A growing number of child abuse cases involve possession and production of child pornography. A particularly shocking case surfaced two weeks ago in Franklin County after investigators arrested a 22-year-old and charged her with 106 cases of child pornography and sodomy involving a six-year-old girl.

The charges included 30 counts each of production of child pornography, possession of child pornography, and dissemination of child pornography, 11 counts of first-degree sexual abuse, three counts

articles.title: Local group seeks to help veterans
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Veterans in Franklin County who need help with groceries, transportation, meals, wellness checks and caregiver support may not always know where to turn, Denel Wardell said.

Wardell, president of the Republican Women of Franklin County Association, said the group created the Honoring Veterans Committee to help connect veterans with available support, regardless of political affiliation.

She said privacy laws prevent volunteers from accessing the contact numbers of veterans, so v

articles.title: Free CPR, home safety programs offered
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — The Russellville Fire Department is offering free CPR classes, smoke detector installation and home safety inspections as part of an expanded effort to help residents prevent emergencies before they happen.

One of the department’s newest offerings is a non-certified CPR class beginning May 21 from 5 to 8 p.m. at Station One. Classes will be offered about every three months and are limited to 30 people.

Fire Chief Joe Mansell said CPR training can make a life-or-death difference

articles.title: Pleasant Valley sweeps Belgreen
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Belgreen Bulldogs’ season came to an end last Thursday after being swept by Pleasant Valley 11-2 and 6-2.

The games weren’t as lopsided as the scores would indicate. Belgreen hit the ball well and pitched well. Fielding on the other hand doomed the Bulldogs.

In game one, Belgreen outhit Pleasant Valley 7-6 despite losing 11-2. The top of the order was especially effective with Colton Ashton and Noah Scott getting two hits each while Rush Berryman had three hits, a RBI and scored a run. Sco

articles.title: RHS softball goes 2-3 in I-65 Showdown
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Russellville softball team went 4-4 during a week of games that included the I-65 Showdown in Athens.

RHS went to Hatton High School on April 20 and fell to the Lady Hornets, 8-5. Russellville had eight hits and Khristyan Huerta led the team with two. Russellville’s RBIs were by Sadie Odom, Chloe James and Kaylee Suggs.

Taitum Bishop pitched five innings and Suggs pitched one for RHS.

Russellville then hosted West Point the following afternoon and the Lady Tigers got a 5-1 area win over t

articles.title: Stadium on target for UNA opener Sept. 19
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
FLORENCE — Bobby Wallace Field at Bank Independent Stadium continues to take shape and major physical elements will be added in the next three months.

University of North Alabama Athletic Director Josh Looney said during a Thursday stadium tour construction remains on schedule for the Sept. 19 home opener against Alcorn State.

As of now, some key dates coming up include installation of video board panels and chairback seats starting May 19; work on the north end zone berm beginning in June; an

articles.title: Music minister’s song places third at conference
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — Garrett Booth has spent about 10 years writing songs, but one written in about 30 minutes recently carried him to national recognition.

Booth, music education minister at First Baptist Church in Russellville, placed third out of 120 entries in the songwriting competition at the Southern Baptist Church Music Conference with his original song, “Jesus Only You.”

The annual conference took place April 19-22 in Jacksonville, Florida.

The song grew from a phrase Pastor Chase Dowdy of

articles.title: Warhurst named curriculum coordinator
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
RUSSELLVILLE — For Tiffany Warhurst, stepping into the curriculum coordinator role for Russellville City Schools means leaving the elementary campus she has led for eight years.

After four years as principal and four as assistant principal at Russellville Elementary School, Warhurst will replace Dr. Natalie Bendall, who left the curriculum coordinator role earlier this year after accepting a regional in-service center director’s position at the University of North Alabama.

Warhurst said the mo

articles.title: Red Bay bounces back to advance to second round
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Red Bay Tigers recovered from a game two shutout to win a decisive game three on Friday to advance to the second round of the Class 2A baseball playoffs.

Red Bay won the opening game of its first-round series on Thursday evening, beating Westminster-Oak Mountain 5-2. The Knight shutout the Tigers in the nightcap, 4-0, to force a decisive game three on Friday. Red Bay beat Westminster 5-1 in that game to move to the next round.

Red Bay will travel to Sumiton Christian on Thursday for a doub

articles.title: Bobcats beat Geraldine; play Oakman in Round 2
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Phil Campbell Bobcats bounced back after a series opening loss to Geraldine to win the next two games and advance to the second round of the Class 3A baseball playoffs.

Phil Campbell will travel to Oakman for round two on Friday with games at 4:30 and 7 p.m. Game three, if necessary, will be on Saturday at 1 p.m.

The Bobcats left the bases loaded three times in game one on Thursday evening and made a couple of critical errors that overshadowed a solid pitching performance by freshman Jack

articles.title: Vina girls track team runner-up at sectionals
articles.author: tecnavia

articles.title: RHS beats Sardis, moves to Round 2
articles.author: tecnavia
articles.body:
The Russellville Golden Tigers took two of three games from Sardis in the AHSAA Class 5A baseball playoffs to advance to the second round.

Russellville will play American Christian Academy.

In game one against Sardis, Brayden Entrekin pitched a complete game for the win. The lefthander struck out six and allowed eight hits.

Caleb Hawkins had two hits for RHS and collected five RBIs. Bryson Cooper and Tristan Ray had three hits each. Tripp Cleveland and Entrekin added two hits apiece, and Mala

articles.title: More than two dozen Alabama mayors endorse Wes Allen for lieutenant governor
articles.body:
More than 30 mayors from across Alabama have endorsed Secretary of StateWes Allenin his campaign for lieutenant governor ahead of the May 19 Republican primary.

“I am honored to have the support of mayors from across the state,” Allen said. “Mayors want what is best for their municipalities, for their state and for their country. They know that I will work hard each and every day as Lt. Governor to put Alabama first.”

Mayors who have endorsed Wes Allen include:

Allen is running in a seven-way

articles.title: ‘Do you stay the course… or push forward?’: Viral Alabama teen’s cancer fight faces new uncertainty
articles.body:
An Alabamateenwhose viral cancer plea helped secure access to an experimental treatment is now facing new challenges, including a delay in travel for care and mounting financial strain, as supporters have launched a fundraiser on his behalf.

In a recent update, Will Roberts’ mother said the family was unable to travel to California for treatment as soon as expected after he did not meet the necessary platelet levels.

“We did not reach platelet count today so a trip to Santa Monica will not be

articles.title: Senate Pro Tem Gudger secures additional $1 million in ADEM funding for Good Hope wastewater project
articles.body:
Senate PresidentPro Tem Garlan Gudger(R-Cullman) announced that the Alabama Department of Environmental Management has approved an additional $1 million in funding for the City of Good Hope to complete a wastewater infrastructure project after construction bids exceeded original estimates.

The Cullman County legislative delegation had previously secured $3.3 million in ADEM grants for the project in 2023, but bids came in more than $1 million over the city’s estimated cost, a gap Gudger attribu

articles.title: Ivey announces retirement of ADOC Commissioner John Hamm, taps Greg Lovelace as successor
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey on Tuesday announced the retirement ofAlabama Department of CorrectionsCommissioner John Hamm and named longtime corrections official Greg Lovelace as his successor through the remainder of her term.

Hamm, who has led the Alabama Department of Corrections since January 2022, is stepping down following what Ivey described as a “transformative tenure,” during which the agency has undertaken major reforms and infrastructure improvements.

“Running the Department of Corrections in

articles.title: Trade groups encourage open communication between data centers and communities for successful projects
articles.body:
Trade groups are encouraging greater collaboration betweendata centerdevelopers and local communities, arguing that open communication and clear expectations are key to ensuring successful projects and long-term economic benefits.

In an industry-backed overview of data center development, trade groups highlighted the growing role these facilities play in supporting the modern economy, noting that sectors ranging from healthcare and education to national security rely heavily on data storage, cl

articles.title: New $100,000 scholarship initiative supports Alabama students in aviation, construction careers
articles.body:
Alabama students pursuing careers in high-demand skilled trades will soon have access to new financial support through a statewide scholarship initiative.

TheAlabama Community College System(ACCS), in partnership with the Alabama Association of Resource Conservation and Development Councils (RCD), is offering $100,000 in scholarships to help residents train for careers in aviation and construction.

The program, titled “Building Futures: Enhancing Lives with Scholarships,” will begin accepting

articles.title: Steve Marshall op-ed: We must listen to survivors – now and always
articles.body:
Often, the public’s fascination with criminals steals attention away from the voices of those who have been harmed. Recent documentaries like Netflix’s “Monster: The Jeffrey Dahmer Story” have turned perpetrators into subjects of cultural intrigue.

Meanwhile, the stories of victims are left in the shadows, with many unaware of their lives, their families, and the impact they left. However, as Attorney General, the victims and their families are always at the forefront of my mind because they ar

articles.title: BCA endorses Ortis over incumbent in Alabama Senate District 15 Republican primary
articles.body:
The Business Council of Alabama’s political arm endorsed challengerScott Ortisover incumbent Sen. Dan Roberts (R-Mountain Brook) in the Republican primary for Alabama Senate District 15, the organization announced Monday.

ProgressPAC, BCA’s political action committee, cited Ortis’ background in education, healthcare, and business ownership in announcing the endorsement ahead of the May 19 primary.

“Scott Ortis has spent his career serving others, first in the classroom, then in healthcare and

articles.title: Former Alabama player pleads guilty in wild fraud case impersonating NFL players
articles.body: Michael Brauner is a Senior Sports Analyst and Contributing Writer for Yellowhammer News. You can follow him on Twitter@MBraunerWNSPand hear him every weekday morning from 6 to 9 a.m. on “The Opening Kickoff” on WNSP-FM 105.5,available free online.

articles.title: Supreme Court rules racial gerrymandering is unconstitutional
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
In a landmark decision Wednesday, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled inLouisiana v. Callaisthat racially gerrymandered districts are unconstitutional.

The Supreme Court held that Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act and the Fourteenth and Fifteenth Amendments of the U.S. Constitution neither require nor allow States to use race to draw voting districts, either to create additional minority-majority districts or to intentionally dilute the vote of minority voters. States must use race-neutral considerat

articles.title: Orthopedic surgeon speaks with local HOSA students
articles.author: Submitted Article
articles.body:
Dr. Robert Liljeberg, orthopedic surgeon and member of the Troy Hospital Health Care Authority Board, recently spoke with students in Alyssa Free’s Health Sciences class from the Troy-Pike Center for Technology.

The students are wrapping up their clinical rotations at Troy Regional this week, having spent three days a week throughout the semester observing and working alongside hospital staff. The experience has given them valuable, hands-on exposure to careers in healthcare.

For Dr. Liljeberg

articles.title: How Courts Handle Conflicting Expert Opinions In Columbia Personal Injury Cases
articles.author: Sponsored Content
articles.body:
When professionals like doctors or engineers offer conflicting stories about an accident, it creates confusion for the jury. One might call a floor hazardous, while another claims it was safe.

Understanding how Columbia courts handle these conflicting opinions clarifies how judges and juries weigh professional testimony. This process is key to deciding who to believe when specialists disagree on the fundamental facts of a case.

Before a professional even gets to talk to a jury, the judge acts

articles.title: Student art is front and center at TroyFest
articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
The Johnson Center for the Arts was busy during the 2026 TroyFest weekend that included the awarding of student art recognitions and of 2026 Art Scholarships to two local high school students.

The 300 student artworks continue on display at the Johnson Center in the art show sponsored by K&W Plastics.

Winners from grades K-12 were awarded First, Second, Third and Best of Show certificates for various art categories in four different grade divisions.

“I really believe that a student art show i

articles.title: Country roads … take me home to the place where I belong
articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
One’s artwork can share a side of a loved one, outside of life.

My daddy, William Johnson Caldwell, was a man’s man of the times.

He went to work every day except Sunday; came home, got on his throne and called for his pipe and bowl of ice cream that Mama took him just as Perry Mason was coming on the “television set.”

Daddy was a man of few words; he didn’t say much but when he did, somebody better listen.

Mama did most of Daddy’s talking on the telephone, which he said was just for “gossip

articles.title: Up, Up and Away: Banks students discover STEM through kites
articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
For the kindergarten students at Banks School, the words, “up and away,” now mean having fun on a warm windy day.

For most of the Banks students flying a kite was a new experience. For the first time, perhaps, many of them felt the tug when their kites first caught the wind and, if so, the feeling was surely unique and memorable.

The “Up, Up and Away” lesson was planned by the Banks kindergarten teachers, Sonjua Dorril and Sabrina Thomas and their Troy University interns and math coach, Kristi

articles.title: Letter to the Editor: Pine Hills Project Built on Proven Technology, Strict Safety Oversight
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
The purpose of this letter is to provide facts and scientific information on geologic carbon dioxide (CO2) storage, also known as carbon sequestration, as well as information on the Pine Hills Storage Hub in Covington County, AL.

I have worked professionally on carbon storage projects since 2006. Between 2001 and 2006 I completed a doctorate in Civil Engineering at Princeton University. My research at Princeton was specifically on understanding how CO2 stored in geologic formations interacts wi

articles.title: Covington County Voters List
articles.author: Andalusia Star-News
articles.body: The Covington County Voters List contains all of the names of the registered voters who reside in Covington County.

articles.title: DOJ: Athens man receives 18 year prison sentence on child pornography charges
articles.author: Ben Bullard
articles.body:
A federal judge has handed an 18-year prison sentence to an Athens man who pleaded guilty late last year to distribution and possession of child pornography.

Hunter William Bell, 29, of Athens, was sentenced to 216 months in prison, to be followed by a 20-year term of supervised release. U.S. District Judge Corey L. Maze, who serves the United States District Court for the Northern District of Alabama, passed Bell’s sentence.

In an April 30 statement, the U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ) said

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL PLAYOFFS: No. 10 Elkmont upended by No. 5 Collinsville
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Elkmont’s postseason journey came to a close on Thursday following a second-round sweep at the hands of Collinsville.

The No. 10 Red Devils (24-13) tallied just two hits and struck out 15 times in a 7-0 loss to the No. 5 Panthers in the opener.

They battled back admirably in a high-scoring nightcap, though, plating five runs in the top half of the seventh inning to tie the game.

Collinsville, however, walked it off shortly afterward on a dropped third strike/wild pitch for a 15-14 win.

Game

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL PLAYOFFS: East Limestone splits with Lincoln to set up tiebreaker
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
East Limestone’s bid for a Class 5A quarterfinal berth will come down to a winner-take-all tiebreaker.

The Indians defeated Lincoln 4-3 in the opener of their second-round series on Thursday before falling 3-2 in the nightcap to set up a pivotal Game 3.

The matchup is scheduled for Friday at 4:30 p.m.

Game 1

East Limestone 4, Lincoln 3

Brodie Coker:2-for-3

Landon Compton:2 RBIs

Josh Thallas:RBI

Charlie Eslick:6 IP, 7 H, 3 ER, 5 K

Game 2

Lincoln 3, East Limestone 2

Landon Compton:RBI

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL PLAYOFFS: No. 1 Lindsay Lane rolls into Class 2A quarterfinals with sweep of Cold Springs
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Lindsay Lane continued its dominant play on Thursday, sweeping Cold Springs in the Class 2A playoffs to advance to the quarterfinals.

The top-ranked Lions, who have won 15 straight games, improved to 32-6 this season following a 4-0 triumph in the opener and a 13-3 victory in the nightcap.

Wyatt Wallace scattered two hits and six strikeouts in a Game 1 shutout, and Lindsay Lane tallied 16 hits in Game 2 to complement Grayson Miller’s outing on the mound.

“Super proud of our boys tonight,” Lin

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL PLAYOFFS: Athens Bible advances to Class 1A quarterfinals with sweep of No. 8 Skyline
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Athens Bible secured a home sweep of Skyline on Thursday to advance to the quarterfinals of the Class 1A playoffs for the first time since 2018.

The Trojans parlayed solid pitching into 7-1 and 4-2 wins over the No. 8 Vikings.

“Ryan Faust set the tone in the opener with a complete game, Justin Crafton pitched six strong innings in the second game, and Luke Murrell closed that one out with three strikeouts in the seventh,” Athens Bible coach Brad Murrell said. “Our defense was on key today and

articles.title: Alabama Extension spotlights Farm-City success
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
From music and movies to books and TV shows, popular culture often draws comedic inspiration by highlighting differences between urban areas and rural communities. However, Alabama Farm-City strives to promote the similarities and interdependence that exist between farmers and city dwellers.

Many Alabama Cooperative Extension System employees volunteer as county Farm-City committee members to support local efforts. These committees plan and host banquets, award programs, proclamation signings,

articles.title: Northview softball tops Jay 7-0 to claim district championship, advance to Class Rural regional semifinal
articles.author: Andrew Garner
articles.body:
Northview’s Mikayla McAnally threw a one-hitter and struck out 11 to help lead the Lady Chiefs to a 7-0 victory over Jay, Fla. Thursday night to claim the Class Rural, District 1 championship.

McAnally threw a complete game during the victory, which lasted less than 2 hours.

The Lady Chiefs got off to a hot start, scoring four runs in the first inning and three runs in the second inning.

NHS head softball coach Amy Holland said getting off to a good start was the goal.

“We got off to a reall

articles.title: Mary J. Linam
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Mary Joan Linam, 86, went home to be with her Lord and Savior on Wed., April 29, 2026.

She was born in Salina, Kansas, on August 25, 1939, where her loving grandmother, Mary Crouthers, raised her.

She is preceded in death by her grandmother; and her sister, Jeanie Sawyer.

Joan is survived by her devoted husband of 68 years, Joseph Ray Linam; her sons, Kevin Linam, Todd and Rhonda Linam, Joel and Lana Linam; her sister, Janet Moore; nine grandchildren; and 18 great-grandchildren.

A private fa

articles.title: Atmore Mayfest still on as rain expected to clear by noon Saturday
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Atmore Area Chamber of Commerce officials said they’ve been closely monitoring the weather for Mayfest, and the rain should clear out around noon on Saturday.

Executive Director Addison Long said on the chamber’s Facebook page to help everyone avoid heavier rainfall, vendors will have until 9 a.m. to complete setup, and the Beautiful Baby Contest will begin at 11 a.m.

“Mayfest will still take place as scheduled and is a rain-or-shine event unless severe weather is predicted,” Long said. “We’ll

articles.title: From Seedlings to Screen: A.G. Gaston Boys & Girls Club Puts STEM at Forefront
articles.body:
By Mia Watkins |For the Birmingham Times

The Walter Howlett Club of the A.G. Gaston Boys & Girls Club may be a place for recreation for local children, but it is also on the cutting edge of science and technology education.

As the club celebrates its 60th anniversary, CEO Andre McFadden said it is looking to its future and how it can equip its members to thrive.

“What can we do now that could open up possibilities for them later?” he said. “Or just encourage and stimulate the imagination, som

articles.title: U.S. Senator Cory Booker To Visit Birmingham on Monday for Town Hall
articles.body:
U.S. Senator Cory Booker (D-N.J.) will be coming to Birmingham on Monday, May 4, for a rescheduled town hall with Blueprint Alabama.

“I’m excited to return to Alabama, because the people of Alabama are no strangers to confronting the kind of moral moment America is in right now,” said Senator Booker.

“In every part of our country, Americans are facing the same kinds of hardships: struggling to get by, disgusted by the corruption in our political system, and yearning for bold change,” he added.

articles.title: Thorsby Together — City rallies behind Barnes family after sudden passing of young son
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
“I know in my heart that everything is going to be okay.”

Eli Barnes’ legacy in Thorsby will live on for decades as the young boy’s impact is being felt after his sudden passing on April 23. A community was shaken to its core, but one that is still rallying behind the Barnes family.

April 23 began like any other day for Eli. Like many eight-year-old boys growing up in Thorsby, he loved baseball. He loved the game and everything that comes with it. He had a game that night, which made the day e

articles.title: Construction on I-65 bridges over Walnut Creek to begin on May 6
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Bridge construction is coming to Interstate 65 in Chilton County as the Alabama Department of Transportation (ALDOT) is preparing to replace both structures over Walnut Creek.

ALDOT announced on April 28 that it plans to replace both bridges, northbound and southbound, at milepost 209 that go over Walnut Creek. The bridges are being replaced with more modern structures to improve safety.

The project will begin on May 6 and continue for around two years, as the weather permits. Nighttime lane c

articles.title: CCHS soccer magical season ends in playoffs at Northridge
articles.author: Carey Reeder
articles.body:
Chilton County High School’s magical run for both of its soccer programs came to an end in the first round of the state playoffs at Northridge High School on April 23.

For the first in program history, both the varsity boys’ and girls’ teams advanced to the Class 6A state playoffs in the same year. The Tigers had their work cut out for them as both teams drew Northridge in the first round — a top-10 Class 6A squad.

In the varsity girls’ match, Northridge built a strong 7-0 lead as the match su

articles.title: Softball regular season wraps up in Chilton County
articles.author: Andrew White
articles.body: As the regular season wraps up, Chilton County area softball teams try to get their pitching and hitting locked in for postseason play. Chilton County High School had a big 10-run win over Marbury High School to end the regular season. In Thorsby High School’s last two regular-season games, they scored 31 unanswered runs. Between those two games, Brooklyn Reeser and Tamara Hinton both threw no-hitters. Maplesville High School ended its regular season with a walk-off, tag-up from Kenzie Vowell to

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL PLAYOFFS: No. 3 Cullman falls to No. 5 Hartselle; No. 1 Lindsay Lane upends Cold Springs
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Cullman’s postseason run came to an abrupt halt on Thursday.

The No. 3 Bearcats suffered a season-ending sweep (5-4 and 8-1) against No. 5 Hartselle to end their championship hopes in the second round of the Class 6A playoffs.

Cullman led 3-0 in the opener following Carson Stancil’s three-run homer in the third inning.

Hartselle eventually drew back even with a three-run fifth before Colt Colvard put the Bearcats ahead 4-3 with a two-out RBI single in the seventh.

The Tigers, however, respon

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL PLAYOFFS: No. 5 Addison punches ticket to quarterfinals with sweep of No. 10 South Lamar
articles.author: Jake Winfrey
articles.body:
Addison is headed back to the Class 1A quarterfinals following a road sweep of South Lamar on Thursday.

The No. 5 Bulldogs (24-6) used a pair of superb pitching performances by Kiah Lake and Tucker Thompson, respectively, to take down the No. 10 Stallions by scores of 9-2 and 2-1.

Thompson was particularly impressive, tossing a one-hitter in the nightcap.

Addison will face top-ranked Appalachian or Ider next week.

Game 1

Addison 9, South Lamar 2

Conner Lane:3-for-3, 2 RBIs

Scout Flynn:2-f

articles.title: PREP BASEBALL: Addison collects second round sweep of South Lamar to move on to 1A quarterfinals
articles.body: SOUTH LAMAR, Ala. –After sweeping Hackleburg to start out their 2026 postseason, the Addison Bulldogs made the trip over to South Lamar to take on the Stallions in the second round of the Class 1A state baseball playoffs. It was close in game one, as Addison held a slim one-run lead after two innings, but the Bulldogs collected a pair of runs in the fifth and broke the game wide open in the seventh inning with four more runs. That was more than enough run support for Kiah Lake, as they ended up

articles.title: PREP SOCCER: Saturday night Elite Eight matchups set for both Cullman soccer clubs
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala. –After Cullman’s boys took a 1-0 shootout victory over Randolph and the girls defeated Decatur 2-1 last night, the stage is set for both teams to host Elite Eight matchups on Saturday night at Cullman High School.

GIRLS ELITE EIGHT:Athens (17-5-2) @ Cullman (19-3), 5 p.m.

Since a 4-0 loss to Homewood on April 7, the Lady Bearcats have outscored opponents 25-2 through their five game winning streak. They find themselves matched up with Athens, an offense equally as hot and averagi

articles.title: Colton Lindsey wins Outstanding Management Student Award at Troy
articles.body:
TROY, Ala.– Colton Lindsey of Cullman was among the students recognized for outstanding achievements in academics, leadership and service during Troy University’s Sorrell College of Business Honors Convocation.

Lindsey received the Outstanding Management Student Award.

The Honors Convocation was held on Wednesday, April 15, at the Troy campus.

articles.title: U.S. 31 improvements at Lacon to detour traffic to I-65 beginning May 4
articles.body:
An upcoming project to improve U.S. Highway 31 at Lacon in Morgan County will divert traffic to I-65.

The $1.9 million project includes safety widening, concrete slab repairs and replacements, and paving an asphalt overlay. The project extends from the Cullman County line to Lacon Road.

The two-lane segment of U.S. Highway 31 north of I-65 Exit 318 to south of Lacon Trade Day will close to traffic Monday, May 4.

The contractor, Wiregrass Construction, plans to begin work that day, weather per

articles.title: ‘What does America mean to me?’
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala. –Hanceville Elementary and Hanceville Middle School on Wednesday, April 29, hosted a special event commemorating America’s Semiquincentennial – the 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence. The theme was “What does America mean to me?”

The program, held in the high school gymnasium, featured special guest speakers including Rep. Heath Allbright, R-Holly Pond; Rep. Cindy Myrex, R-Cullman; Cullman County Sheriff’s Office Lt. Chad Whaley, Cullman County

articles.title: The Aspen Institute names Wallace State semifinalist for $1M Aspen Prize
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– The Aspen Institute on Wednesday, April 29, named Wallace State Community College one of 25 semifinalists for the 2027 Aspen Prize for Community College Excellence. Rooted in deep research and backed by $1 million in award money, the Aspen Prize is the nation’s signature recognition of community colleges that deliver value for their students.

“We are excited to once again be selected as a semifinalist for the Aspen Prize for Community College Excellence,” said Wallace State Pr

articles.title: Valley’s Patterson attends ceremony for King Charles and Queen Camilla
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
On Tuesday, Valleyan native James Patterson attended the arrival ceremony for the United Kingdom’s King Charles III and Queen Camilla’s visit to the White House.

The ceremony took place on the South Lawn of the White House on Tuesday morning and was full of much fanfare with the occasion marking the first state visit from a British monarch since 2007, when Queen Elizabeth II met with President George W. Bush. The ceremony also marks the 250th celebration of America’s independence from the Unite

articles.title: Grants spark downtown upgrades in LaFayette
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
Downtown LaFayette is blooming this spring thanks to several grant-funded improvement projects across the city. From the new patio furniture for Zēk’s 37 to the mural outside of Auburn Opelika Dental, businesses across the downtown area are demonstrating that a little help can go a long way toward ensuring prosperity.

According to LaFayette Main Street Director DeAnna Hand, the Small Business Improvement Grant was meant to support local businesses by allowing them to invest directly in self-ide

articles.title: Huguley house fire claims two lives
articles.author: Staff Report
articles.body:
On Monday at approximately 11:40 p.m. (EST), the Huguley Fire Department was dispatched to a reported residential structure fire with possible entrapment in the 4300 block of 20th St. SW in Lanett.

The first arriving Huguley engine company was on scene within four minutes of dispatch and encountered heavy fire conditions involving the structure. Crews from Engine 554 initiated an interior fire attack while attempting to locate and rescue any occupants inside.

Huguley Fire Department personnel,

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Bill King: This Little Line of Mine
articles.author: Bill King
articles.body: I know the song says, “Amarillo by morning, up from San Antone,” but on our tour across the South of Spain, I found myself singing “Marbella by morning, up from Fuengirola.” It was a Friday morning, to be exact, when we boarded the big red bus for a ride to Marbella by the Mediterranean. The city is a seacoast town about halfway between Malaga and the Strait of Gibraltar. It is known as the Spanish playground of the rich and famous. When I saw the whitewashed Andalusian buildings, the trees drip

articles.title: Westrock helps raise money for Children’s of Alabama
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
Representatives from Smurfit Westrock and Children’s of Alabama will be assisting the Log A Load for Kids Capital District in a golfing event in Lanett to raise money for the Children's Hospital Intervention and Prevention Services (CHIPS) Center at Children’s of Alabama. The Log A Load scramble-style golf tournament will take place at the Point University Golf Club on May 14 in Lanett (2001 Country Club Rd.).

Children’s of Alabama has been a source of specialized medical care for ill and injur

articles.title: LETTER TO THE EDITOR: Luanne Gross Stone: Prison Reform is Long Overdue
articles.author: Luanne Gross Stone
articles.body: Today, there are roughly 2 million people living in prisons and jails in the United States. Living conditions are deplorable in all but a very small number of facilities. Most provide food that is barely edible and healthcare that is grossly inadequate. Some inmates literally eat toothpaste or toilet paper to stave off hunger. The threat of violence is constant. Few institutions offer meaningful education or rehabilitation programs. Many people languish in six-by-eight cells for 22 to 24 hours a

articles.title: Ivey awards grants to fund victim programs
articles.author: Staff Reports
articles.body:
Gov. Kay Ivey has awarded 15 grants totaling about $673,000 to nonprofit organizations and county governments in central Alabama to assist victims of domestic violence, sexual assault, human trafficking and other crimes.

One of those organizations, Legal Services Alabama, received a $54,243 award for services provided directly to Crenshaw, Lowndes, Autauga, Butler, Chilton, Elmore and Pik counties. The money will go to hire an attorney and project manager for the organization's Project Leviticu

articles.title: 5 Alabama companies named among America’s most trusted
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
Newsweekand Statista recognized five Alabama-based companies for earning strong public confidence among customers, employees and investors nationwide.

Trust has become one of the rarest commodities in modern American life.

Confidence in government has eroded. Faith in media remains fractured. Public institutions Americans once viewed as stable now face growing skepticism and division.

Yet amid that uncertainty, several Alabama companies are earning something increasingly difficult to obtain:

articles.title: Opinion | The cheating is not OK
articles.author: Josh Moon
articles.body:
So, just so I’m clear, y’all are all OK with cheating now?

Rules don’t matter? Fairness doesn’t matter? Basic rights don’t matter? Not to anyone anymore?

This rush to cheat at voting in the wake of this week’s U.S. Supreme Court decision further gutting the Voting Rights Act tells me that’s true. And boy, is that ever depressing.

That’s not sour grapes from a lib worried that “my side” is going to lose the gerrymandering race. I don’t think that, actually. If “my side” is the left side, I act

articles.title: AG Marshall asks Supreme Court to revisit congressional map ruling
articles.author: Mary Claire Wooten
articles.body:
Alabama officials are again turning to the U.S. Supreme Court in the fight over the state’s congressional maps, arguing that a new high court decision has changed the legal landscape.

Attorney General Steve Marshall filed three emergency motions seeking to lift federal injunctions blocking Alabama’s 2023 congressional map. The request at the heels of the Court’s ruling inLouisiana v. Callais, a decision state leaders say warrants swift reconsideration of Alabama’s case.

The filings seek relief

articles.title: House passes 2026 Farm Bill, Alabama delegation splits along party lines
articles.author: Wesley Walter
articles.body:
The U.S. House of Representatives passed the 2026 Farm Bill on Thursday, with Alabama lawmakers splitting on the measure along party lines.

The $390 billion measure, which reauthorizes major food and agriculture programs for the next five years, saw all of Alabama’s Republican U.S. representatives vote in its favor Thursday morning, while receiving “nays” from the state’s two congressional Democrats.

The successful vote marks the farthest the Farm, Food, and National Security Act has advanced

articles.title: Final work and detour scheduled ahead of new Intracoastal Waterway Bridge traffic pattern
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The Alabama Department of Transportation announced Thursday it is preparing to open the new Intracoastal Waterway Bridge in Baldwin County with a new traffic pattern aimed at improving travel to Alabama’s beach communities.

To finish construction of the two southbound lanes at the north roundabout, ALDOT will put a temporary detour in place for drivers heading south on SR-161, also known as Beach Express.

The detour will begin Sunday, May 10, 2026, at 7 a.m. and continue through Wednesday, May

articles.title: Sen. Britt priorities advanced out of Senate Judiciary Committee
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
U.S. Senator Katie Britt, R-Alabama, a member of the Senate Judiciary Committee, at an executive business meeting Thursday voted to advance several bills out of committee, including two of her legislative priorities.

Britt is a lead sponsor of theGuidelines for User Age-verification and Responsible Dialogue, GUARD, Actand is a cosponsor of theFederal Carjacking Enforcement Act, legislation to reduce juvenile crime and crack down on carjacking by removing barriers to bringing federal prosecution

articles.title: SPLC Action Fund endorses four Alabama judicial candidates
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
The SPLC Action Fund on Thursday endorsed four candidates running in upcoming judicial elections in Alabama.

Jefferson County attorney AshLeigh Dunham received an endorsement in her race for a seat on the Alabama Supreme Court. A Huntsville native with a law degree from Cumberland School of Law at Samford University, Dunham has spent her career advocating for families treated unfairly by the legal system. She currently serves as a referee in Jefferson County’s Juvenile Court, where she adjudica

articles.title: Gov. Ivey praises MACS unit as Montgomery crime drops
articles.author: Staff
articles.body:
Governor Kay Ivey this week met with Alabama Law Enforcement Agency Secretary Hal Taylor, State Bureau of Investigation Director Christopher Inabinett, Assistant Special Agent in Charge Paul Hayes, who oversees the Metro Area Crime Suppression Unit, and Assistant Special Agent in Charge Billy Fulmer, who oversees the Alabama Law Enforcement Agency SWAT team, to receive an in-depth update on MACS and its continued impact in and around Alabama’s capital city.

During the meeting, agency leaders pr

articles.title: How will the Supreme Court’s voting rights decision affect Alabama?
articles.author: Chance Phillips
articles.body:
On Wednesday, the U.S. Supreme Court issued a 6-3 decision in the voting rights caseLouisiana v. Callais. The decision was split along party lines, with Republican-appointed justices all in the majority and all three Democrat-appointed justices signing onto the dissent written by Justice Elena Kagan.

The majority opinion significantly decreased the scope of Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, which has historically been interpreted as requiring states to create and maintain “majority-minority”

articles.author: Anna Barrett
articles.body:
Democratic voters will choose between (left to right) Rudolph Drake of Madison, Alex House of Huntsville and Guy Sotomayor of Harvest for the Democratic nomination for Senate District 2, a wide open seat in north Alabama. (Photos by Anna Barrett/Alabama Reflector and courtesy of Sotomayor; graphic by Anna Barrett)

In a wide open state Senate race in north Alabama, voters will choose between three Democrats, who will face the sole Republican who qualified for the race.

Senate District 2 coverst

articles.title: US House passes ‘skinny’ farm bill that keeps big GOP cuts to food assistance
articles.author: Jacob Fischler

articles.body:
Sue Cooper Huffman, 88, of Hickory, N.C., died peacefully at her home on Wednesday, April 22, 2026, surrounded by her loving family. She was born on May 24, 1937, in Selma, Ala., the second daughter of Ruth Messer Cooper and Claude Miller Cooper.

Sue graduated from Selma High School (1955), Salem College (B.A. in English and History, 1959) and the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill (M.A. in English, 1960).

articles.author: Jaine Treadwell
articles.body:
Have you ever seen a dancing wooden man?

Or have you ever seen a wooden man dancing?

Wayne Brunson said, they are one and the same.

And, he should know. He gives “life” to men made of wood. And they dance.

Brunson, of Chancellor Alabama, will be one of the folklife demonstrators at Pioneer Heritage Day at the Pioneer Museum of Alabama on Saturday, May 2.

And, he will bring along several of his dancing men.

articles.title: Southern Classic announces major expansion
articles.author: Huck Treadwell
articles.body:
Southern Classic Foods, a division Trillium Food Group, in Brundidge announced a major expansion that will require a $12 million investment.

Southern Classic Foods President Chuck Caraway said some details of the expansion are still being worked out and the investment may reach $14 million. He said the expansion will include seven new production lines and will employ a minimum of 82 people. He said the number of new jobs may increase to 100 or more as production gets underway in late summer.

articles.title: Monday is last day to register to vote in May 19 primary
articles.author: Huck Treadwell
articles.body:
The deadline to register to vote in the 2026 Alabama primary elections is Monday.

Alabama’s Republican and Democratic Primaries are both Tuesday, May 19. In order to vote in the primary election, new voters must be registered by May 4.

A citizen may register to vote, or update voter registration online or at the following locations:

• online atalabamavotes.govif they have a valid Alabama Driver’s License or Non-Driver ID card
• when applying for or renewing a State of Alabama Driver’s License

articles.body:
Don’t miss Pioneer Heritage Day at the Pioneer Museum of Alabama from 10 a.m. until 4 p.m. Saturday.

Barbara Tatom, museum director, said Pioneer Heritage Day is an opportunity to step back in time and experience those good ol’ days of yesteryear in a hands-on kind of way.

The National Day of Prayer is an annual observation held on the first Thursday of May, inviting people to pray for the nation. The observance was created in 1952 by a joint resolution of the United States Congress and signed into law by President Harry S. Truman.

With the win, the Indians advance to the Class 5A quarterfinals, where they will meet No. 3 American Christian Academy.

East Limestone (25-9) recorded all four of its runs in the third inning.

According to ECSO officials, the vehicle was occupied by two white males.

“After the bus stopped and the student exited, the student reported that the passenger (of the white vehicle) attempted to offer them candy,” officials said. “The student immediately notified their parents.”

Members of the Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority began their southeastern regional conference on Wednesday. The gathering brings together 122 college and graduate chapters from Alabama, Tennessee and Mississippi from April 29 to May 2.

articles.body:
The Cullman County Sports Hall of Fame revealed its Class of 2026 inductees this week.

Deserae Campbell (Cold Springs), Bob Bryan (Cullman), Trey Jones (Cullman), AJ Lamar (Cullman), Matt Black (Fairview), Susan Cofer (Good Hope), Rod Elliott (Hanceville), Ronald Swann (Hanceville), Chuck Gambrill (Holly Pond), Joseph Sherrill (Vinemont) and Neil Lowry (West Point) will be ushered in as members of the 26th class during a banquet ceremony at Stone Bridge Farms on July 25 at 6 p.m.

articles.title: Bloomin’ Festival car winner announced
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body: Christopher Metress of Birmingham, Alabama, claimed the grand prize — a 2026 Buick Encore — at the 2026 Bloomin’ Festival held at St. Bernard Preparatory School. He is pictured with Fr. Joel Martin.

articles.title: Tree Commission approves tree purchase, highlights Arbor Day
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
The Cullman Tree Commission met April 21 at 10 a.m. at Cullman City Hall. City Arborist Shane Bivins reported business as usual and responded to several inquiries about tree clearing and other work currently taking place at city parks.

The only new business on the agenda was a vote to approve an invoice to CPRST (Cullman Parks, Recreation & Sports Tourism) for two Chinese Pistache trees that were recently planted at the request of the commission. The invoice, in the amount of $71.92, will be su

articles.title: WSCC Future Foundation Scholarship brunch announces more than $500K in scholarships
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
The Wallace State Community College Future Foundation recently hosted its 14th annual Scholarship Celebration Brunch, recognizing the generosity of its donors and celebrating the achievement of Wallace State’s students. The event included the announcement of more than $500,000 in scholarships for the 2026–2027 academic year.

During the event, held in the Traditions Bank Arena of the Tom Drake Coliseum, it was announced that scholarships would benefit more than 270 students.

“Each student recog

articles.title: US 31 improvements at Lacon to detour traffic to I-65
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
An upcoming project to improve US-31 at Lacon in Morgan County will divert traffic to I-65.

The $1.9 million project includes safety widening, concrete slab repairs and replacements, and paving an asphalt overlay. The project extends from the Cullman County line to Lacon Road.

The two-lane segment of US-31 north of I-65 Exit 318 to south of Lacon Trade Day will close to traffic Monday, May 4.

The contractor, Wiregrass Construction, plans to begin work that day, weather permitting. A subcontra

articles.title: St. Bernard Prep hosts academic honors banquet
articles.author: Staff reports
articles.body:
St. Bernard Preparatory School hosted its annual Academic Honors Banquet on Thursday, April 30, bringing together students, families, faculty and distinguished guests in the Fazi-Richard Athletic Center to celebrate a year of academic accomplishment.

The evening began with a welcome and opening prayer from Fr. Joel Martin, President of St. Bernard Preparatory School, who commended students for their dedication to scholarship and the Benedictine values that guide their education.

Robert Tidwell

articles.title: PREP SOFTBALL: Butler fans 13, scores two to push Hanceville over Locust Fork in 2-0 area semifinal win
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala. –The Hanceville Lady Bulldogs, host of the Class 3A, Area 12 softball tournament, took on Locust Fork after taking down J.B. Pennington 8-3 earlier on Friday. Hanceville struck early, and held on to their lead with stellar defense to advance to the area championship game Monday night.

Taking the mound for the home team was KK Butler, who put on a show, striking out 13 Hornet batters and only gave up three hits across seven innings.  On the other side, Brylee Calloway put up a s

articles.title: New partnership to add advanced cardiovascular care at Cullman Regional
articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– Baptist Health’s Cardiovascular Associates, Cullman Regional and Cullman Regional Medical Group have partnered to expand advanced cardiology care in Cullman.

Well known for providing high-quality cardiovascular services in north Alabama, Cardiovascular Associates (CVA) providers moved their Cullman practice to the new Heart & Vascular Clinic on Cullman Regional campus. With years of experience in specialized areas of cardiology including electrophysiology and vascular disease tre

articles.body:
CULLMAN, Ala.– The Cullman Tree Commission met April 21 at Cullman City Hall. City Arborist Shane Bivins reported business as usual and responded to several inquiries about tree clearing and other work currently taking place at city parks.

The only new business on the agenda was a vote to approve an invoice to Cullman Parks, Recreation & Sports Tourism (CPRST) for two Chinese Pistache trees that were recently planted at the request of the Commission. The invoice, in the amount of $71.92, will b

articles.title: Community College Month spotlight: Finding a future in welding at Wallace State
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– When Robert Henry Cook III began searching for the right place to launch his career, Wallace State Community College stood out for one clear reason: its reputation for excellence in welding.

“I chose Wallace State because it has great welding program,” Cook said. It was also close to home, he said.

From 2021 to 2023, Cook immersed himself in the Welding Technology program, gaining the hands-on skills and technical knowledge that would quickly set him on a successful path. By

articles.title: Wallace State Nursing hosts Innovations in Simulations workshop
articles.body:
HANCEVILLE, Ala.– More than 100 educators and healthcare professionals gathered at Wallace State Community College last week for the Department of Nursing Education’s annual workshop, which this year focused on artificial intelligence in simulation-based learning.

The workshop, Innovations in Healthcare Simulations, provided attendees with opportunities to learn how artificial intelligence, or AI, can be integrated into the training of future health care professionals.

Two main sessions addres

articles.title: NFL draftee a longtime customer of Kenny Knox Tire
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Keldric Faulk, a first-round NFL draft pick for the Tennessee Titans who played for Auburn, stopped by Kenny Knox Tire Center in Valley on Wednesday, getting his tires ready to head to training in Nashville. He was joined by his brother J.J., who currently plays for Auburn.

Faulk is a long-time customer of Knox, who is a big Auburn fan. Knox is an Auburn football season ticket holder and regularly donates to the program to support players, and has been helping players get the best tires to keep

articles.title: Valley Rams baseball caps off hard fought season
articles.author: By Chad Martin
articles.body:
As the final out on April 25 brought the Rams baseball season to a heartbreaking close in the first round of the playoffs, looking back on the season and the perseverance of these Rams showcases a season that can be looked at as a success.

“You know we played a lot of guys that do not have a lot of varsity experience in the past. We had to play a lot of young guys this year and dealt with several injuries early in the year, so our lineup changed a lot during the year,” Head Coach Mike Meadows s

articles.title: Rebel baseball advances to Final Four
articles.author: Hannah Barron
articles.body:
Chambers Academy’s varsity baseball team is surviving and advancing to the Final Four round of the AISA 2A state tournament.

Chambers Academy played Bessemer Academy on Monday in an Elite Eight doubleheader. The Rebels swept the first game 11-1, with a strong performance in both hitting and pitching to end the game after five innings. Peyton Yerta and Philip Belcher led the Rebels with three RBIs apiece. Yerta, Landon Hand and Tucker Thrower all contributed two runs. Hunter Andrews singled to c

articles.title: County voters meet local candidates
articles.author: Brandon Ussery
articles.body:
The Greater Valley Area Chamber of Commerce hosted a candidate meet-and-greet ahead of the May 19 elections on April 30. Candidates gathered at the community center at the Valley Sportsplex to meet constituents and share their platforms with potential voters.

Seventeen candidates across five races showed up to put their best foot forward, and voters from across the area gradually made their way from table to table. Candidates eagerly shared kind words along with their election goals.

The candi

articles.title: Smith feels called to run for coroner
articles.author: Wayne Clark
articles.body:
David Smith has three key words in his current campaign for Chambers County Coroner — experience, compassion and dedication.

“I want to serve the public of Chambers County,” he said. “I don’t see the position of coroner as a job. It's a calling.”

Smith has 28 years of local law enforcement experience. Since 2022 he’s been a deputy coroner under current coroner Jeff Jones, who will be retiring at the end of his present term.

“I have spent the majority of my adult life serving the people of the

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Loran Smith: Kehoe
articles.author: Loran Smith
articles.body:
The recent death of Marilyn Kehoe brought grief to a broad spectrum of people from Athens to Cortona, Italy, her longtime second home where she ably assisted her husband, Jack, in establishing the premier studies abroad program throughout collegiate America.

They spent their summers in the hilltop town in Tuscany where you are often hailed with “Go Dawgs” when you walk down the street and that included natives as well as the colony of Georgia students who have spent time there.

Count me among

articles.title: CONTRIBUTOR'S VIEW - Dean Collins: Living as the Message
articles.author: Dean Collins
articles.body:
The path to receiving comfort from the horrors of the present and of the past has not changed. These comforts and the peace that follows are available today. What is essential, however, is that we turn to the true source of our comfort and peace daily. And we must be willing to “prepare the way of the Lord” as part of the process of everyone receiving the comfort the Lord has promised. God’s solutions are better than anything the world has to offer.

Jerusalem fell not because God couldn’t save

articles.title: Students practice financial literacy 'Your money, Your life'
articles.author: Conner Howell
articles.body:
Your Money, Your Life is a hands-on financial literacy simulation created and provided by the Alabama Cooperative Extension System. The program, present in local high schools, gives students a real world look at managing money as an adult.

The event was held at each Crenshaw County School for seniors during April as part of Financial Literacy Month and career readiness and life skills programming. Each Career Prep Class and all ninth-grade students also completed an activity to explore their pe

articles.title: Gov. Ivey calls special session as Alabama renews congressional map fight
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
Alabama’s long-running congressional redistricting war entered a new phase Friday as Governor Kay Ivey called lawmakers back to Montgomery in hopes the U.S. Supreme Court has handed Republicans an opportunity to reclaim control of the state’s congressional maps—and potentially reshape Alabama’s role in the battle for control of Congress.

The special session will begin May 4 at 4 p.m., following this week’s U.S. Supreme Court ruling inLouisiana v. Callais, a decision many conservatives across th

articles.title: Alicia Escott Lumpkin pitches change in Alabama House race
articles.author: Alex Jobin
articles.body:
APR recently spoke with Alicia Escott Lumpkin, Democratic challenger for Alabama House District 60, to learn more about her campaign ahead of the upcoming May 19 primary elections, where she will face off against incumbent state Representative Juandalynn Givan, D-Birmingham, and fellow challenger Nina Taylor.

“I’m running because I believe that we’re at a turning point with District 60 and we can’t afford to miss out on what’s next,” Lumpkin told APR when asked what motivated her to enter the r

articles.title: Campaign filings reveal hidden network behind Alabama attack mailers
articles.author: Bill Britt
articles.body:
A business registered in Wyoming.

A political action committee registered to a UPS mailbox in Montgomery.

Another business registered in Virginia.

A political consultant from Wisconsin.

Taken separately, none of those things are unusual in modern politics. Together, however, they form a complicated financial structure now tied to attack mailers, campaign websites and Republican primary races across Alabama.

The story began several weeks ago, when political mailers targeting Senator Greg Alb

articles.title: Immigration street sweeps led to more ‘collateral’ arrests of noncriminals
articles.author: Tim Henderson
articles.body:
ICE agents search the passenger of a truck as they arrest both him and the driver during a traffic stop in February in Robbinsdale, Minn. Almost a quarter of ICE arrests in recent months have been "collateral," a category that has raised legal questions, rather than "targeted" arrests based on preexisting warrants or removal orders. (Photo by Nicole Neri/Minnesota Reformer)

articles.author: Jonathan Shorman
articles.body:
The U.S. Supreme Court, pictured April 9, 2026. Some progressives are seeking to restructure the court after seeing decisions in recent years they believe have provided political support to President Donald Trump and Republicans. (Photo by Ashley Murray/States Newsroom)

articles.author: Ralph Chapoco
articles.body:
Alabama Gov. Kay Ivey is greeted by Gordon Stone, executive director of the Higher Education Partnership of Alabama, prior to a speech on Higher Education Day at the Alabama Statehouse on Feb. 19, 2026 in Montgomery, Alabama. Ivey Friday called a special session of the Alabama Legislature to move back the May 19th primary in at least some districts if the U.S. Supreme COurt lifts an injunction against the state redrawing its legislative maps. articles.title: Lighthouse hosting resident art show and auction
articles.author: Briana Wilson
articles.body:
Lighthouse Rehab and Healthcare is inviting the Dallas County community to its upcoming fundraiser, Brushes for Parkinson's on Wednesday, May 13, at 5 p.m.

During the event, artwork created by the residents of Lighthouse will be auctioned. Profits from the auction will be donated to the Parkinson’s Foundation, a national organization that funds research and provides educational resources to Parkinson's disease patients and caregivers.

Rehab Director Taylor Johnson said Lighthouse residents hav

articles.title: 16th annual Special Needs Fun Day brings “Bling and Boots” to students
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
Boots, bling and a mechanical bull highlighted the 16th annual Special Needs Fun Day on Thursday, April 30, continuing a long-standing community tradition aimed at celebrating local students with disabilities.

The event, which serves students from Selma City Schools, Dallas County Schools, and the Cahaba Center, was founded by the late George Evans. The former mayor of Selma and special needs supervisor created the initiative to provide an inclusive environment for children who often lack acces

articles.title: Lowndes Academy overcome Morgan Academy in tiebreaker matchup
articles.author: Nichlos Cutrell
articles.body:
Lowndesboro–Lowndes Academy secured a spot in the AISA state semifinals Thursday, April 30, defeating Morgan Academy 12-4 in a decisive game 3 to claim a hard-fought second-round playoff series. After the two teams split a competitive double header, the Rebels utilized a massive nine-run second inning in the finale to overwhelm the Senators and clinch the best-of-three set.

The series began Wednesday, April 29, with a back-and-forth battle that saw Lowndes Academy take the early advantage with

articles.title: Alabama’s two-month grocery tax holiday begins today
articles.author: Briana Wilson
articles.body:
Grocery runs just got a little cheaper for Alabama shoppers with the state’s two-month grocery tax holiday beginning today, May 1.

Now through June 30, the state’s 2% sales tax on groceries is suspended. The tax holiday is a part of HB527 that was sponsored by Rep. James Lomax, R-Huntsville, and signed into law by Gov. Kay Ivey on April 16. Sen. Arthur Orr carried the legislation in the Senate.

Previous legislation reduced the tax from 4% to 3% in 2023, and then to 2% last year.

The tax exemp

articles.title: Thorsby community, baseball teams rally around Barnes family to honor young player’s legacy
articles.body:
The small-town community of Thorsby is coming together in a powerful show of support following the recent loss of 8-year-old Eli Barnes, ayouth baseballplayer remembered for his energy, kindness, and love of the game.

In the days since his passing, Thorsby’s baseball community and residents alike have rallied around the Barnes family, turning their grief into a series of tributes that highlight the joy, sportsmanship, and encouragement Eli brought to those around him.

Eli, known on the field a

articles.title: David Rainer: AWF opens Delta & Bay Conservation Center at 5 Rivers
articles.body:
After a year of upgrades, theAlabama Wildlife Federation’s (AWF) Delta & Bay Conservation Center at 5 Rivers, the former 5 Rivers Delta Resource Center, was officially reopened last week with dignitaries and local and state officials hailing the new direction of the property.

AWF Executive Director Tim Gothard welcomed a large crowd to cut the ribbon on the 70-acre property located on the Battleship Parkway (Causeway) on the headwaters of Mobile Bay.

Gothard recalled the 20-year journey of ide

articles.title: Steve Flowers: Few open or contested State House races
articles.body:
Like the Alabama State Senate, the State House of Representatives will have very little turnover. Our state legislative seats are becoming analogous to congressional seats when it comes to incumbency. Over 80% of the legislature is unopposed.

The partisanship makeup and faces will be pretty much unchanged. The House will have a supermajority Republican complexion. It will remain 75% Republican. There will be a strong continuity of leadership in the House of Representatives between this quadrenn

articles.title: Alabama Power lands in top 10 of Newsweek’s ‘Most Trustworthy Companies in America’ for 2026
articles.body:
Alabama Power has been named one of the most trustworthy companies in America for 2026, ranking No. 8 in the Energy & Utilities category onNewsweek’sannual list index tracking exactly that.

The 2026 edition of “Most Trustworthy Companies in America” is produced in partnership with global market research firm Statista.

Itevaluates700 companies across 23 industries based on an independent survey of approximately 25,000 Americans who submitted more than 100,000 evaluations as customers, employees

articles.title: Moore calls for new Alabama congressional map as Ivey sets special redistricting session
articles.body:
U.S. Rep. Barry Moore (R-Enterprise) is calling for Alabama lawmakers to redraw the state’s congressionalmapafter a recent U.S. Supreme Court decision involving Louisiana, arguing the ruling supports a move away from race-based redistricting.

Moore’s comments come as Governor Kay Ivey has called aspecial sessionto redraw Alabama’s congressional map in response to the ruling, giving lawmakers a new opportunity to revisit district lines ahead of the 2026 elections.

The case,Louisiana v. Callais,

articles.title: Rightside Media, Katelyn Bussey launch ‘The Bussey Brief’ – new Gen Z-focused commentary segment joins ‘The Rightside’ lineup
articles.body:
Rightside Media is proud to announce the addition of Katelyn Bussey and a new recurring segment, The Bussey Brief, which launched today, May 1, in its debut — now available across all platforms.

Bussey will join hosts Allison Sinclair and Amie Beth Shaver to deliver sharp, unfiltered commentary on the political moments, cultural shifts, and current events defining America right now, through a Gen Z lens.

The Bussey Brief is where Washington meets the real world. Each segment will feature Busse

articles.body:
Redistricting comes to the South, another Trump attack, and more on Alabama Politics This Week…

Can Alabama redistrict like other states are doing?

Why isn’t this assassination a bigger deal?

Who is winning in straw polls for the U.S. Senate, Lt. Gov., or Governor races?

Why would the Cullman County Sheriff take gambling money?

articles.title: Governor Ivey calls special session for redistricting – Alabama lawmakers will have a new shot at a full-GOP-sweep congressional map
articles.body:
Afterinitiallytempering expectations for one earlier this week, Governor Kay Ivey will convene a special session of the Alabama Legislature on Sunday to begin drawing new congressional mapsfollowingthe U.S. Supreme Court’s ruling inLouisiana v. Callais.

The Alabama House and Senate will convene at 4:00 p.m. on May 4.

Alabama remains under a federal court order preventing the Legislature from redrawing its congressional maps until 2030, but on Friday,  Ivey and legislative leaders said the sess

articles.title: Alabama AI and children’s internet safety commission holds first meeting, elects leadership
articles.body:
The Alabama Study Commission on Artificial Intelligence and Children’s Internet Safety held its first meeting this week, electing State Rep. Robbins (R-Sylacauga) as chairman and State Rep. Matt Woods (R-Jasper) as vice chairman.

The commission was established byHJR51during the2026 legislative sessionto study the effects of AI, social media, and internet access on children and develop recommendations for future legislation.

The commission includes legislators from both chambers, a representati

articles.title: Jay Mitchell op-ed: Alabama must act now to repeal the racist congressional map
articles.body:
We’re in a redistricting war, and President Trump needs reinforcements.

Democrats are gerrymandering at unprecedented levels to eliminate Republican districts. One of Alabama’s own Congressional Representatives, DemocratTerri Sewell, openly called for California and Illinois to sendzeroRepublicans to Congress. If you have any doubt whether our opponents are redistricting in bad faith, look at Virginia’s map that will turn a 55-45 state into a Democrat stronghold.

articles.title: Alabama GOP lawmakers targeted by Wisconsin consultant whose record is tangled in election-law investigations across the nation
articles.body:
Over the past month, GOP voters in five Alabama legislative districts have been bombarded withmailers, texts, and digital ads attacking their incumbent state lawmakers.

The targets are State Sens. Greg Albritton (R-Range) and Andrew Jones (R-Centre), and State Reps. Frances Holk-Jones (R-Foley), Matt Simpson (R-Daphne), and Phillip Pettus (R-Killen).

The disclaimers all point to a single source, Alabama Values PAC,registeredwith the Alabama Secretary of State on March 12, 2026to a rented mailb

articles.title: Tuberville targets ‘ghost student’ fraud with new federal aid legislation
articles.body:
U.S. Sen. Tommy Tuberville helpedintroduce legislation this weekaimed at stopping fraudulent federal student aid applications, while also co-sponsoring a resolution designating April 2026 as Financial Literacy Month.

The No Aid for Ghost Students Act targets individuals who use stolen or fake identities to submitFAFSAapplications and collect federal student aid. The bill would require the Department of Education to implement an identity fraud detection system to flag suspicious applications and

articles.title: Boone signs cheer scholarship with Troy University
articles.author: Huck Treadwell
articles.body:
Pike Liberal Arts School senior Casidee Boone signed a cheerleading scholarship  with Troy University on Friday morning.

Boone said she was excited about the opportunity to continue cheerleading at the collegiate level. She said she’d already had the opportunity to meet members of the university’s squad and was looking forward to cheering the Trojans on in the fall.

“I had a chance to meet the team already,” Boone said. “They’ve been very welcoming and have been very helpful with teaching me a

articles.title: Banks highway project underway
articles.author: Submitted Article
articles.body:
An Alabama Department of Transportation (ALDOT) project on SR-130 in Pike and Barbour County has started.

The project will take place on SR-130 from the junction of SR-15 (US-29) east of Banks to the junction of SR-51 in Louisville, a span of 15.443 miles.

This project includes resurfacing, safety widening, bridge guardrail retrofit and traffic striping.

The approximately $4 million project was awarded to Wiregrass Construction Company, Inc., out of Dothan, who had the lowest bid that met the
